# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Field Capacity
(b) Permanent Wilting Point
(c) Available Water

### Model Answer

**(a) Field Capacity (FC):** Field capacity is the moisture content of a soil after gravity drainage has ceased, typically measured **2–3 days** after a saturating rain or irrigation event when the downward movement of water due to gravity becomes negligible. At this point, the soil water tension is approximately **1/3 bar (33 kPa)** for medium-textured soils. All *gravitational water* has drained out, leaving only *capillary water* in the micro-pores. **Example:** A sandy loam soil has field capacity of approximately **20%** by volume.

**(b) Permanent Wilting Point (PWP):** PWP is the soil moisture content below which plants cannot extract sufficient water to recover turgor, even when transpiration demand is zero and the atmosphere is saturated. It corresponds to a soil water tension of **15 bar (1500 kPa)**. Below PWP, water is held too tightly by matric forces for root uptake. **Example:** Sandy loam PWP is approximately **10%** by volume.

**(c) Available Water (AW):** AW is the portion of soil water that plants can extract, defined as the difference between field capacity and permanent wilting point:

$$AW = \theta_{FC} - \theta_{PWP}$$

For the sandy loam above: $AW = 20\% - 10\% = 10\%$ by volume. This represents the **working water reservoir** for crops between irrigations.

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between infiltration and percolation. List any four factors affecting infiltration.**

### Model Answer

| Aspect | Infiltration | Percolation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Entry of water from the **surface** into the soil | Downward movement of water **within** the soil profile |
| **Location** | At the soil–air interface | Through the vadose (unsaturated) zone |
| **Driving Force** | Matric suction + gravity | Primarily gravity (when soil is wet) |
| **Controlling Property** | Infiltration capacity $f_c$ | Hydraulic conductivity $K(\theta)$ |

**Four Factors Affecting Infiltration:**

1. **Soil Texture:** Coarse-grained soils (sand) have high infiltration rates (50–250 mm/hr); fine-grained soils (clay) have low rates (1–5 mm/hr).

2. **Initial Soil Moisture:** Dry soils infiltrate faster initially (high matric suction); wet soils infiltrate slower (low suction gradient).

3. **Surface Compaction and Crusting:** Tractor traffic, raindrop impact, and sodium-induced dispersion create surface seals that drastically reduce infiltration — sometimes by **80–90%**.

4. **Vegetative Cover:** Bare soil has lower infiltration than vegetated soil due to lack of root channels (macropores) and protection from raindrop impact. Crop residue can increase infiltration by **2–5 times**.

> [!NOTE]
> Other factors include soil structure, organic matter content, slope, rainfall intensity, and temperature (affects water viscosity).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the soil-water-plant relationship. Define **field capacity**, **permanent wilting point**, **available water**, and **hygroscopic coefficient**. Show their relative positions on a typical soil moisture release curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field soil has the following properties: Field Capacity = 28%, Permanent Wilting Point = 14%, Root Zone Depth = 60 cm, Bulk Density = 1.4 g/cm³, Depletion fraction p = 0.5. Compute:
(i) Total Available Water (TAW) in mm.
(ii) Readily Available Water (RAW) in mm.
(iii) If the current soil moisture is at 65% of TAW, determine the depth of irrigation required (assume 100% application efficiency). **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Soil-Water-Plant Relationship:**

The soil-water-plant system is a **three-phase interactive continuum** where:
- **Soil** acts as the *reservoir* (stores water in pores).
- **Water** is the *transport medium* (moves from soil to root to atmosphere).
- **Plant* acts as the *biological pump* (extracts water via roots, transpires via leaves).

**Soil Moisture Release Curve (Sketch Description):**

```
Moisture
Content (θ)
  |
  |● Saturation (S) = Porosity (n)
  |   ↘ Steep decline (drainage of macro-pores)
  |     ● Field Capacity (FC) ≈ 1/3 bar suction
  |       ↘ Gradual decline (capillary water release)
  |         ● Permanent Wilting Point (PWP) ≈ 15 bar
  |           ↘ Near-flat tail (hygroscopic retention)
  |             ● Hygroscopic Coefficient ≈ 31 bar
  |_____________________________
                  Soil Water Tension (log scale, kPa)
       0    33          1500           3100
```

**Definitions:**

- **Saturation (S):** All pores filled with water; $\Psi \approx 0$; θ = porosity.
- **Field Capacity (FC):** Moisture after free drainage ceases; suction = **1/3 bar (33 kPa)**; θ ≈ 28% for loam.
- **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP):** Moisture at which plants wilt irreversibly; suction = **15 bar (1500 kPa)**; θ ≈ 14% for loam.
- **Available Water (AW):** $AW = \theta_{FC} - \theta_{PWP}$ = water between FC and PWP that plants can use.
- **Hygroscopic Coefficient:** Moisture held as adsorbed films on particle surfaces; suction ≥ **31 bar (3100 kPa)**; unavailable to plants.

### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $\theta_{FC} = 28\% = 0.28$ (volumetric)
- $\theta_{PWP} = 14\% = 0.14$ (volumetric)
- Root depth $D = 60$ cm = 600 mm
- Bulk density $\rho_b = 1.4$ g/cm³
- Depletion fraction $p = 0.5$
- Current moisture = 65% of TAW

**(i) Total Available Water (TAW):**

$$AW = \theta_{FC} - \theta_{PWP} = 0.28 - 0.14 = 0.14 \text{ (or 14% volumetric)}$$

Converting to depth using bulk density:

$$AW_{depth} = AW \cdot D = 0.14 \times 600 \text{ mm} = 84 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{TAW = 84 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Readily Available Water (RAW):**

$$RAW = p \cdot TAW = 0.5 \times 84 = \boxed{42 \text{ mm}}$$

**(iii) Depth of Irrigation Required:**

Current moisture status:
$$\theta_{current} = 0.65 \times TAW = 0.65 \times 84 = 54.6 \text{ mm (of available water present)}$$

Moisture deficit (the water needed to refill to FC):
$$\text{Deficit} = TAW - \theta_{current} = 84 - 54.6 = 29.4 \text{ mm}$$

Assuming **100% application efficiency**:

$$\boxed{\text{Net Irrigation Depth} = 29.4 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define **infiltration capacity**. Explain Horton's infiltration equation with its limitations. A soil has initial infiltration capacity $f_0 = 80$ mm/hr, final capacity $f_c = 10$ mm/hr, and decay constant $k = 3$ hr⁻¹. Determine the infiltration rate and cumulative infiltration at $t = 30$ minutes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **hydraulic conductivity**. Derive the capillary rise equation (Jurin's Law) and explain how pore size affects soil water availability with numerical examples for sand (pore radius 50 μm) and clay (pore radius 0.1 μm). **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Infiltration Capacity (f_c):**
The maximum rate at which a given soil surface can absorb water at a given instant, expressed in mm/hr or cm/hr. It is a **time-dependent** property, not a constant.

**Horton's Equation:**

$$f_c(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$$

where:
- $f_0$ = initial infiltration capacity
- $f_c$ = final (steady) infiltration capacity
- $k$ = decay constant (depends on soil and cover)

**Limitations of Horton's Equation:**

1. **Empirical** — no physical basis; parameters must be measured for each soil.
2. Assumes ponded conditions throughout.
3. **Long-time validity:** predicts approach to $f_c = K_{sat}$, which is correct, but the exponential form may not fit the actual physics for layered soils.
4. Cannot handle **rainfall variability** directly — needs modification (Mein–Larson extension).
5. Parameters $f_0$, $f_c$, $k$ change with initial moisture, cover, and season.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $f_0 = 80$ mm/hr, $f_c = 10$ mm/hr, $k = 3$ hr⁻¹, $t = 30$ min = 0.5 hr.

**Infiltration Rate at $t = 0.5$ hr:**

$$f_c(0.5) = 10 + (80 - 10) e^{-3 \times 0.5}$$

$$= 10 + 70 \cdot e^{-1.5}$$

$$= 10 + 70 \times 0.2231$$

$$= 10 + 15.62$$

$$\boxed{f_c(0.5) = 25.62 \text{ mm/hr}}$$

**Cumulative Infiltration at $t = 0.5$ hr:**

$$F(t) = f_c \cdot t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k} \left[ 1 - e^{-kt} \right]$$

$$F(0.5) = 10 \times 0.5 + \frac{(80-10)}{3} \left[ 1 - e^{-1.5} \right]$$

$$= 5 + \frac{70}{3} \times (1 - 0.2231)$$

$$= 5 + 23.33 \times 0.7769$$

$$= 5 + 18.13$$

$$\boxed{F(0.5) = 23.13 \text{ mm}}$$

### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Hydraulic Conductivity (K):**
The proportionality constant in Darcy's Law that quantifies the ease with which water moves through soil pores. It has units of velocity (cm/hr or m/day) and depends on:
- **Soil texture** (pore size distribution)
- **Soil structure** (cracks, macropores)
- **Water content** (saturated vs unsaturated)
- **Temperature** (via water viscosity)

**Derivation of Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise):**

Consider a capillary tube of radius $r$ dipped in water. The water rises to height $h_c$ where the upward **surface tension force** equals the downward **weight of the water column**.

**Upward force** (surface tension acting along the tube circumference):

$$F_{up} = 2 \pi r \cdot \sigma \cos \alpha$$

where $\sigma$ = surface tension (N/m), $\alpha$ = contact angle (≈ 0° for water-glass).

**Downward force** (weight of water column of height $h_c$):

$$F_{down} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot h_c$$

**At equilibrium** ($F_{up} = F_{down}$):

$$2 \pi r \cdot \sigma \cos \alpha = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot h_c$$

Solving for $h_c$:

$$\boxed{h_c = \frac{2 \sigma \cos \alpha}{\rho_w \cdot g \cdot r}}$$

**Numerical Examples:**

Using $\sigma = 0.0728$ N/m, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\alpha = 0°$:

**For Sand (r = 50 μm = 50 × 10⁻⁶ m):**

$$h_c = \frac{2 \times 0.0728}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{0.1456}{0.4905} = \boxed{0.297 \text{ m} \approx 30 \text{ cm}}$$

**For Clay (r = 0.1 μm = 0.1 × 10⁻⁶ m):**

$$h_c = \frac{2 \times 0.0728}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{0.1456}{0.000981} = \boxed{148.4 \text{ m}}$$

**Interpretation:**

| Soil | Pore Radius | Capillary Rise | Practical Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sand | 50 μm | 0.3 m | Water rises only to shallow depth — root zone dries quickly |
| Clay | 0.1 μm | 148 m (theoretical) | Water held tightly in micro-pores; plant cannot extract (held at suction > 1500 kPa = PWP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This counter-intuitive result explains the **clay paradox**: Clay has enormous *theoretical* capillary capacity but very low *practical* plant-available water because the pores are **too small** for root extraction. Sandy soils have low capillary rise but the water *that is held* (in the 1–50 μm range) is **easily extractable** by plants.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
>
> 1. **Units Confusion in Available Water:** Students frequently compute AW in **%** but forget to convert to **depth (mm)** using root zone depth. *Examiner penalty: 1–2 marks deducted per occurrence.*
>
> 2. **PWP vs Hygroscopic Confusion:** PWP = **15 bar**; Hygroscopic coefficient = **31 bar**. They are NOT the same. PWP defines *plant* unavailability; hygroscopic defines *physical* retention.
>
> 3. **Infiltration Rate vs Cumulative Infiltration:** $f_c(t)$ is rate (mm/hr); $F(t)$ is depth (mm). Mixing these up in numerical problems costs **3–4 marks** instantly.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in Darcy's Law:** Water flows from high head to low head — the gradient sign convention matters. Examiners check this.
>
> 5. **Horton's "k" vs Kostiakov's "k":** They are **completely different parameters**. Horton's k has units of **hr⁻¹**; Kostiakov's k is dimensionless. Writing the wrong units = 0 marks for that step.
>
> 6. **Not Stating Assumptions:** In derivations (Green–Ampt, Richards), state assumptions: 1-D flow, uniform soil, sharp wetting front, etc. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for assumptions.
>
> 7. **Matric Potential Sign:** Matric potential is **always negative** in unsaturated soil. Writing it as positive is a fundamental conceptual error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Mastery Verification:**

### Soil–Water–Plant Fundamentals
- [ ] Soil serves as a **water reservoir** with limited storage (Field Capacity is the upper limit).
- [ ] **Available Water = FC − PWP** (volumetric basis).
- [ ] **Total Available Water (TAW) = AW × Root Depth** (expressed as mm).
- [ ] **Readily Available Water (RAW) = p × TAW** — trigger irrigation before RAW is exhausted.
- [ ] **Saturation → FC:** 2–3 days drainage; tension ≈ 1/3 bar.
- [ ] **FC → PWP:** tension increases to 15 bar; plants progressively stressed.
- [ ] **PWP → Hygroscopic:** tension > 31 bar; water held by adsorption, unavailable.

### Soil Water Movement
- [ ] **Darcy's Law (saturated):** $q = -K_{sat} \cdot \dfrac{dH}{dx}$ — valid for laminar flow.
- [ ] **Richards' Equation (unsaturated):** $\dfrac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[K(\theta)\dfrac{\partial \Psi}{\partial x}\right]$ — no closed-form solution.
- [ ] **K_sat** varies over **7 orders of magnitude** across soil textures.
- [ ] **Jurin's Law:** $h_c = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\alpha}{\rho_w g r}$ — smaller pores → higher (but slower) capillary rise.

### Infiltration Models
- [ ] **Horton:** $f_c(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)e^{-kt}$ — best for long-duration events.
- [ ] **Philip:** $f_c(t) = \frac{1}{2}St^{-1/2} + A$ — best for short-time, ponded conditions; $S$ = sorptivity.
- [ ] **Kostiakov:** $f_c(t) = akt^{k-1}$ — simplest empirical; fails at large $t$.
- [ ] **Green–Ampt:** $F(t) = K_{sat}t + \psi\Delta\theta\ln\left(1 + \dfrac{F}{\psi\Delta\theta}\right)$ — implicit, physical basis.

### High-Yield Numerical Patterns
- [ ] Always convert θ from % to **decimal** before multiplying by depth.
- [ ] Always state **units** explicitly in the final answer.
- [ ] In Horton's, verify $t = 0 \Rightarrow F = 0$ as a sanity check.
- [ ] In capillary rise, remember: smaller pores = higher rise = tighter water = LESS available.

### Key Constants to Memorize
- [ ] **Surface tension of water:** $\sigma = 0.0728$ N/m (at 20°C)
- [ ] **Density of water:** $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³
- [ ] **FC tension:** 1/3 bar = **33 kPa**
- [ ] **PWP tension:** 15 bar = **1500 kPa**
- [ ] **Hygroscopic tension:** 31 bar = **3100 kPa**

### Conceptual Clarity Check
- [ ] Why is clay's *available* water less than its *theoretical* capacity? *(Answer: pores too small for root extraction.)*
- [ ] Why do sandy soils need more frequent irrigation despite high infiltration? *(Answer: low AW, water drains past root zone quickly.)*
- [ ] What is the physical meaning of "negative" matric potential? *(Answer: water is under tension; energy state below free water.)*
- [ ] How does infiltration differ from percolation? *(Answer: infiltration = surface entry; percolation = subsurface movement.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Module 1 questions in KTU typically combine **2 marks definition + 3 marks explanation + 7 marks numerical** for Part B. Practice both **derivation** (Horton, Green–Ampt) and **numerical computation** (FC–PWP problems, infiltration rate calculation) equally. The **relationship between Darcy's Law, Richards' Equation, and the SWCC** is a frequently asked **7-mark conceptual question** — draw the diagram to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Evapotranspiration - Crop Water Requirement, Irrigation Requirement, Irrigation Scheduling and Irrigation Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Determination of Evapotranspiration - Crop Water Requirement, Irrigation Requirement, Irrigation Scheduling and Irrigation Efficiencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Evapotranspiration (ET)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Evapotranspiration (ET)** is the combined process by which water is transferred from the Earth's surface to the atmosphere through **evaporation** from soil and water surfaces plus **transpiration** from plants. It is conventionally expressed in **mm/day** or **mm/season**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$ET_c = E + T$$

where $E$ = evaporation component and $T$ = transpiration component.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Cooling Bill" of a Field:**
> Imagine a green lawn in summer as a small factory. The grass leaves act like radiators; water inside the plant evaporates to keep the plant cool, just like sweat cools a runner. At the same time, the bare soil around the grass also loses moisture by direct sun-baking. The **total water lost** from both these processes together is *evapotranspiration*. If you don't refill that "water tank" (irrigate), the factory shuts down — the plant wilts.

### Types of Evapotranspiration

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Reference Crop Evapotranspiration | $ET_o$ | ET from a hypothetical grass reference crop (height 0.12 m, surface resistance 70 s/m, albedo 0.23) |
| Potential Evapotranspiration | $PET$ | ET that would occur if water is abundantly available |
| Actual Crop Evapotranspiration | $ET_c$ | ET actually occurring under field conditions |
| Crop ET under non-standard conditions | $ET_c \, adj$ | ET under water stress, salinity, or disease |

---

## 1.2 Crop Water Requirement (CWR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Crop Water Requirement (CWR)** is the total quantity of water needed by a crop from sowing to harvest, including evapotranspiration, metabolic needs, and water for salt leaching, expressed in **mm** or **m³/ha**.

The general relationship is:

$$CWR = ET_c + \text{Leaching Requirement} + \text{Miscellaneous Needs}$$

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Daily Diet" of a Plant:**
> Think of Crop Water Requirement as the **calorie requirement** of a human being — it varies by age, activity, weight, and weather. A rice plant in tropical Kerala during summer has a much higher "water diet" than a wheat plant in winter. CWR tells the farmer *how much* water the plant actually consumes.

---

## 1.3 Irrigation Requirement (IR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Irrigation Requirement (IR)** is the quantity of water that must be supplied through irrigation to meet the crop water demand, after accounting for **effective rainfall** and **soil moisture contribution**. It is of two types:
> - **Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR)**
> - **Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR)**

$$NIR = CWR - \text{Effective Rainfall (ER)}$$

$$GIR = \dfrac{NIR}{\text{Overall Irrigation Efficiency}\,(\eta_o)}$$

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Net Salary" Concept:**
> Think of your gross salary minus tax and PF deductions = take-home (net) salary. Similarly, the **Total Water Need (CWR)** minus the **free water received from rainfall (ER)** = the **Net Water You Must Pump (NIR)**. But because some water is lost in pipes, channels, and field spills, you need to pump more. That extra amount is the **Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR)**.

---

## 1.4 Irrigation Scheduling

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Irrigation Scheduling** is the scientific decision-making process that determines **when** to irrigate and **how much** water to apply at each irrigation event, based on soil moisture status, crop growth stage, and climatic conditions.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — Feeding a Baby on Schedule:**
> A mother doesn't feed her baby randomly — she watches for hunger cues (crying), weighs the baby, and feeds at fixed intervals based on age. **Irrigation scheduling** is exactly the same: we monitor the "thirst cues" (soil moisture depletion, leaf wilting, weather forecast) and feed the crop at the right time and right quantity. Under-irrigation = hunger; over-irrigation = indigestion and root rot!

---

## 1.5 Irrigation Efficiencies

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Irrigation Efficiency** is the ratio of water beneficially used by crops to the total water diverted from the source. It quantifies the losses occurring in **conveyance**, **distribution**, **application**, and **storage**.

The classical chain of efficiencies is:

$$\eta_o = \eta_c \times \eta_a = \eta_{con} \times \eta_{app} \times \eta_a$$

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" Theory:**
> Imagine you are filling a plant pot from a tap using a long hose. Some water leaks from the hose joints (**conveyance loss**), some spills on the path before reaching the pot (**distribution loss**), some splashes off the soil (**application loss**), and only a fraction actually soaks the root zone. **Irrigation efficiency** simply measures what % of the water you pulled from the tap actually reached the plant roots. A good KTU student should always design to minimize these losses!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Methods of Determining Evapotranspiration

The determination of ET is the **foundation of all irrigation design**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following methods:

### 2.1.1 Lysimeter Method (Direct Measurement)

- A **lysimeter** is a large container filled with soil and crops, installed in a field to directly measure water balance.
- It is the most accurate method but expensive.

The water balance equation:

$$ET = P + I - R - D \pm \Delta S$$

where:
- $P$ = Precipitation
- $I$ = Irrigation applied
- $R$ = Surface runoff
- $D$ = Deep percolation
- $\Delta S$ = Change in soil moisture storage

### 2.1.2 Pan Evaporation Method (FAO Recommended)

- A **Class A Evaporation Pan** (circular, 1210 mm diameter, 255 mm depth) is used.
- Pan evaporation is multiplied by a **pan coefficient ($K_p$)** to estimate reference ET.

$$ET_o = K_p \times E_{pan}$$

Typical value of $K_p = \mathbf{0.70}$ to **0.80** for Class A pan.

### 2.1.3 Blaney-Criddle Method (Temperature Based)

Uses only mean air temperature and daylight hours:

$$ET_o = \dfrac{p \times (0.46 \times T_{mean} + 8.13)}{100}$$

where:
- $p$ = Mean daily percentage of annual daytime hours (from tables)
- $T_{mean}$ = Mean monthly temperature in °C

### 2.1.4 Hargreaves Method (Temperature + Radiation Based)

$$ET_o = 0.0023 \times (T_{mean} + 17.8) \times (T_{max} - T_{min})^{0.5} \times R_a$$

where $R_a$ = extraterrestrial radiation (mm/day equivalent).

### 2.1.5 Penman Method (Combination Method) — Most Rigorous

Combines energy balance with aerodynamic transport:

$$ET_o = \dfrac{\Delta}{\Delta + \gamma}(R_n) + \dfrac{\gamma}{\Delta + \gamma}(E_a)$$

where:
- $\Delta$ = Slope of saturation vapor pressure curve (kPa/°C)
- $\gamma$ = Psychrometric constant ($\approx$ 0.066 kPa/°C)
- $R_n$ = Net radiation (mm/day equivalent)
- $E_a$ = Aerodynamic term

### 2.1.6 Penman-Monteith Method (FAO-56 Standard)

The current international standard defined by **FAO Irrigation & Drainage Paper 56**:

$$ET_o = \dfrac{0.408 \, \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \dfrac{900}{T+273} u_2 (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma(1 + 0.34 u_2)}$$

where:
- $G$ = Soil heat flux density
- $u_2$ = Wind speed at 2 m height
- $e_s - e_a$ = Vapor pressure deficit

### 2.1.7 Crop Coefficient Approach

Once $ET_o$ is known, actual crop ET is:

$$ET_c = K_c \times ET_o$$

where $K_c$ = Crop coefficient (tabulated for each crop and growth stage: Initial, Development, Mid-season, Late-season).

For water-stressed conditions:

$$ET_c \, adj = K_s \times K_c \times ET_o$$

where $K_s$ = Water stress coefficient (0 to 1).

---

## 2.2 Effective Rainfall

Not all rainfall is useful; some is lost to runoff and deep percolation.

$$ER = P - R_{off} - D_p$$

Common empirical formulas:
- **USDA Soil Conservation Service:** $ER = P \times \left(\dfrac{125 - 0.2P}{125}\right)$ for $P \leq 250$ mm
- **FAO-23:** $ER = 0.8 P$ (for monthly basis)

---

## 2.3 Net and Gross Irrigation Requirement

$$NIR = ET_c - ER$$

$$GIR = \dfrac{NIR}{\eta_a}$$

where $\eta_a$ = field application efficiency.

For full irrigation accounting including leaching:

$$NIR = \dfrac{ET_c - ER}{1 - LR}$$

where $LR = \dfrac{EC_w}{5 \times EC_e - EC_w}$ (leaching ratio for salinity control).

---

## 2.4 Irrigation Efficiencies — Detailed Breakdown

| Efficiency | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Water Conveyance Efficiency | $\eta_c$ | Ratio of water delivered at field to water diverted at source | **60–80%** |
| Water Application Efficiency | $\eta_a$ | Ratio of water stored in root zone to water delivered to field | **60–70%** |
| Water Distribution Efficiency | $\eta_d$ | Uniformity of water distribution over field | **70–85%** |
| Water Storage Efficiency | $\eta_s$ | Ability to store water in root zone against drainage | **60–80%** |
| Consumptive Use Efficiency | $\eta_{cu}$ | Ratio of consumptive use to irrigation water applied | **50–70%** |
| Overall Project Efficiency | $\eta_o$ | Combined effect of all efficiencies | **30–60%** |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table and memorize it — these are the equations that appear in every KTU ESE question on this topic!**

| # | Equation | Purpose | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $ET = P + I - R - D \pm \Delta S$ | Lysimeter water balance | mm |
| 2 | $ET_o = K_p \times E_{pan}$ | Pan evaporation method | mm/day |
| 3 | $ET_o = \dfrac{p}{100}[0.46T_{mean}+8.13]$ | Blaney-Criddle | mm/day |
| 4 | $ET_o = 0.0023(T+17.8)(T_{max}-T_{min})^{0.5}R_a$ | Hargreaves | mm/day |
| 5 | $ET_o = \dfrac{\Delta}{\Delta+\gamma}R_n + \dfrac{\gamma}{\Delta+\gamma}E_a$ | Penman | mm/day |
| 6 | $ET_o = \dfrac{0.408\Delta(R_n-G)+\gamma\frac{900}{T+273}u_2(e_s-e_a)}{\Delta+\gamma(1+0.34u_2)}$ | Penman-Monteith (FAO-56) | mm/day |
| 7 | $ET_c = K_c \times ET_o$ | Actual crop ET | mm/day |
| 8 | $ER = 0.8P$ (monthly) | Effective rainfall | mm |
| 9 | $NIR = ET_c - ER$ | Net irrigation requirement | mm |
| 10 | $NIR = \dfrac{ET_c - ER}{1-LR}$ | NIR with leaching | mm |
| 11 | $LR = \dfrac{EC_w}{5EC_e - EC_w}$ | Leaching requirement | dimensionless |
| 12 | $GIR = \dfrac{NIR}{\eta_a}$ | Gross irrigation requirement | mm |
| 13 | $V = GIR \times A$ | Volume of water to be supplied | m³ |
| 14 | $\eta_o = \eta_c \times \eta_a$ | Overall efficiency | dimensionless |
| 15 | $d = \dfrac{S_{MAD}}{100} \times \frac{ASW}{100}$ | Readily available moisture depth | mm |

where:
- $S_{MAD}$ = Soil moisture allowable depletion (%)
- $ASW$ = Available soil water (mm/m depth)

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU Civil Engineering cover this?**
> In Kerala's tropical climate with two monsoons, engineers designing dams, canals, lift-irrigation schemes, and drip systems (like the **Jaldoot** and **PMKSY** projects) must precisely estimate ET to size reservoirs and pipe networks. A 10% error in ET estimation can cause crores of rupees in either waterlogging (over-design) or crop failure (under-design). These equations are the **bedrock of every irrigation design software**, from CROPWAT to WEAP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Full Irrigation Requirement Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a classic 14-mark KTU ESE pattern question. Practice this stepwise.**

### Problem Statement
A field of area **10 hectares** is to be irrigated for **wheat** cultivation. The following data are given:
- Reference ET ($ET_o$) for the growing season = **5.5 mm/day**
- Crop coefficient $K_c$ values:
  - Initial stage (20 days): $K_{c1} = 0.5$
  - Development stage (30 days): $K_{c2} = 0.75$ (avg)
  - Mid-season (60 days): $K_{c3} = 1.10$
  - Late season (40 days): $K_{c4} = 0.65$ (avg)
- Effective rainfall during the season = **120 mm**
- Field application efficiency, $\eta_a$ = **70%**
- Conveyance efficiency, $\eta_c$ = **80%**
- Bulk density of soil = 1.4 g/cc
- Depth of root zone = 0.9 m
- Field capacity = 28%, Permanent wilting point = 12%
- Water stress occurs when 50% of available water is depleted.

**Determine:**
1. Total Crop Water Requirement (in mm and m³)
2. Net Irrigation Requirement
3. Gross Irrigation Requirement
4. Volume of water to be pumped at the head works
5. Frequency of irrigation during the mid-season stage

---

### Step 1: Calculate Stage-wise ETc

$$ET_c = K_c \times ET_o$$

**Initial Stage:**
$$ET_{c1} = 0.5 \times 5.5 = 2.75 \, \text{mm/day}$$
$$\text{Total}_1 = 2.75 \times 20 = 55 \, \text{mm}$$

**Development Stage:**
$$ET_{c2} = 0.75 \times 5.5 = 4.125 \, \text{mm/day}$$
$$\text{Total}_2 = 4.125 \times 30 = 123.75 \, \text{mm}$$

**Mid-Season Stage:**
$$ET_{c3} = 1.10 \times 5.5 = 6.05 \, \text{mm/day}$$
$$\text{Total}_3 = 6.05 \times 60 = 363 \, \text{mm}$$

**Late Season Stage:**
$$ET_{c4} = 0.65 \times 5.5 = 3.575 \, \text{mm/day}$$
$$\text{Total}_4 = 3.575 \times 40 = 143 \, \text{mm}$$

### Step 2: Total Seasonal Crop Water Requirement

$$\begin{aligned}
CWR &= 55 + 123.75 + 363 + 143 \\
CWR &= 684.75 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

In volume:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CWR} &= CWR \times A \\
&= 684.75 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} \times 10 \times 10^4 \, \text{m}^2 \\
&= 68475 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Net Irrigation Requirement

$$\begin{aligned}
NIR &= CWR - ER \\
&= 684.75 - 120 \\
&= 564.75 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

In volume:
$$V_{NIR} = 564.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^4 = 56475 \, \text{m}^3$$

### Step 4: Gross Irrigation Requirement

Overall efficiency first:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_o &= \eta_c \times \eta_a \\
&= 0.80 \times 0.70 \\
&= 0.56
\end{aligned}$$

Now:

$$\begin{aligned}
GIR &= \frac{NIR}{\eta_o} \\
&= \frac{564.75}{0.56} \\
&= 1008.48 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

In volume:

$$V_{GIR} = 1008.48 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^4 = 100848 \, \text{m}^3$$

### Step 5: Frequency of Irrigation During Mid-Season

**Available Soil Water (ASW):**
$$\begin{aligned}
ASW &= \frac{(FC - PWP)}{100} \times \rho_b \times d \\
&= \frac{(28 - 12)}{100} \times 1.4 \times 900 \, (\text{mm}) \\
&= 0.16 \times 1.4 \times 900 \\
&= 201.6 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Readily Available Moisture (RAM):**
$$\begin{aligned}
RAM &= ASW \times MAD \\
&= 201.6 \times 0.50 \\
&= 100.8 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Daily ET during mid-season:**
$$ET_{c3} = 6.05 \, \text{mm/day}$$

**Irrigation Interval:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Interval} &= \frac{RAM}{ET_{c3}} \\
&= \frac{100.8}{6.05} \\
&= 16.66 \approx \mathbf{17 \, \text{days}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of ET Estimation Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently asked in KTU as Part A (3 marks) or Part B sub-part (a).**

| Method | Data Required | Accuracy | Cost | Suitability for Kerala |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Lysimeter | All water balance terms | **Highest** | Very High | Research stations only |
| Pan Evaporation | Pan reading, humidity, wind | High | Low | **Highly Suitable** |
| Blaney-Criddle | Temperature, latitude | Moderate | Very Low | When data is scarce |
| Hargreaves | Max/Min temp, radiation | Moderate-Good | Low | **Suitable** |
| Penman | Temp, humidity, wind, radiation | Very High | Moderate | Good |
| Penman-Monteith | Full met data | **Highest (Standard)** | High | **Best for precision work** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Penman Equation (Board Favorite)

The Penman equation combines the **energy balance** with the **aerodynamic** approach.

**Energy balance for evaporation:**

$$R_n = \lambda E + H + G$$

where $\lambda E$ = latent heat flux, $H$ = sensible heat, $G$ = soil heat flux.

**Bowen ratio:**
$$\beta = \frac{H}{\lambda E} = \gamma \frac{(T_s - T_a)}{(e_s - e_a)} \cdot \frac{P_a}{P}$$

Penman rearranged the energy balance using this Bowen ratio and obtained the classic form:

$$E = \dfrac{\Delta}{\Delta + \gamma}(R_n - G) + \dfrac{\gamma}{\Delta + \gamma}E_a$$

The first term represents the **radiation term** (energy available), while the second term is the **aerodynamic term** (vapor transport by wind). This elegant combination is what makes Penman so accurate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Irrigation Water Requirement Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Climatic Data T, RH, Wind, Radiation] --> B[Calculate ET_o Reference ET]
    B --> C[Apply Crop Coefficient K_c]
    C --> D[Calculate ET_c Crop Evapotranspiration]
    D --> E[Add Leaching Requirement LR]
    E --> F[Determine CWR Crop Water Requirement]
    F --> G[Subtract Effective Rainfall ER]
    G --> H[Net Irrigation Requirement NIR]
    H --> I[Apply Overall Efficiency eta_o]
    I --> J[Gross Irrigation Requirement GIR]
    J --> K[Volume of Water m3]
    K --> L[Canal Reservoir Pump Design]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Irrigation Efficiency Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Water Diverted at Source Q_in] --> B[Conveyance Channel eta_c]
    B --> C[Water Delivered at Field Q_f]
    C --> D[Field Application eta_a]
    D --> E[Water Stored in Root Zone Q_s]
    E --> F[Consumptive Use ET_c]
    F --> G[Beneficial Use]
    A2[Seepage Evaporation] -.Loss.-> B
    B2[Percolation Evaporation] -.Loss.-> C
    C2[Runoff Deep Percolation] -.Loss.-> D
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Irrigation Scheduling Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Growing Season] --> B[Determine Field Capacity FC and PWP]
    B --> C[Track Daily ET_c]
    C --> D{Available Soil Water less than MAD?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Determine Depth of Water to Apply]
    E --> F[Apply Irrigation]
    F --> G[Recharge Root Zone to FC]
    G --> C
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — ET Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Evapotranspiration Estimation Methods] --> B1[Direct Lysimeter]
    A --> B2[Empirical]
    A --> B3[Semi-Empirical]
    A --> B4[Combination]
    B2 --> C1[Blaney-Criddle Temperature Only]
    B2 --> C2[Hargreaves Temp + Radiation]
    B3 --> C3[Pan Evaporation Method]
    B4 --> C4[Penman Energy + Aerodynamic]
    B4 --> C5[Penman-Monteith FAO-56 Standard]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Reference Crop Evapotranspiration (ET₀). What are its standard reference conditions?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Crop Evapotranspiration (ET₀)** is defined as the rate of evapotranspiration from a hypothetical reference crop (similar to an extensive surface of green grass of uniform height, actively growing, well-watered, and completely shading the ground).

**FAO-56 Standard Reference Conditions:**
1. Crop height: **0.12 m**
2. Surface (canopy) resistance: **70 s/m**
3. Albedo: **0.23**
4. Soil heat flux density for daily periods: **~0**

It is expressed in **mm/day** and forms the base for computing actual crop ET using $ET_c = K_c \times ET_o$.

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR) and Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR) | Gross Irrigation Requirement (GIR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Quantity of water needed by crop after deducting effective rainfall | Quantity of water to be applied at the source to meet NIR |
| Formula | $NIR = ET_c - ER$ | $GIR = NIR / \eta_o$ |
| Accounts for | Crop water need and natural rain | All conveyance and application losses |
| Use | Sizing of root zone storage | Sizing of canal, pump, and reservoir |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **Penman-Monteith method** of estimating reference evapotranspiration, stating clearly its data requirements and its standing as the FAO standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sugarcane crop is irrigated for 150 days. The crop coefficient values during the four growth stages are:
- Initial (20 days): $K_{c1} = 0.40$
- Development (35 days): avg $K_{c2} = 0.85$
- Mid-season (60 days): $K_{c3} = 1.25$
- Late season (35 days): avg $K_{c4} = 0.75$

If the average $ET_o$ for the season is **5.0 mm/day** and effective rainfall is **220 mm**, determine:
1. Total Crop Water Requirement in mm and m³ for a **20-hectare** field.
2. Net and Gross Irrigation Requirement if field application efficiency is **65%** and conveyance efficiency is **85%**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Penman-Monteith Method (7 marks)

The **Penman-Monteith method** is the FAO-recommended standard for estimating reference crop evapotranspiration ($ET_o$). It is a physically-based combination equation that integrates:

**1. Energy Balance Component** — captures the energy available for ET ($R_n - G$).
**2. Aerodynamic Component** — captures the vapor removal by wind.

**The FAO-56 Equation:**

$$ET_o = \dfrac{0.408 \, \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \dfrac{900}{T + 273} u_2 (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + 0.34 u_2)}$$

where:
- $ET_o$ = Reference evapotranspiration (mm/day)
- $R_n$ = Net radiation at crop surface (MJ/m²/day)
- $G$ = Soil heat flux density (MJ/m²/day)
- $T$ = Mean daily air temperature at 2 m height (°C)
- $u_2$ = Wind speed at 2 m height (m/s)
- $e_s$ = Saturation vapor pressure (kPa)
- $e_a$ = Actual vapor pressure (kPa)
- $e_s - e_a$ = Vapor pressure deficit (kPa)
- $\Delta$ = Slope of saturation vapor pressure curve (kPa/°C)
- $\gamma$ = Psychrometric constant (kPa/°C)

**Data Requirements:**
- Air temperature (max/min)
- Humidity (RH)
- Wind speed
- Solar radiation
- Atmospheric pressure (or altitude)

**Why the FAO Standard?**
- It can be applied globally without local calibration.
- Valid for both arid and humid climates.
- Provides consistency across regions.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Stage-wise ET_c**

$$\begin{aligned}
ET_{c1} &= 0.40 \times 5.0 = 2.0 \, \text{mm/day}, \quad \text{Total} = 2.0 \times 20 = 40 \, \text{mm} \\
ET_{c2} &= 0.85 \times 5.0 = 4.25 \, \text{mm/day}, \quad \text{Total} = 4.25 \times 35 = 148.75 \, \text{mm} \\
ET_{c3} &= 1.25 \times 5.0 = 6.25 \, \text{mm/day}, \quad \text{Total} = 6.25 \times 60 = 375 \, \text{mm} \\
ET_{c4} &= 0.75 \times 5.0 = 3.75 \, \text{mm/day}, \quad \text{Total} = 3.75 \times 35 = 131.25 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Total Crop Water Requirement**

$$\begin{aligned}
CWR &= 40 + 148.75 + 375 + 131.25 = 695 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

In volume:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CWR} &= 695 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 10^4 = 139000 \, \text{m}^3 = 1.39 \times 10^5 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Net Irrigation Requirement**

$$\begin{aligned}
NIR &= CWR - ER = 695 - 220 = 475 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{NIR} &= 475 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 10^4 = 95000 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Gross Irrigation Requirement**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_o &= \eta_c \times \eta_a = 0.85 \times 0.65 = 0.5525 \\
GIR &= \frac{NIR}{\eta_o} = \frac{475}{0.5525} = 859.73 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{GIR} &= 859.73 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 10^4 = 171945 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- CWR = **695 mm** = **1,39,000 m³**
- NIR = **475 mm** = **95,000 m³**
- GIR = **859.73 mm** = **1,71,945 m³**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **components of irrigation efficiencies**. Derive the relationship between overall efficiency, conveyance efficiency, and application efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field is irrigated by **border strip method**. The following data are given:
- Field length = **120 m**, width = **60 m**
- Depth of water applied = **75 mm**
- Depth of water stored in root zone = **55 mm**
- Soil moisture deficiency = **50 mm**
- Water delivered to the field = **540 m³**

**Calculate:**
1. Water conveyance efficiency, $\eta_c$
2. Water application efficiency, $\eta_a$
3. Water distribution efficiency, $\eta_d$
4. Water storage efficiency, $\eta_s$ **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Irrigation Efficiencies (7 marks)

The various irrigation efficiencies are explained through a flow diagram showing water flowing from source → conveyance → field → root zone → plant.

**Key Efficiencies:**

1. **Water Conveyance Efficiency ($\eta_c$)** — accounts for seepage and evaporation losses while water travels from reservoir to field.
$$\eta_c = \dfrac{W_f}{W_r}$$

2. **Water Application Efficiency ($\eta_a$)** — accounts for losses during field application (runoff + deep percolation).
$$\eta_a = \dfrac{W_s}{W_f}$$

3. **Water Storage Efficiency ($\eta_s$)** — measures how completely the required water is stored.
$$\eta_s = \dfrac{W_s}{W_n}$$

4. **Water Distribution Efficiency ($\eta_d$)** — measures uniformity.
$$\eta_d = \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{d}\right) \times 100$$
where $y$ = average numerical deviation from mean depth, $d$ = mean depth stored.

5. **Overall Project Efficiency ($\eta_o$)** — combines all losses:
$$\eta_o = \eta_c \times \eta_a$$

**Derivation:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_c &= \frac{W_f}{W_r} \\
\eta_a &= \frac{W_s}{W_f}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_c \times \eta_a &= \frac{W_f}{W_r} \times \frac{W_s}{W_f} = \frac{W_s}{W_r} = \eta_o
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore: **$\eta_o = \eta_c \times \eta_a$**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Area $= 120 \times 60 = 7200 \, \text{m}^2$
- Volume delivered $W_f = 540 \, \text{m}^3$

**Step 1: Volume of water actually stored in root zone:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_s &= d_{stored} \times A \\
&= 55 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} \times 7200 \, \text{m}^2 \\
&= 396 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Volume needed to refill root zone (deficiency):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_n &= d_{deficit} \times A \\
&= 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 7200 \\
&= 360 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Volume of water applied to field (assume $\eta_c = 1$ if not given, then computing below):**

**Step 2: Water Conveyance Efficiency**
Not directly given without source data. If $W_r$ is not provided, mark this part as requiring source data; assume source delivers 600 m³ for illustration:

$$\eta_c = \frac{540}{600} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

**Step 3: Water Application Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_a &= \frac{W_s}{W_f} \\
&= \frac{396}{540} \\
&= 0.7333 \\
&= \mathbf{73.33\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Water Distribution Efficiency:**
Assume depths vary with average deviation $y = 8$ mm; mean stored depth $d = 55$ mm:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_d &= \left(1 - \frac{y}{d}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{8}{55}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 85.45\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Water Storage Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_s &= \frac{W_s}{W_n} \\
&= \frac{396}{360} \\
&= 1.10 \, (110\%)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> A storage efficiency **greater than 100%** indicates **over-irrigation** — the field received more water than the root zone could hold, leading to **deep percolation loss** and possible **waterlogging**. Examiners look for this critical interpretation; merely computing a number without commenting on it will cost you 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Yield Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam!**

- **ET₀** = Reference ET (hypothetical grass, FAO-56 conditions); **ET_c** = $K_c \times ET_o$.
- **Penman-Monteith (FAO-56)** is the **global standard** equation; must be memorized.
- **Crop Coefficient $K_c$** varies with growth stage: Initial → Development → Mid-season → Late.
- **Effective Rainfall (ER)**: use $ER = 0.8P$ for monthly data, USDA formula for storm data.
- **NIR** = $ET_c - ER$ (or $\frac{ET_c - ER}{1 - LR}$ if leaching needed).
- **GIR** = $NIR / \eta_o$ where $\eta_o = \eta_c \times \eta_a$.
- **Leaching Requirement**: $LR = \frac{EC_w}{5EC_e - EC_w}$ (for salinity control).
- **Irrigation Scheduling** is decided by **MAD (Management Allowed Depletion)** and **RAM**.
- **Frequency of irrigation**: $\text{Interval} = \frac{RAM}{ET_c \, \text{per day}}$.
- **Always convert mm to volume**: $V (m^3) = d(mm) \times A(ha) \times 10$.
- **Typical $\eta_c$** = 60–80%, **$\eta_a$** = 60–70%, **$\eta_o$** = 30–60% (unlined channels).
- **Kerala context**: Use pan evaporation method for quick estimates; Hargreaves for monsoons when radiation data is missing.
- Always include a **water balance equation** in lysimeter problems.
- Mention **deep percolation** and **runoff** as unavoidable losses — they're examiner favorites.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** Most KTU students lose marks by forgetting to convert mm to m³ before writing the final answer. Always state both forms. A "14-mark" numerical without proper units loses 2–3 marks instantly. Be meticulous!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Different Types of Irrigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Different Types of Irrigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Irrigation

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Irrigation** is the *artificial application of water to crops* to supplement rainfall for ensuring sustained agricultural production, particularly in areas where precipitation is inadequate, unreliable, or seasonally mis-matched with crop water requirements. The science of irrigation encompasses the planning, design, development, and management of water resources for agricultural productivity.

In broader engineering terms (as per KTU 2024 Scheme for B.Tech Civil Engineering), irrigation engineering also includes:

- **Storage of water** (reservoirs, tanks, dams)
- **Conveyance of water** (canals, pipelines)
- **Distribution of water** (canal networks, field channels)
- **Application of water** (surface, sprinkler, drip methods)
- **Removal of excess water** (drainage component)

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy**
> Think of a plant like a person drinking water. Rain is the natural drink the plant gets. But what if the rain is too little, comes at the wrong time, or stops entirely? That is where **irrigation steps in** — it is the engineered, controlled, and reliable water-supply system that ensures the plant never goes thirsty, much like a piped water supply to a house when municipal supply is irregular.

## 1.2 Why Irrigation? (Necessity of Irrigation)

> [!NOTE] **Why irrigation is essential for India and Kerala specifically**
> India receives an average annual rainfall of about **1,200 mm**, but **over 80%** of it occurs in just 4 monsoon months (June–September). Kerala, despite high rainfall (~3,000 mm/year), faces **deficient irrigation coverage** because of undulating terrain, laterite soils with low water-holding capacity, and absence of perennial rivers of the Indo-Gangetic type.

The principal reasons necessitating irrigation are:

| S.No | Reason | Engineering Significance |
|------|--------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Inadequate rainfall** | Many regions receive < 500 mm/year (e.g., Rajasthan, Gujarat) |
| 2 | **Uneven/seasonal distribution** | Monsoon-dependent agriculture leads to crop failure in dry months |
| 3 | **Growing multiple crops** | Rabi and Kharif both need assured water supply |
| 4 | **Commercial/High-yielding crops** | HYV seeds, sugarcane, rice require controlled water |
| 5 | **Drought mitigation** | Acts as insurance against meteorological droughts |
| 6 | **Reclamation of arid lands** | Desert/sterile lands can be made cultivable |

## 1.3 Advantages and Disadvantages of Irrigation

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Comparison**

| Advantages of Irrigation | Disadvantages of Irrigation |
|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Increased crop yield (often 2× to 4×) | High initial capital cost (dams, canals) |
| Assured agricultural production | Waterlogging and salinization if over-irrigated |
| Cultivation of cash/commercial crops | May cause soil erosion (uncontrolled flooding) |
| Reclamation of alkaline / saline soils | Loss of land to waterlogging |
| Domestic & industrial water supply | High maintenance and operational cost |
| Generation of hydro-electric power | Displacement of population (reservoir submergence) |
| Inland navigation, fisheries | Health hazards (vector-borne diseases) |
| Recharges ground water table | Ecological disturbance |

## 1.4 Scope of Irrigation Engineering in India

> [!NOTE] **Key Statistics for KTU Answers**
> - India has the **largest irrigated area in the world** (~73 million hectares as on date).
> - About **48%** of India's cultivated land is irrigated.
> - Major irrigation projects: Bhakra Nangal, Indira Gandhi Canal, Hirakud, Nagarjuna Sagar.
> - Kerala's major irrigation projects: Neyyar, Idukki (multi-purpose), Periyar Valley, Kallada, Peechi.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Irrigation – Master Framework

The KTU 2024 Scheme follows the **two-tier classification** recommended by the Indian Standard codes and the Irrigation Commission of India:

### Tier 1 – Based on **Source of Water & Operational Mode**

> [!NOTE] **The Three Principal Modes**
> 1. **Surface Irrigation (Flow Irrigation)** – water flows over the land surface by gravity
> 2. **Sub-Surface Irrigation** – water is applied below the ground surface
> 3. **Sprinkler Irrigation** – water is sprayed as artificial rain
> 4. **Drip / Trickle Irrigation** – water is applied drop-by-drop at the root zone

### Tier 2 – Based on **Method of Water Application**

Each mode has several sub-methods, classified based on the *way water is conveyed and distributed* to the field.

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Irrigation Types

### A. Surface (Flow) Irrigation

> [!IMPORTANT] **Most common in India** – covers nearly **80% of irrigated area**.

Water is conveyed from a higher source (river/reservoir) through canals and applied to the field by gravity flow.

#### A.1 Flooding Method
The entire field surface is uniformly covered with water.

| Sub-Type | Description | Suitability |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Wild Flooding** | Water released from field channel with no control; no bunds | Pasture land, meadows |
| **Controlled Flooding** | Field is divided into smaller plots by levees/bunds | Close-growing crops |
| **Check Flooding** | Field divided into small checks (rectangular basins) of 0.05–0.2 ha | Suitable for nearly all close-growing crops |
| **Border Flooding (Strip Flooding)** | Long, narrow strips separated by low borders; water flows down the strip | Wheat, fodder, groundnut |
| **Zigzag Flooding** | Water forced to follow a zigzag path to increase contact time | Sloping fields |

> [!NOTE] **Why the names matter in KTU exams**: Examiners test students on the *operational distinction* between Wild vs. Controlled vs. Check flooding. Always mention the **size of plot / boundary conditions** in answers.

#### A.2 Furrow Irrigation
- Small parallel channels (furrows) carry water between crop rows.
- Suitable for **row crops** like cotton, sugarcane, potato, maize.
- Two variants: **Straight Furrows** and **Contour Furrows**.
- **Advantages**: Less water, less soil erosion, suitable for uneven land.

#### A.3 Contour Farming
- Field operations (ploughing, sowing) and irrigation follow the natural **contour lines** of the slope.
- Used in **hilly regions** (relevant to Kerala's laterite topography).

#### A.4 Basin Irrigation
- A flat, level area surrounded by bunds on all four sides.
- Water is allowed to stand in the basin until infiltration is complete.
- Used for **paddy (rice) cultivation** – extremely important in Kerala.
- Two types: **Rectangular Basins** and **Circular Basins** (for fruit trees).

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Memory Tip**: Paddy = Basin; Wheat/Fodder = Border; Cotton/Maize = Furrow; Hilly tea/coffee = Contour.

### B. Sub-Surface Irrigation

Water is applied **below the ground surface** through a system of open jointed drains, mole drains, or perforated pipes.

| Sub-Type | Mechanism | Drawback |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Natural Sub-Surface Irrigation** | Groundwater rises by capillary action to crop root zone | Requires high water table; limited control |
| **Artificial Sub-Surface Irrigation** | Water supplied through buried perforated pipes / open jointed drains | High installation cost; clogging of pipes |

> [!NOTE] **Use Case**: Mainly for permeable sandy soils where surface application causes excessive deep percolation losses.

### C. Sprinkler Irrigation

> [!IMPORTANT] **Also called "Overhead Irrigation" or "Mechanized Rainfall"**
> Water is sprayed through nozzles under pressure into the air, falling as artificial rain.

**Components**: Pump, main pipe, lateral pipe, risers, **sprinkler nozzles** (impact / rotary type).

**Advantages**:
- Suitable for **undulating, sandy, or shallow soils**
- Saves water (efficiency 60–70% vs. 30–40% in surface)
- No land leveling required

**Disadvantages**:
- High initial & running cost (power for pumping)
- Affected by **wind** (drift losses)
- Not suitable for very strong winds or sticky/clayey soils

### D. Drip (Trickle) Irrigation

> [!IMPORTANT] **Most water-efficient method (90–95% application efficiency)**
> Water is delivered **drop by drop** at the root zone of each plant through a network of valves, pipes, tubing, and emitters.

**Components**: Pump, filter unit, fertigation device, mainline, sub-mains, laterals, **emitters** (drippers).

**Use Case**: Coconut, banana, arecanut, pepper, cardamom, grapes, mango – **highly relevant to Kerala's plantation crops**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] **Master Formulas for Irrigation Type Selection**

| Concept | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Application |
|---------|--------------------------|------|-------------|
| Crop Water Requirement | $CWR = ET_c + P_{\text{eff}}$ | mm | Total water to be supplied |
| Consumptive Use (Blaney-Criddle) | $U = k \cdot p \cdot t$ | mm/day | Reference evapotranspiration estimate |
| Field Application Efficiency | $\eta_a = \dfrac{W_s}{W_f} \times 100$ | % | Water stored in root zone / water delivered |
| Conveyance Efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{W_f}{W_r} \times 100$ | % | Water at field / Water at source |
| Overall Project Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_c \times \eta_a \times \eta_p$ | % | Total system efficiency |
| Water Application Rate (Sprinkler) | $q = \dfrac{d \cdot s}{3600} \cdot 1000$ | L/s | Sprinkler discharge |
| Sprinkler Spacing | $S_L = 1.2 R,\ S_m = 1.5 R$ | m | Lateral & main spacing |
| Dripper Discharge | $q_d = 2 \text{ to } 8$ | L/h | Standard range |
| Wetted Area Fraction (Drip) | $f_w = \dfrac{A_w}{A_t}$ | – | Emitter wet area ratio |

**Where**:
- $W_s$ = water stored in root zone
- $W_f$ = water delivered to the field
- $W_r$ = water diverted from the source
- $R$ = wetted radius of sprinkler
- $d$ = depth of water applied, $s$ = spacing

> [!IMPORTANT] **Empirical Standards for Memory**
> - Furrow spacing: **0.6 m to 1.2 m**
> - Basin size for paddy: **0.05 ha to 0.2 ha**
> - Border strip width: **10 m to 30 m**, length: **100 m to 400 m**
> - Sprinkler pressure: **2 to 4 kg/cm²**
> - Dripper spacing: **0.5 m to 1.5 m** for vegetables, **2 m to 5 m** for orchards
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Reasoning & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 Comparative Selection Matrix (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!NOTE] **How to select an irrigation method – Decision Logic**
> 1. **Check soil type** → sandy → sprinkler/drip; clay → basin/furrow
> 2. **Check topography** → undulating → sprinkler; flat → surface
> 3. **Check water availability** → scarce → drip; abundant → surface
> 4. **Check crop type** → row crop → furrow; orchard → drip; paddy → basin
> 5. **Check capital & energy** → abundant → any; limited → surface

### Exhaustive Comparison Table

| Parameter | Surface | Sub-Surface | Sprinkler | Drip |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-----------|------|
| **Water Efficiency** | 30–45% | 50–60% | 60–75% | 85–95% |
| **Capital Cost (per ha)** | Low (₹ 25,000–50,000) | High | Medium-High | High (₹ 1,00,000+) |
| **Energy Requirement** | Lowest | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Land Leveling Needed** | Yes (high) | No | No | No |
| **Suitable Topography** | Flat/gentle slope | Flat, permeable | Undulating | Any |
| **Suitable Soil** | Medium to heavy | Sandy, permeable | Most soils | Most soils |
| **Suitable Crops** | Paddy, wheat, fodder | Pasture, orchards | Cereals, pulses | Orchards, plantation |
| **Labour Requirement** | High | Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Wind Effect** | Nil | Nil | High | Very Low |
| **Salinity Hazard** | High | Low | Medium | Very Low |
| **Fertigation Feasibility** | Difficult | Limited | Possible | Best |

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation – Water Application Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT] **Worked-out Derivation (Frequently asked in 7-mark questions)**

**Step 1: Define water stored in root zone**
The depth of water stored in the root zone after an irrigation event is $D_s$ (mm).

**Step 2: Define water delivered to the field**
Total depth of water applied at the head of the field is $D_f$ (mm).

**Step 3: Application efficiency equation**
Application efficiency is defined as the ratio of water *beneficially stored* in the root zone to the water *actually delivered* to the field.

$$\eta_a = \frac{D_s}{D_f} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

**Step 4: Expand with losses**
If $D_p$ is the deep percolation loss and $D_r$ is the surface runoff loss, then:

$$D_f = D_s + D_p + D_r$$

**Step 5: Substitute and simplify**

$$\eta_a = \frac{D_s}{D_s + D_p + D_r} \times 100$$

**Step 6: Express in volumetric terms**
For a field of area $A$ ha and depth $D$ mm:

$$\eta_a = \frac{A \cdot D_s}{A \cdot D_f} \times 100 = \frac{V_{\text{stored}}}{V_{\text{delivered}}} \times 100$$

**Real-world use**: This derivation is the **foundation of irrigation scheduling** — by minimizing $D_p$ and $D_r$, the farmer achieves higher application efficiency and saves water.

## 3.3 Derivation of Sprinkler Discharge & Spacing

**Step 1: Sprinkler application rate (mm/h)**
The application rate of a sprinkler must be less than the **basic infiltration rate** of the soil to avoid runoff.

$$q_a = \frac{Q_{\text{sp}}}{A_{\text{sp}}} \times 1000 \quad \text{(mm/h)}$$

Where $Q_{\text{sp}}$ is the sprinkler discharge in L/s and $A_{\text{sp}}$ is the wetted area in m².

**Step 2: Spacing based on wetted radius**
For a single-leg sprinkler with wetted diameter $D_w$:

$$S_L = k_1 \cdot D_w,\quad S_m = k_2 \cdot D_w$$

For the most common **square layout**:

$$S_L = 1.2 R,\quad S_m = 1.5 R$$

where $R$ is the wetted radius ($R = D_w/2$).

**Step 3: Total discharge required**
For $N$ sprinklers operating simultaneously on a lateral of length $L$:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = N \cdot q_{\text{sp}} \quad \text{(L/s)}$$

**Step 4: Power requirement of pump**
The pump power required to operate the sprinkler system:

$$P = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

Where $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $Q$ is the discharge in m³/s, and $H$ is the total head in metres.

**Real-world use**: This derivation is used to size pumps, pipes, and select nozzle sizes for sprinkler systems in hilly plantation areas.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Problem)

> [!NOTE] **Problem**: A sprinkler with a discharge of **0.6 L/s** is to be spaced on a square layout. The wetted diameter is **30 m**. Find the (a) application rate, (b) suitable spacing, (c) area covered per sprinkler, and (d) depth of water applied in **4 hours**.

**Given**:
- $Q_{\text{sp}} = 0.6$ L/s $= 0.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- $D_w = 30$ m, so $R = 15$ m
- Time of operation $t = 4$ h

**Step (a): Spacing (square layout)**
Using $S_L = 1.2 R$ and $S_m = 1.5 R$:

$$S_L = 1.2 \times 15 = 18 \text{ m}$$

$$S_m = 1.5 \times 15 = 22.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step (b): Area covered per sprinkler**

$$A_{\text{sp}} = S_L \times S_m = 18 \times 22.5 = 405 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (c): Application rate**

$$q_a = \frac{Q_{\text{sp}}}{A_{\text{sp}}} \times 1000 = \frac{0.6}{405} \times 1000 = 1.48 \text{ mm/h}$$

**Step (d): Depth of water applied in 4 hours**

$$D = q_a \times t = 1.48 \times 4 = 5.93 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineer's Check**: The application rate $1.48$ mm/h must be less than the basic infiltration rate of the soil (typically 5–15 mm/h for loam). Since 1.48 < 5, the design is **safe — no surface runoff expected**.

## 3.5 Selection Logic – Flow Chart Style Reasoning

A typical KTU 14-mark answer must follow this **step-by-step reasoning structure** when asked *"Select a suitable irrigation method for a given situation"*:

1. **Identify crop type** → Row crop / Close-growing / Orchard / Plantation
2. **Identify soil type** → Sandy / Loam / Clay / Laterite
3. **Identify topography** → Flat / Gentle slope / Steep / Undulating
4. **Identify water source** → Abundant (canal/river) / Limited (well/borewell)
5. **Identify climate** → Hot, dry, windy, humid
6. **Apply decision rule**:

> **Decision Rule (KTU Standard)**:
> - If *water source is abundant* AND *land is flat* AND *soil is medium-to-heavy* → **Surface (Basin/Furrow)**
> - If *topography is undulating* AND *water is moderate* → **Sprinkler**
> - If *water is scarce* AND *crop is orchard/plantation* AND *precision needed* → **Drip**
> - If *soil is highly permeable* AND *high water table* → **Sub-surface**

## 3.6 Historical & Modern Irrigation in India – KTU Context

> [!NOTE] **Important for 2-mark definitions in KTU exams**
> - **Inundation Irrigation**: Ancient method; water from a river is allowed to inundate (flood) the adjoining low-lying areas during floods.
> - **Perennial Irrigation**: Water is supplied throughout the year through a network of canals from a permanent storage reservoir.
> - **Khadar Irrigation**: Similar to inundation but with some control.
> - **Modern Methods**: Drip and Sprinkler, promoted under the **PMKSY (Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana)** scheme.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Irrigation Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["IRRIGATION ENGINEERING"] --> B["Surface Flow Irrigation"]
    A --> C["Sub Surface Irrigation"]
    A --> D["Sprinkler Irrigation"]
    A --> E["Drip Trickle Irrigation"]
    A --> F["Lift Irrigation"]
    A --> G["Inundation Irrigation"]
    A --> H["Perennial Irrigation"]

    B --> B1["Flooding Method"]
    B --> B2["Furrow Method"]
    B --> B3["Basin Method"]
    B --> B4["Border Strip Method"]
    B --> B5["Contour Farming"]

    B1 --> B1a["Wild Flooding"]
    B1 --> B1b["Controlled Flooding"]
    B1 --> B1c["Check Flooding"]
    B1 --> B1d["Zigzag Flooding"]

    C --> C1["Natural Sub Surface"]
    C --> C2["Artificial Sub Surface"]

    D --> D1["Rotary Impact Sprinkler"]
    D --> D2["Fixed Spray Nozzle"]
    D --> D3["Perforated Pipe System"]

    E --> E1["Surface Drip"]
    E --> E2["Sub Surface Drip"]
    E --> E3["Inline Dripper"]
    E --> E4["Online Dripper"]

    F --> F1["Well Irrigation"]
    F --> F2["Tube Well Irrigation"]
    F --> F3["Pump Irrigation"]
```

## 4.2 Sprinkler System – Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Water Source River Well"] --> B["Pump Unit Centrifugal"]
    B --> C["Filter Unit Sand Screen"]
    C --> D["Fertigation Unit Optional"]
    D --> E["Main Pipeline PVC GI"]
    E --> F["Sub Main Pipeline"]
    F --> G["Lateral Pipeline"]
    G --> H["Riser Pipe 1.5 m"]
    H --> I["Sprinkler Head Impact Rotary"]
    I --> J["Water Spray Artificial Rain"]
    J --> K["Crop Root Zone"]
```

## 4.3 Drip Irrigation System – Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Source Well Reservoir"] --> B["Pump Station"]
    B --> C["Control Valve"]

    D --> E["Fertigation Tank"]
    E --> F["Pressure Gauge"]
    F --> G["Mainline Pipe"]
    G --> H["Sub Main"]
    H --> I["Laterals with Emitters"]
    I --> J["Drippers 2 to 8 L per h"]
    J --> K["Plant Root Zone"]
    K --> L["Wetted Soil Bulb"]
```

## 4.4 Surface Irrigation Layout – Block Diagram

```mermaid
    A["Reservoir or River"] --> B["Headworks Diversion Weir"]
```
