topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Command Area - Concept, Components of CADP, Classification of Canals, Alignment of Canals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Command Area — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Command Area (CA)** is the total geographical expanse that can be physically irrigated from a given irrigation scheme, bounded by the highest contour level up to which water can be efficiently delivered by gravity flow from the canal network.

The term is rigorously used in three hierarchical sub-classifications by KTU 2024 scheme examiners:

| Sub-Type | Definition | Practical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Gross Command Area (GCA)** | Total area bounded by the outermost ridge line of the project, including non-cultivable land. | Theoretical outer boundary. |
| **Net Command Area (NCA)** | Area within GCA excluding barren, forested, and built-up zones. | Actual cultivable service zone. |
| **Culturable Command Area (CCA)** | Area within NCA that is actually put under cultivation during a base period. | The **only** area used for design duty, delta, and water budgeting. |

**Formula Relationship:**

$$CCA = GCA - (\text{Bad lands, Forests, Habitations, Roads, Tanks})$$

**Empirical Reduction Factor (GCA → CCA):**
$$CCA \approx 0.75 \times GCA \quad \text{(for plains)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Watering Can Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine holding a watering can at the top of a small hill. The total area that gets *wetted* is your **Command Area**. Now, exclude the rocky patch in the middle (non-cultivable) — that gives you **NCA**. Finally, only count the portion where you actually grow plants this season — that is your **CCA**. The "can" is the **Main Canal**, the spout is the **distributary**, and the drops are **watercourses** reaching individual farms.

### 1.3 Command Area Development Programme (CADP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CADP** is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme launched by the Government of India in **1974–75** with the principal objective of bridging the persistent gap between *irrigation potential created (IPC)* and *irrigation potential utilized (IPU)* in major and medium irrigation projects.

**Core Aim:** To ensure that the water delivered at the outlet is actually *conveyed* to the root zone of crops, with minimum conveyance and application losses.

### 1.4 Canals — The Distributive Arteries

A **canal** is an artificial open channel constructed on the ground surface (or with lining) to convey water from a source (reservoir/river) to the agricultural fields, following the **law of gravitation** as the primary driving force.

**Standard KTU Hierarchy of Canals:**

$$Q_{\text{Main Canal}} \rightarrow Q_{\text{Branch Canal}} \rightarrow Q_{\text{Distributary}} \rightarrow Q_{\text{Minor}} \rightarrow Q_{\text{Watercourse}} \rightarrow \text{Field}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Sub-Classification of Command Area

The KTU syllabus demands a layered understanding:

**Step 1 — Outer Boundary Identification (GCA):**
The ridge line determines the gravity-flow limit. Everything below the ridge is *theoretically* irrigable.

**Step 2 — Exclusion of Non-Usable Land (NCA):**
Forests, alkaline/saline patches, town areas, roads, and water bodies are deducted from GCA.

**Step 3 — Active Cultivation Zone (CCA):**
Only the area actually sown during the *design base period* is counted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the distinction matters:** Water duty ($\delta$), delta ($\Delta$), and cropping intensity are always computed for **CCA**, not GCA. Examiners will deduct marks if GCA is used directly in the Kenndy/NYC formula.

### 2.2 Components of CADP (K.T.U. Examiner's Focus Topic)

The CADP integrates engineering, agricultural, and institutional reforms. The 10 critical components as per KTU 2024 module 2 are:

1. **Construction of Field Channels and Field Drains** — Micro-distribution network (third-tier watercourses).
2. **Land Levelling and Shaping** — Uniform application of irrigation water, reducing deep percolation.
3. **Introduction of Warabandi / Shejpali** — Equitable rotational water distribution in proportion to land holding.
4. **Reclamation of Waterlogged and Saline/Alkali Soils** — Conjunctive use and sub-surface drainage.
5. **Adaptive Trials and Demonstrations** — On-farm water management (OFWM) trials for new crops/water-saving methods.
6. **Modernization of the Existing Irrigation System** — Lining, control structures, telemetry.
7. **Strengthening of Extension Services** — Farmer training, Water Users' Associations (WUAs).
8. **Farm Mechanization** — Custom Hiring Centres for tractors, laser levellers, sprinklers.
9. **Soil and Water Conservation** — Watershed treatments, afforestation in catchments.
10. **Crop Planning and Diversification** — Shift from water-guzzler crops (paddy) to millets/legumes based on agro-climatic zones.

### 2.3 Classification of Canals — The Master Framework

KTU Module 2 groups canals into **6 principal classes**:

**Class A — Based on Nature of Supply**
- *Perennial Canals* — Year-round flow (regulated by reservoir).
- *Non-Perennial / Seasonal Canals* — Run only in monsoon (run-of-river diversion).

**Class B — Based on Function**
- *Irrigation Canal* — For crop water supply.
- *Carrier Canal* — Carries irrigation water *plus* feeds another canal.
- *Feeder Canal* — Feeds another canal (no direct irrigation).
- *Navigation Canal* — Inland waterway transport.
- *Power Canal* — Feeds hydropower turbines.

**Class C — Based on Discharge Capacity**
| Canal Type | Discharge Range (cumecs) |
|---|---|
| Main Canal | $> 25$ |
| Branch Canal | $10 - 25$ |
| Major Distributary | $2.5 - 10$ |
| Minor Distributary | $0.25 - 2.5$ |
| Watercourse | $< 0.25$ |

**Class D — Based on Alignment (KTU Most-Aasked)**
- *Ridge Canal / Watershed Canal* — Runs along the ridge.
- *Contour Canal* — Runs along a constant elevation contour.
- *Side-Slope Canal* — Runs along the slope between ridge and valley.

**Class E — Based on Soil**
- *Alluvial Canal* — Soft, sandy, silt-bearing strata.
- *Non-Alluvial Canal* — Hard rock or clay.

**Class F — Based on Financial / Protection Criterion**
- *Protective Canal* — Subsidized, anti-famine (returns < 2%).
- *Productive Canal* — Self-financing (returns > 2%).

### 2.4 Alignment of Canals — The Topographical Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alignment** is the art of fixing the *path* of a canal on the ground such that the maximum command area is served with minimum earthwork, minimal lining, and zero waterlogging.

The three classical alignments are:

**(i) Ridge / Watershed Canal Alignment**
- Runs along the *highest points* (ridgelines) of the topography.
- Water flows *away* from the canal on **both sides** by gravity.
- **No waterlogging**, but heavy initial cut.

**(ii) Contour Canal Alignment**
- Runs along a *constant elevation*.
- Irrigates only the area on the *lower* side of the canal.
- Cross-drainage works are *minimal*.
- Risk of waterlogging on the upper side.

**(iii) Side-Slope Canal Alignment**
- A compromise between ridge and contour.
- Runs obliquely down the slope, irrigating areas on both sides in stages.
- Requires **balanced depth of cutting and filling** (balanced cross-section).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Value | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $GCA$ | Total area within ridge | hectares (ha) | Master planning |
| $CCA$ | $GCA \times 0.6$ to $0.75$ | ha | Design base |
| $ICA$ (Irrigable CA) | $CCA \times 0.9$ | ha | Crop planning |
| $Q$ (Discharge) | $A \times V$ | cumecs (m³/s) | Canal sizing |
| $V$ (Velocity) | $\frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ (Manning) | m/s | Flow design |
| Cross-section | $A = (b + ny)y$ | m² | Trapezoidal section |
| Balanced Section | $R_{b} = 0.83$ | ratio | Side slope matching |
| Lacey Regime | $P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q}$ | m | Wetted perimeter |
| Freeboard | $\frac{1}{3} \times$ depth or $0.5$ m | m | Embankment safety |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From GCA to CCA — The Reduction Logic

This derivation is fundamental for all KTU numerical questions on *area classification*.

**Step 1 — Define the Outer Perimeter.**
The ridge line of the catchment encloses the **Gross Command Area**.

$$GCA = \text{Area enclosed by ridge contour of the canal source}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract Non-Cultivable Patches.**
Forests, alkaline flats, towns, and tanks are deducted.

$$NCA = GCA - (A_{\text{forest}} + A_{\text{builtup}} + A_{\text{waste}})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Cultural Operations Filter.**
Out of NCA, only that land is *cultured* where sowing is feasible in the base period.

$$CCA = NCA \times \text{Culturable Efficiency}$$
where, $\text{Culturable Efficiency} \approx 0.85 \text{ to } 0.95$

**Step 4 — Final Combined Expression:**
$$CCA = [GCA - A_{\text{uncultivable}}] \times 0.90$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (Worked-Out for KTU Practice):**
A proposed reservoir commands a GCA of **12,000 ha**. Forest occupies 1,500 ha; town + roads occupy 800 ha; alkaline waste 700 ha. The culturable efficiency is 0.9.

\begin{aligned}
NCA &= GCA - (A_{\text{forest}} + A_{\text{builtup}} + A_{\text{waste}}) \\
    &= 12000 - (1500 + 800 + 700) \\
    &= 12000 - 3000 = 9000 \text{ ha}
\end{aligned}

$$CCA = NCA \times 0.9 = 9000 \times 0.9 = 8100 \text{ ha}$$

This is the value used in all subsequent water requirement calculations.

### 3.2 Derivation: Balanced Section for Side-Slope Canal

For a canal in cutting (excavation), the most economical design is the **balanced section** where the volume of excavation equals the volume of embankment.

**Step 1 — Assume Trapezoidal Section.**
Area of excavation:
$$A_{\text{exc}} = b \cdot d + s \cdot d^{2}$$
where $b$ = bed width, $d$ = depth, $s$ = side slope ($H:V = 1:s$).

**Step 2 — Area of Embankment (using berm and side slope n).**
$$A_{\text{emb}} = (b + 2s \cdot d + k \cdot d)(k \cdot d) + k^{2} \cdot d^{2}$$
where $k$ is the embankment side slope.

**Step 3 — Equate and Solve for b.**
The simplified KTU result (balancing cut = fill) gives:
$$R_{b} = \frac{b}{d} = 0.83 \quad (\text{for } s = k = 1:1 \text{ in loam})$$

**Step 4 — Discharge Capacity:**
$$Q = A \cdot V = (b + d)d \cdot \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

This is the foundation for Kennedy's and Lacey's regime theory.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Canal Alignment

| Criterion | Ridge Canal | Contour Canal | Side-Slope Canal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Topography required | Steep, undulating | Moderate, sloping | Gentle, rolling |
| Cross-drainage works | Frequent | Few | Moderate |
| Waterlogging risk | None | High on upper side | Low |
| Initial earthwork | Very high | Low | Balanced |
| Command area served | Both sides | One side only | Both sides |
| Maintenance cost | Moderate | High (silt) | Low |
| Best suited for | Steep catchments | Gentle valley flanks | Plains with mild gradient |
| KTU exam weightage | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In a 14-mark question, you are *expected* to justify the alignment choice using a cost-vs-command trade-off table. The matrix above is your ready-made examiner-pleaser.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**

**Q1. Define Culturable Command Area (CCA). How is it different from Gross Command Area (GCA)?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Culturable Command Area (CCA):** It is that portion of the Net Command Area (NCA) which is *actually cultivated* during the design base period of an irrigation project. It excludes fallow lands and areas not sown in the base year.

**Difference Table:**

| Parameter | GCA | CCA |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Total area within ridge | Area actually cultivated |
| Includes forests/towns | Yes | No |
| Used in design | No | Yes (for duty/delta) |
| Typical ratio | 1.0 | $0.6 - 0.75$ of GCA |

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q2. List any four components of the Command Area Development Programme (CADP).**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (any 4):**
1. Construction of field channels and field drains.
2. Land levelling and shaping for uniform water application.
3. Introduction of rotational water supply (Warabandi / Shejpali).
4. Reclamation of waterlogged and saline/alkali soils.
5. Adaptive trials and demonstrations.
6. Modernization of the existing canal system.
7. Strengthening of extension services and Water Users' Associations.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **classifications of canals** based on (i) nature of supply, (ii) function, (iii) discharge, and (iv) alignment. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **components of the Command Area Development Programme (CADP)** and explain how it bridges the gap between irrigation potential created and utilized. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

The classification of canals is essential to design and operate an irrigation network efficiently.

**(i) Based on Nature of Supply:**
- *Perennial Canals:* These canals have a year-round supply of water. They are fed by a storage reservoir (dam/barrage) and can irrigate both Rabi and Kharif crops.
- *Non-Perennial Canals:* These carry water only during the monsoon season. They are typically run-of-river diversion schemes (e.g., older Upper Ganga Canal). They can irrigate only one crop (Kharif).

**(ii) Based on Function:**
- *Irrigation Canal:* Carries water directly to the agricultural fields.
- *Carrier Canal:* Carries water through a region while simultaneously irrigating a part of it. It is a hybrid — transport + irrigation.
- *Feeder Canal:* Carries water from the source to *another* canal; it does not irrigate directly.
- *Navigation Canal:* Used for inland water transport (e.g., Buckingham Canal).
- *Power Canal:* Carries water to hydropower turbines for electricity generation.

**(iii) Based on Discharge Capacity:**

| Canal | Discharge (cumecs) |
|---|---|
| Main Canal | $> 25$ |
| Branch Canal | $10 - 25$ |
| Major Distributary | $2.5 - 10$ |
| Minor Distributary | $0.25 - 2.5$ |
| Watercourse | $< 0.25$ |

**(iv) Based on Alignment:**
- *Ridge Canal:* Runs along the watershed ridge; irrigates both sides; no waterlogging.
- *Contour Canal:* Runs along a constant contour; irrigates one side only; prone to waterlogging.
- *Side-Slope Canal:* Runs obliquely across the slope; balanced cut-and-fill.

**Model Solution (b):**

The **Command Area Development Programme (CADP)** was launched in **December 1974** by the Ministry of Irrigation, Government of India, as a Centrally Sponsored Scheme. Its genesis lies in the chronic observation that, despite massive investments in major and medium irrigation projects, only a fraction of the *created* irrigation potential was actually *utilized* on the fields — the so-called "gap" between IPC and IPU.

**The Gap Equation:**
$$\text{Gap} = IPC - IPU \quad \text{(typically } 30-50\% \text{ in India)}$$

**Key Components of CADP:**

1. **Field Channel & Field Drain Network:** Extension of the canal system down to the individual farm gate.
2. **Land Levelling & Shaping:** Reduces over-irrigation and deep percolation losses.
3. **Warabandi / Shejpali System:** Time-based rotation ensures equitable supply.
4. **Reclamation of Waterlogged/Saline Lands:** Sub-surface drainage and gypsum application.
5. **Modernization:** Lining, automatic gates, SCADA.
6. **Crop Planning:** Diversification away from paddy to pulses/millets.
7. **Farm Mechanization:** Custom Hiring Centres for laser levellers and sprinklers.
8. **Extension & WUA Training:** Capacity building of farmers.

**How CADP Bridges the Gap:**

CADP addresses the *on-farm* inefficiencies. The original IPC counts water reaching the *outlet* of a distributary. CADP ensures that this water *also* reaches the field and is used *efficiently*. By integrating engineering (drainage), agronomy (crop choice), and institutions (WUAs), CADP transforms "created potential" into "utilized potential."

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *CCA* with *ICA* (Irrigable Command Area). ICA = $CCA \times 0.9$ and is used for the *intensity of irrigation*, not the *area design*. Marks are docked if you interchange these terms.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is meant by **alignment of canals**? Compare in detail the **Ridge, Contour, and Side-Slope alignments** with the help of neat sketches and a comparative table. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A proposed irrigation project has a GCA of 20,000 ha. The forest cover occupies 1,800 ha, town and roads occupy 1,200 ha, and alkaline waste occupies 800 ha. The culturable efficiency is 0.9. Compute the **NCA, CCA, and ICA**, and comment on the intensity of irrigation if the project is designed for 100% rabi and 50% kharif cropping. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition:** Alignment of a canal refers to the fixing of its *route/path* on the ground such that it commands the maximum irrigable area with minimum cost, minimum cross-drainage works, and minimal waterlogging.

**Three Classical Alignments:**

**1. Ridge / Watershed Canal:**
- Runs along the *ridge line* of the catchment.
- The canal is *in cutting* for its entire length.
- Water flows *away* from the canal on both sides.
- **Advantage:** Zero waterlogging.
- **Disadvantage:** Heavy initial excavation; many cross-drainage works (aqueducts/syphons to pass valleys).

**2. Contour Canal:**
- Runs along a *contour* of constant elevation.
- Irrigates only the area on the *lower* side.
- **Advantage:** Very little earthwork.
- **Disadvantage:** Waterlogging on the upper side; limited one-sided command.

**3. Side-Slope Canal:**
- A compromise: runs obliquely down the slope.
- Irrigates both sides in *stages* (by minor distributaries).
- **Advantage:** Balanced cut-fill section; lower cost.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires careful design to avoid excessive slope.

**Comparative Table:**

| Feature | Ridge | Contour | Side-Slope |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position | Watershed | Constant elevation | In between |
| Command | Both sides | One side | Both sides (staged) |
| Cross-drainage | Many | Few | Moderate |
| Earthwork | Maximum | Minimum | Balanced |
| Waterlogging | None | High | Low |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate |
| Best use | Hilly terrain | Gentle valley | Plains |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $GCA = 20{,}000$ ha
- $A_{\text{forest}} = 1800$ ha
- $A_{\text{builtup}} = 1200$ ha
- $A_{\text{waste}} = 800$ ha
- Culturable efficiency = 0.9

**Step 1 — Compute NCA:**
\begin{aligned}
NCA &= GCA - (A_{\text{forest}} + A_{\text{builtup}} + A_{\text{waste}}) \\
    &= 20000 - (1800 + 1200 + 800) \\
    &= 20000 - 3800 \\
    &= 16200 \text{ ha}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Compute CCA:**
\begin{aligned}
CCA &= NCA \times \text{Culturable Efficiency} \\
    &= 16200 \times 0.9 \\
    &= 14580 \text{ ha}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Compute ICA:**
\begin{aligned}
ICA &= CCA \times 0.9 \\
    &= 14580 \times 0.9 \\
    &= 13122 \text{ ha}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Cropping Intensity:**
The project is designed for 100% Rabi and 50% Kharif.
- Rabi area = $1.0 \times ICA = 13122$ ha
- Kharif area = $0.5 \times ICA = 6561$ ha

**Total cropped area:**
$$A_{\text{total}} = 13122 + 6561 = 19683 \text{ ha}$$

**Intensity of Irrigation:**
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{crop}} &= \frac{A_{\text{total}}}{CCA} \times 100 \\
                &= \frac{19683}{14580} \times 100 \\
                &\approx 135\%
\end{aligned}

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A *cropping intensity of 135%* means more than one crop per year is being grown on the same plot. The canal must therefore be *perennial*, and the storage reservoir must be sized for both Rabi *and* partial Kharif demand. Students often forget to compute the cropping intensity and lose 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 3 Traps

> [!WARNING]
> **Trap 1 — Confusing GCA, NCA, CCA, ICA:** All four are *not* interchangeable. The design of duty, delta, and cropping intensity is based on **CCA**, not GCA.
>
> **Trap 2 — Spelling "Perinneal" or "Perenial":** Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for incorrect spellings of key terms in the answer sheet.
>
> **Trap 3 — Skipping the comparative table:** In alignment questions, a *table* is mandatory. A long paragraph without a table fetches only 4/7 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Command Area = Gravity-fed irrigable area** of a project; subdivided into GCA, NCA, CCA, and ICA.
- **CCA is the design area**; the ratio CCA:GCA is typically **0.6 – 0.75**.
- **CADP (1974–75)** is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme aimed at bridging the **IPC–IPU gap**.
- **10 Key Components of CADP:** Field channels, drainage, levelling, warabandi, reclamation, modernization, mechanization, extension, crop planning, soil conservation.
- **Canals classified by 6 criteria:** Supply (perennial/non-perennial), Function (irrigation/carrier/feeder/navigation/power), Discharge (main/branch/distributary/minor/watercourse), Alignment (ridge/contour/side-slope), Soil (alluvial/non-alluvial), Finance (protective/productive).
- **Ridge alignment** = no waterlogging, both-side command, heavy cross-drainage.
- **Contour alignment** = one-side command, prone to waterlogging, least earthwork.
- **Side-slope alignment** = balanced cut-fill, both sides (staged), best for plains.
- **Balanced cross-section** is achieved when $\frac{b}{d} = 0.83$ (for unit side slopes in loam).
- **Lacey's regime** gives wetted perimeter $P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q}$ — must remember for Module 3 linkage.
- **Discharge hierarchy** is the standard KTU 5-tier classification by **cumecs**.
- Always cite **CADP year (1974–75)** and the **ministry (Ministry of Irrigation, GOI)** in CADP questions to earn full marks.
- The **Warabandi** system is rotational distribution based on land-holding size — not equal time per farmer, but **proportional time per hectare**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Irrigation Components - Drip, Micro Sprinkler, Mist, Fogger, etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micro Irrigation Components - Drip, Micro Sprinkler, Mist, Fogger, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Micro Irrigation?

**Micro Irrigation (MI)** is a modern, low-pressure, low-flow-rate method of artificial water application in which water is delivered directly to the **root zone** of crops through a network of pipelines, valves, and emitters. The application is localized, frequent, and slow enough to maintain an ideal moisture regime in the soil surrounding each plant.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus definition:
> **"Micro Irrigation is the slow, controlled application of water through discrete emitters (drippers, micro-sprinklers, micro-jets, misters, or foggers) operating at low pressure, designed to wet only a specified portion of the soil volume in which plant roots develop."**

The four main categories of micro irrigation emitters are:

| S.No | Emitter Type | Operating Pressure | Typical Discharge | Droplet / Spray Size |
|------|--------------|--------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Drip (Trickle)** | **0.5 – 1.5 kg/cm²** | **1 – 8 L/hr** | Drips / streams |
| 2 | **Micro Sprinkler** | **1.0 – 3.0 kg/cm²** | **20 – 200 L/hr** | **0.5 – 2.0 mm** droplets |
| 3 | **Mist System** | **2.0 – 4.0 kg/cm²** | **30 – 60 L/hr** | **< 0.5 mm** droplets |
| 4 | **Fogger (Fog Jet)** | **3.0 – 5.0 kg/cm²** | **5 – 15 L/hr** | **< 0.05 mm** aerosols |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The course expects students to understand the **functional classification** of emitters based on the **wetted pattern geometry** (point source vs. line source vs. area source) and the **mechanism of energy dissipation** (long-path, short-path, orifice, pressure-compensating, vortex).

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Medicine Dropper vs. The Garden Hose"

Imagine you are watering two very different things:

1. **A bouquet of roses in narrow pots (Drip Irrigation):** You do not need to flood the whole table — you simply place a few **medicine droppers (drippers)** at the base of each stem and let slow drops seep in. Almost no water is wasted, and each plant gets exactly what its root ball needs.

2. **A delicate mushroom bed or greenhouse nursery (Mist / Fogger):** You don't pour water; you create a **fine cloud** (like a perfume diffuser) so the humidity around the leaves increases. The droplets are so small they float in the air.

3. **A row of fruit saplings (Micro Sprinkler):** You don't flood the soil — you use a **gentle lawn sprinkler that throws water only 2–4 m** in a small circle around each plant, wetting a defined bulb of soil.

**Key Insight:** All these methods share a common philosophy — *"Apply water precisely where, when, and in the quantity the plant needs."* This is the foundation of **precision irrigation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Efficiency (Ea) Reality:** Surface flood irrigation typically achieves **35–45 %** application efficiency, whereas properly designed micro irrigation can achieve **85–95 %**. This 50 %+ water saving is why Kerala's "Jala Samrudhi" mission and the **PMKSY (Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana)** strongly promote drip and micro-sprinkler adoption.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used Throughout This Module

- **1 ha = 10,000 m² = 2.47 acres**
- **1 m³ = 1,000 litres = 264.17 US gallons**
- **1 kg/cm² ≈ 1 bar ≈ 14.22 psi ≈ 10 m of water head**
- **Field Capacity (FC)** = water content held after gravity drainage (~ -0.33 bar suction)
- **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP)** = water held at -15 bar suction
- **Available Water Capacity (AWC) = FC − PWP** (typically **0.10 – 0.20 cm³/cm³** for most Kerala laterite / alluvial soils)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Components of Any Micro Irrigation System

A micro irrigation system, regardless of emitter type, consists of **seven functional sub-units**:

1. **Water Source & Pump Station** — well, open well, pond, or canal.
2. **Head Control Unit** — includes a **non-return valve**, **air vacuum relief valve**, **screen/disc filter**, **sand separator**, **fertilizer / chemigation tank** (Venturi or injection pump), and **pressure gauge**.
3. **Mainline (M)** — conveys water from the pump to sub-mains; typically HDPE/PVC **Class 2.5 to 4** pipe.
4. **Sub-Mainline (SM)** — distributes water to manifolds; often **63 mm or 75 mm HDPE**.
5. **Manifold** — short, valve-controlled pipe feeding a group of laterals.
6. **Laterals (L)** — small-diameter (**12 mm, 16 mm, 20 mm** LDPE) pipes carrying water to emitters.
7. **Emitters** — the heart of the system. Classified by:
   - **Discharge type:** Point / Line / Area
   - **Pressure regulation:** Non-PC vs. PC (Pressure Compensating)
   - **Hydraulic type:** Long-path, Short-path, Orifice, Vortex, Diaphragm

## 2.2 The Four Emitter Families — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Drip Emitters (Drippers)

A **drip emitter** discharges water in the form of **drops, trickles, or mini-streams** at very low flow rates (**1–8 L/h**) at low pressures (**0.5–1.5 kg/cm²**).

**Hydraulic classifications:**

| Type | Energy Dissipation Mechanism | Typical Application |
|------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Long-path (tortuous/labyrinth)** | Friction along a long, zig-zag path | Vegetables, row crops |
| **Short-path** | Short turbulent flow path | Orchards, wide-spaced crops |
| **Orifice** | Single small hole (often self-cleaning) | Home gardens |
| **Vortex (Centrifugal)** | Whirling flow creates a pressure drop | Orchards |
| **Pressure Compensating (PC)** | Flexible diaphragm equalizes flow | Sloppy / undulating terrain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PC Emitters?** On undulating land (a common feature in Kerala's Western Ghats), pressure varies along laterals. Without PC, downstream emitters under-pressurize and upstream over-discharge. PC emitters maintain a **near-constant discharge (± 5%)** over a pressure range of **0.5 to 4.0 kg/cm²**.

**Emission Uniformity (EU):** The KTU expects you to remember the classic equation:

$$
EU \;=\; \frac{q_{\min}}{q_{\text{avg}}} \times 100
$$

where $q_{\min}$ is the average of the lowest 25 % of emitter discharges and $q_{\text{avg}}$ is the mean of all measured discharges. A well-designed system aims for **EU ≥ 90 %**.

### 2.2.2 Micro Sprinklers

A **micro sprinkler (micro-jet)** is a small, rotating or stationary spray device mounted on a stake, typically wetted diameter **2.5 to 9 m**, discharge **20–200 L/h**, and operating pressure **1.0–3.0 kg/cm²**.

- **Spinning (rotary) type** — uses a swinging arm to break the jet; produces a circular wetted pattern.
- **Fixed spray / spray head** — sprays from a fixed nozzle; produces a small, non-rotating cone.
- Used for orchards, nurseries, ground crops like **groundnut, potato**, and for **overhead cooling** in poly-houses.

**Wetted Diameter (D_w) for Micro Sprinklers** — empirical power model:

$$
D_w \;=\; a \cdot Q^{b}
$$

where $Q$ = discharge (L/h), $a$ and $b$ are manufacturer-supplied coefficients (typical $b \approx 0.4$–$0.5$).

### 2.2.3 Mist Systems

A **mist system** produces droplets of **0.05 to 0.5 mm** in diameter, falling over a small wetted area (typically **1.0–2.0 m diameter**). Used in:

- Greenhouses for **humidity control and propagation chambers**.
- Cutting / grafting nurseries (rooting of cuttings under mist prevents leaf desiccation).
- Cooling in poultry farms and poly-houses.

Droplet size is so fine that they tend to **drift** with even mild air currents.

### 2.2.4 Foggers (Fog Jets)

A **fogger** is essentially a high-pressure mist system that creates a true **aerosol (droplet < 0.05 mm)**. Operating at **3.5–5.0 kg/cm²**, foggers use either:

- **Single-nozzle vortex foggers** (with deflector plate), or
- **Pneumatic / twin-fluid foggers** (using compressed air + water for ultra-fine droplets).

**Uses:** Propagation chambers, mushroom houses, cooling pads, greenhouse climate control. The fog evaporates before reaching the plant — it cools the **air**, not the leaves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala-Specific Use Case:** Foggers are now mandated in many commercial **polyhouse cut-flower farms** (orchids, anthuriums) in Wayanad, Idukki, and Thrissur districts, where they maintain **85–95 % relative humidity**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** you are likely to be tested on for this topic in the KTU ESE. Memorize the symbol definitions — they appear in the question paper verbatim.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $q = k \cdot H^{x}$ | Emitter discharge (Hagen-Poiseuille style) where $H$ = pressure head, $k$ = discharge coefficient, $x$ = emitter exponent (0.5 = turbulent, 1.0 = laminar) | $q$ in L/h, $H$ in m |
| 2 | $D_w = a \cdot Q^{b}$ | Wetted diameter of a micro-sprinkler as a function of its discharge $Q$ | $D_w$ in m, $Q$ in L/h |
| 3 | $AR = \dfrac{q \cdot n}{s_l \cdot s_e}$ | Application rate (depth of water applied per hour) per unit wetted area | mm/h, L/h/m² |
| 4 | $EU = \dfrac{q_{\min}}{q_{\text{avg}}} \times 100$ | Emission Uniformity | % |
| 5 | $CU = \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert d_i \vert}{n \cdot q_{\text{avg}}}\right) \times 100$ | Christiansen's Coefficient of Uniformity (also written with $\sigma$) | % |
| 6 | $h_f = \dfrac{K \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | **Hazen-Williams** head loss in lateral/sub-main | m |
| 7 | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | **Darcy-Weisbach** head loss for turbulent flow | m |
| 8 | $V = \dfrac{0.408 \cdot Q}{D^{2}}$ | Velocity in a pipe (Q in L/s, D in m) | m/s |
| 9 | $T = \dfrac{d \cdot s_l \cdot s_e}{q \cdot n}$ | Time required to apply depth $d$ of water | hours |
| 10 | $SL = \dfrac{db - PWP}{100} \cdot \rho_s \cdot D_r$ | Total Available Soil Moisture (ASM) per metre depth | mm/m |
| 11 | $P = \dfrac{n \cdot q \cdot s_l \cdot s_e}{A}$ | System capacity (total pump discharge) | L/h, m³/h |
| 12 | $E_a = \dfrac{W_s}{W_f} \times 100$ | Application efficiency: water stored in root zone ÷ water delivered | % |

**Notation Key:**
- $q$ = emitter discharge, $n$ = no. of emitters per plant, $s_l$ = lateral spacing, $s_e$ = emitter spacing
- $D$ = pipe diameter, $L$ = pipe length, $C$ = Hazen-Williams coefficient (HDPE = **140**, PVC = **150**)
- $K$ = constant (10.67 in SI units for Q in m³/s)
- $d_i$ = deviation of i-th emitter discharge from mean

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Micro Irrigation |
|--------|------------------------|
| **Kerala coconut farming** | Drip under mulched basins → 30–50 % water savings |
| **Banana (Nendran, Palayankodan)** | Surface drip with 4–6 L/h drippers, 2 m spacing |
| **Tea / coffee estates (Wayanad)** | Micro sprinklers on rolling terrain |
| **Floriculture poly-houses** | Mist + fogger for propagation |
| **Mushroom (Volvariella in Kottayam)** | Fogger humidity control |
| **Urban landscaping** | Bubbler for trees, drip for shrubs |
| **Saline water use** | Drip minimizes leaf-salt contact |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Emitter Discharge Equation $q = k H^{x}$

**Starting Principle:** Flow through a small orifice-type emitter is governed by the **energy equation** between the lateral (inlet, point 1) and the emitter exit (point 2).

For a small emitter, the head loss $H_L$ across the emitter is the operating pressure head $H$:

$$
H_L = H_1 - H_2 \;\approx\; H \;(\text{in metres of water head})
$$

If the emitter flow path behaves **laminarly** (smooth, viscous), the Hagen-Poiseuille law applies:

$$
q \;\propto\; \frac{H}{L_{\text{eff}}} \cdot D_e^{4}
$$

where $L_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective path length and $D_e$ is the equivalent diameter. Therefore $q \propto H^{1.0}$, so the **emitter exponent $x = 1.0$** (laminar flow emitters).

If the flow is **turbulent** (rough path, vortex, orifice-type), the orifice law applies:

$$
q \;\propto\; \sqrt{2gH}
$$

Therefore $q \propto H^{0.5}$, so the **emitter exponent $x = 0.5$** (fully turbulent flow emitter).

**Generalized Form (Karmeli's discharge equation):**

$$
q \;=\; k \cdot H^{x}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $x$ matters in design:** If a system pressure varies by ± 20 %, a **turbulent emitter** ($x = 0.5$) will show discharge variation of only $\pm 9.5$ % (good). A **laminar emitter** ($x = 1.0$) will show ± 20 % variation (bad). Hence KTU examiners love asking: *"Why are turbulent-flow emitters preferred in field design?"*

## 3.2 Derivation of Application Rate (AR) for a Point-Source Dripper

**Given:** Emitter discharge $q$ (L/h), number of emitters per plant $n$, lateral spacing $s_l$ (m), emitter spacing $s_e$ (m).

**Step 1 — Volume applied per plant per hour:**

$$
V_{\text{plant}} \;=\; n \cdot q \quad \text{[L/h]}
$$

**Step 2 — Wetted soil surface area (per plant) — assuming a rectangular grid:**

$$
A_{\text{plant}} \;=\; s_l \cdot s_e \quad \text{[m}^{2}\text{]}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to depth of water applied:**

$$
AR \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{plant}}}{A_{\text{plant}}} \;=\; \frac{n \cdot q}{s_l \cdot s_e}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mm/h** (since $1 \; \text{L/m}^{2} = 1 \; \text{mm}$ of depth):

$$
AR \;[\text{mm/h}] \;=\; \frac{n \cdot q \;[\text{L/h}]}{s_l \cdot s_e \;[\text{m}^{2}]}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

A banana field is laid out in a $2 \, \text{m} \times 2 \, \text{m}$ grid. Each banana plant has **2 drippers**, each discharging **4 L/h**. Compute the application rate.

$$
AR \;=\; \frac{2 \times 4}{2 \times 2} \;=\; \frac{8}{4} \;=\; 2 \; \text{mm/h}
$$

If the system runs for 3 hours:

$$
d_{\text{applied}} \;=\; 2 \times 3 \;=\; 6 \; \text{mm of water depth}
$$

**Verification with the depth formula:**

$$
d \;=\; \frac{AR \times T}{1} \quad \text{with } T = 3 \text{ h} \;\Rightarrow\; d = 6 \; \text{mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Wetted Diameter for a Micro Sprinkler

Manufacturer data (e.g., from **Netafim, Jain, Naan Dan Jain**) shows that wetted diameter is best fit by:

$$
D_w \;=\; a \cdot Q^{b}
$$

where for a typical micro-sprinkler, $a = 0.85$ and $b = 0.45$ (for Q in L/h, $D_w$ in m).

**Worked Example:**
A micro-sprinkler discharges **120 L/h** with $a = 0.85$, $b = 0.45$.

$$
D_w \;=\; 0.85 \times (120)^{0.45}
$$

$$
\ln D_w \;=\; \ln 0.85 + 0.45 \cdot \ln 120
$$

$$
\ln D_w \;=\; (-0.1625) + 0.45 \times (4.7875)
$$

$$
\ln D_w \;=\; -0.1625 + 2.1544 \;=\; 1.9919
$$

$$
D_w \;=\; e^{1.9919} \;\approx\; 7.33 \; \text{m}
$$

**Cross-Check by raising the power directly:**

$$
120^{0.45} \;\approx\; 8.62 \quad \text{(via calculator)}
$$

$$
D_w \;\approx\; 0.85 \times 8.62 \;\approx\; 7.33 \; \text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Christiansen Uniformity Coefficient

Consider $n$ emitters on a lateral. The mean discharge is:

$$
\bar{q} \;=\; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i
$$

The **mean absolute deviation** of discharges is:

$$
MAD \;=\; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert
$$

Christiansen defined uniformity as:

$$
CU \;=\; \left( 1 - \frac{MAD}{\bar{q}} \right) \times 100 \;=\; \left( 1 - \frac{\sum \vert d_i \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}} \right) \times 100
$$

**Worked Example (5 emitters):**

| Emitter | $q_i$ (L/h) | $\vert d_i \vert = \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert$ |
|---------|-------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | 4.0 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 4.3 | 0.3 |
| 3 | 3.7 | 0.3 |
| 4 | 4.1 | 0.1 |
| 5 | 3.9 | 0.1 |

$$
\bar{q} \;=\; \frac{4.0 + 4.3 + 3.7 + 4.1 + 3.9}{5} \;=\; 4.0 \; \text{L/h}
$$

$$
\sum \vert d_i \vert \;=\; 0.0 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.1 + 0.1 \;=\; 0.8
$$

$$
CU \;=\; \left( 1 - \frac{0.8}{5 \times 4.0} \right) \times 100 \;=\; \left( 1 - 0.04 \right) \times 100 \;=\; 96 \; \% \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Field Acceptance Criterion (KTU / BIS 14482):**
> - $CU \geq 95$ % — Excellent design
> - $CU \; 85$–$95$ % — Acceptable
> - $CU < 85$ % — Redesign required

## 3.5 Derivation of Total System Capacity

**Given:** Field area $A$ (ha), gross depth of water to be applied $d_g$ (mm), and time available $T$ (hours/day for the irrigation set).

$$
\text{Volume required per day} \;=\; \frac{A \times 10{,}000 \times d_g}{1000} \quad \text{m}^{3}
$$

$$
\text{System Discharge} \;=\; Q_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Volume}}{T} \quad \text{m}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

**Worked Example:**
- Field area = **1.0 ha**
- Net irrigation depth $d_{\text{net}} = 40$ mm
- Application efficiency $E_a = 90$ %
- Operating hours = 6 h/day

**Step 1 — Gross depth:**

$$
d_g \;=\; \frac{d_{\text{net}}}{E_a} \;=\; \frac{40}{0.90} \;\approx\; 44.4 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume to be pumped:**

$$
V \;=\; \frac{1.0 \times 10{,}000 \times 44.4}{1000} \;=\; 444 \; \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — System discharge:**

$$
Q_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \frac{444}{6} \;\approx\; 74 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{h} \;\approx\; 20.6 \; \text{L/s}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Drip Irrigation and Micro Sprinkler Irrigation in terms of (i) operating pressure, (ii) discharge rate, (iii) wetted area, and (iv) primary application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Drip Irrigation | Micro Sprinkler |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Operating pressure | **0.5 – 1.5 kg/cm²** | **1.0 – 3.0 kg/cm²** |
| Discharge | **1 – 8 L/h** | **20 – 200 L/h** |
| Wetted area | **Small bulb (0.3–0.6 m)** at root zone | **Circular area (2.5–9 m)** around plant |
| Primary use | **Point-source root-zone wetting** for row crops, orchards | **Area-source over-the-canopy** for ground cover, nurseries |

Drip applies water to the **root zone only**, while micro sprinkler wets both **above-ground canopy and soil surface**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a Pressure Compensating (PC) emitter? Why is it recommended for hilly terrain?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Pressure Compensating (PC) emitter** is one in which a flexible silicone diaphragm or rubber disc **automatically varies the effective flow area** in response to changes in inlet pressure, thereby maintaining a **nearly constant discharge** (within ± 5 %) over a wide pressure range of **0.5 to 4.0 kg/cm²**.

**Reasons recommended for hilly terrain:**
- In undulating land, elevation differences cause **hydraulic pressure variation** along the lateral.
- A non-PC emitter will over-discharge at low elevations and under-discharge at high elevations, leading to **uneven water application** and **low CU**.
- A PC emitter flattens this variation, ensuring **uniform discharge** regardless of slope position, resulting in **higher EU and CU** and **better crop uniformity**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **components of a micro irrigation system** and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field of **1.0 ha** is to be irrigated by drip system. The **crop is banana** planted at **2 m × 2 m**. Each plant is provided with **4 drippers** of discharge **8 L/h each**. Lateral spacing = **2 m**, emitter spacing along lateral = **1 m**. The system is to be operated for **5 hours per day**. Compute the **system capacity in m³/h and the gross depth of water applied per day**, assuming application efficiency = 85 %. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Block diagram — components of micro irrigation system** (see Section 4.1 above for full Mermaid block).

**Functions:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Pump station** | Lifts water from source and pressurizes the system |
| 2 | **Non-return valve** | Prevents backflow when pump stops |
| 3 | **Air-vacuum relief valve** | Releases trapped air on system start; admits air on shut-off to prevent vacuum collapse of pipes |
| 4 | **Sand separator** | Removes sand/heavy particles by centrifugal action |
| 5 | **Fertilizer injector** | Injects water-soluble nutrients into the mainline (Venturi / fertilizer tank) |
| 6 | **Screen / Disc filter** | Removes fine organic matter and suspended impurities to prevent emitter clogging |
| 7 | **Pressure gauge** | Indicates system pressure at head |
| 8 | **Mainline** | Carries water from head control to sub-mains (HDPE / PVC) |
| 9 | **Sub-main** | Distributes water to several manifolds |
| 10 | **Manifold** | Connects a group of laterals through a control valve |
| 11 | **Lateral (LDPE 12–20 mm)** | Carries water to emitters; runs along each crop row |
| 12 | **Emitter** | Dissipates pressure and discharges water as drops/mist/spray at root zone |
| 13 | **Flushing valves** | Periodic flushing of laterals |
| 14 | **Pressure regulators** | Maintain uniform sub-main pressure |

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Number of plants per hectare:**

Banana spacing = 2 m × 2 m

$$
N_{\text{plants}} \;=\; \frac{10{,}000}{2 \times 2} \;=\; 2{,}500 \; \text{plants/ha}
$$

**Step 2 — Total discharge per plant:**

$$
q_{\text{plant}} \;=\; 4 \times 8 \;=\; 32 \; \text{L/h per plant}
$$

**Step 3 — Total system discharge required:**

$$
Q_{\text{sys}} \;=\; 2{,}500 \times 32 \;=\; 80{,}000 \; \text{L/h} \;=\; 80 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify with the formula $P = (n \cdot q \cdot s_l \cdot s_e)/A$:**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{4 \times 8 \times 2 \times 1}{1.0 \times 10{,}000} \times 10{,}000 \;=\; 80{,}000 \; \text{L/h} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Gross volume delivered per day (5 hours of operation):**

$$
V_{\text{gross}} \;=\; 80 \times 5 \;=\; 400 \; \text{m}^{3/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent gross depth applied (per ha basis):**

$$
d_g \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{gross}} \times 1000}{A \times 10{,}000} \;=\; \frac{400 \times 1000}{10{,}000} \;=\; 40 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute net depth stored in root zone:**

$$
d_{\text{net}} \;=\; d_g \times E_a \;=\; 40 \times 0.85 \;=\; 34 \; \text{mm/day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students often confuse the *area per plant* with the *emitter spacing*. The **gross depth** $d_g$ is applied over the **1 ha area**, not per plant. Many students erroneously compute $d$ as $q_{\text{plant}}/A_{\text{plant}} = 32/4 = 8$ mm/h, then multiply by 5 to get 40 mm — this answer is **correct by coincidence** here, but on a 1 m × 1 m spacing it would be wrong. **Always verify using the $10{,}000 \,\text{m}^{2}/\text{ha}$ conversion.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **micro sprinkler** and derive an expression for its **wetted diameter** in terms of discharge. State the typical operating range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A micro sprinkler with discharge **150 L/h** wets a circle of diameter **8.5 m**. Compute:
   (i) The application rate in **mm/h** assuming the wetted area is fully wetted.
   (ii) The depth of water applied in **2.5 hours** of operation.
   (iii) The **percentage of the field area actually wetted** if the micro sprinklers are placed on a **9 m × 9 m** grid. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Working principle of micro sprinkler:**
A micro sprinkler consists of a **nozzle, a rotating arm (or fixed deflector), and a stake / riser**. Pressurized water (1.0–3.0 kg/cm²) entering the nozzle is converted into a **high-velocity jet**. The jet impinges on the rotating arm (or a fixed deflector), causing it to **swing** in a circular motion (or to spray in a fixed cone). This **breaks the jet** into fine droplets (0.5–2 mm diameter) that fall under gravity to form a **circular wetted pattern** of 2.5–9 m diameter.

**Derivation of wetted diameter:**

By dimensional analysis, the wetted diameter $D_w$ of a micro sprinkler depends on:
- Discharge $Q$ (L/h)
- Operating pressure $H$ (kg/cm² or m head)
- Nozzle diameter $d_n$ (mm)
- Droplet fall height $h$ (m)
- Wind speed $W$ (m/s)

Empirically, for a fixed nozzle and negligible wind, manufacturers fit the simple power law:

$$
D_w \;=\; a \cdot Q^{b}
$$

where $a$ and $b$ are constants. Typical range:
- **$a = 0.7$ to $1.0$**, **$b = 0.40$ to $0.50$** (most common $b \approx 0.45$)

**Operating range:** Pressure **1.0 – 3.0 kg/cm²**, Discharge **20 – 200 L/h**, Wetted diameter **2.5 – 9.0 m**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Given:** $Q = 150 \; \text{L/h}$, $D_w = 8.5 \; \text{m}$, grid = $9 \; \text{m} \times 9 \; \text{m}$, $T = 2.5 \; \text{h}$.

**(i) Application rate:**

Wetted area:

$$
A_w \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times (8.5)^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times 72.25 \;\approx\; 56.74 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Application rate:

$$
AR \;=\; \frac{Q}{A_w} \;=\; \frac{150}{56.74} \;\approx\; 2.64 \; \text{L/h/m}^{2} \;\equiv\; 2.64 \; \text{mm/h}
$$

**(ii) Depth applied in 2.5 h:**

$$
d \;=\; AR \times T \;=\; 2.64 \times 2.5 \;\approx\; 6.6 \; \text{mm}
$$

**(iii) Percentage of field area wetted:**

Grid area per sprinkler:

$$
A_{\text{grid}} \;=\; 9 \times 9 \;=\; 81 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Wetted fraction:

$$
f_w \;=\; \frac{A_w}{A_{\text{grid}}} \;=\; \frac{56.74}{81} \;\approx\; 0.700 \;\equiv\; 70.0 \; \%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> When $D_w$ **exceeds the grid spacing**, the wetted circles **overlap**, and the wetted fraction can exceed 100 % if computed naively. The correct interpretation: report $f_w$ as the **fraction of overlap** (i.e., $D_w >$ spacing ⇒ overlap). For exam purposes, cap the answer at **100 %** when overlap occurs. The current problem ($8.5 \; \text{m} < 9 \; \text{m}$ grid) has **no overlap** — the wetted area is strictly 70 %.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3 Micro Irrigation Components**

- **Micro irrigation (MI)** = low pressure, low discharge, localized water application. The four families: **Drip, Micro Sprinkler, Mist, Fogger**.
- **Emitter discharge equation:** $q = k H^{x}$. **$x = 0.5$ (turbulent) is field-preferred** for low pressure sensitivity; **$x = 1.0$ (laminar) is highly pressure-sensitive**.
- **Operating pressures** (memorize the order): Drip < Micro Sprinkler < Mist < **Fogger (highest)**.
- **Wetted area** increases in the order: Drip < Mist < Micro Sprinkler < Fogger (drift).
- **PC (pressure compensating) emitters** use a **flexible diaphragm** to maintain constant discharge over **0.5–4.0 kg/cm²** — essential for hilly/sloppy fields.
- **EU = $q_{\min}/q_{\text{avg}} \times 100$**; **CU = Christiansen's formula** using MAD. Field acceptance: $CU \geq 85$ %, $EU \geq 90$ %.
- **Head control unit must contain** (in order): NRV → Air release valve → Sand separator → Fertilizer injector → Screen/Disc filter → Pressure gauge → Flow meter → Master valve.
- **Dripper spacing (s_e)** is set by **soil texture**: sandy → 0.3–0.5 m, loam → 0.5–0.75 m, clay → 0.75–1.0 m.
- **Emitter clogging** is the #1 operational problem; **filtration + flushing valves + chemigation** are the standard mitigations.
- **Application efficiency** of MI: **85–95 %** vs. surface flood: **35–45 %**.
- **Bubbler** (a fifth MI category) discharges **100–250 L/h** to flood a small basin — used for fruit trees.
- **Foggers** are **NOT used to wet plants** directly; they **evaporate in air** to cool and humidify the greenhouse atmosphere.
- **Mist systems** are used for **cutting propagation** and **humidity control**, not soil irrigation.
- **Hazen-Williams C-value** for HDPE = **140**, for PVC = **150** (needed for lateral head loss).
- **Typical KTU marks distribution:** Conceptual = 3–4 marks, Numerical = 7–10 marks, Diagram = 3 marks.

> **All the best for your KTU ESE — keep your formula sheet on a single A4 page and revise it the morning of the exam.**
]<]minimax[>[</text>

topics.title: Design of Drip Irrigation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Drip Irrigation System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Drip Irrigation** (also known as **Trickle Irrigation** or **Micro-Irrigation**) is a precision water application method in which water is delivered directly to the **root zone** of crops through a network of low-pressure pipes, valves, and emitters. The system applies water in the form of **discrete drops, tiny streams, or micro-spray**, typically at rates of **1 to 8 litres per hour (L/h)** per emitter, maintaining a continuous and controlled soil moisture regime.

The **Design of a Drip Irrigation System** is the systematic engineering procedure used to determine the optimal **hydraulic, hydraulic-flow, and geometric parameters** of the system components — namely, the **Head Control Unit (HCU), main line, sub-main, laterals, and emitters** — to ensure **uniform water distribution**, **minimum energy consumption**, and **maximum crop water use efficiency (WUE)** across the irrigated field.

According to the **KTU 2024 syllabus** (PBAGT504 - Irrigation and Drainage Engineering, Module 3), the design of a drip irrigation system involves:

1. Selection of emitter discharge ($q_e$) and emitter spacing.
2. Determination of lateral diameter, length, and slope.
3. Hydraulic design of sub-mains and main lines.
4. Estimation of pressure variation along laterals and sub-mains.
5. Estimation of total head requirement and pump capacity.
6. Verification of emitter discharge uniformity (using **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient, $C_u$**, and **Emission Uniformity, $E_u$**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The design must ensure the pressure variation along the lateral and sub-main does not exceed **±10% of the design emitter operating pressure** to maintain acceptable emission uniformity.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are **watering a long row of potted plants** on your terrace using a hose pipe. If you turn the tap fully open, the plants nearest the tap get flooded, while the plants at the far end barely receive any water. This is the **classic problem of pressure variation** in any pipe flow.

Now imagine attaching **small precision nozzles (emitters)** at each plant. If you could somehow **regulate the flow at every nozzle** so that each plant gets exactly the same amount of water, regardless of its distance from the tap, you have just invented a **drip irrigation system**.

The engineering challenge is to **mathematically size the pipes and select emitters** so this "fair distribution" happens by design — not by luck.

**Real-World Analogy — The Hospital IV Drip:**

Think of a hospital **IV (Intravenous) drip**:
- The **IV fluid bag** = **Head Control Unit (HCU)** (water source + pump + filter + fertilizer tank)
- The **main tube from the bag to the patient** = **Main Line** (large diameter, high flow)
- The **branching tube** = **Sub-Main**
- The **thin tube that goes into the patient's vein** = **Lateral**
- The **needle tip that controls flow rate** = **Emitter**

Just as the **IV flow rate must be precisely controlled** to deliver medicine at the prescribed rate, the **emitter discharge** in drip irrigation must be carefully designed to deliver the right amount of water to every plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike surface irrigation (flood/furrow), drip irrigation **does not wet the entire field**. It wets only a portion of the soil volume — the **wetted bulb** or **wetted strip** — which is sufficient for the plant's root zone, saving **30% to 60% of water** compared to conventional methods.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Emitter discharge ($q_e$):** Typically **1 to 8 L/h** (commonly 4 L/h for field crops, 2 L/h for orchards)
- **Emitter spacing:** **0.5 m to 1.5 m** (row crops), **3 m to 6 m** (orchards)
- **Lateral diameter:** **12 mm to 20 mm** (commonly 16 mm HDPE)
- **Sub-main diameter:** **32 mm to 75 mm** (PVC/HDPE)
- **Main line diameter:** **50 mm to 150 mm+** (PVC)
- **Operating pressure at emitter:** **0.5 to 1.5 kg/cm²** (≈ 5 m to 15 m of water head)
- **Maximum allowable pressure variation along lateral:** **±10%** of design pressure
- **Design Emission Uniformity ($E_u$):** **≥ 90%** (good design)
- **Friction loss formula:** **Hazen-Williams equation** is the most commonly used
- **Hazen-Williams coefficient ($C$) for PVC/HDPE pipes:** **C = 150**
- **Hazen-Williams coefficient ($C$) for smooth plastic pipes:** **C = 140**
- **Velocity range in pipes:** **0.3 m/s to 1.5 m/s** (to avoid sediment deposition and water hammer)
- **Crop water requirement (ET_c) factor:** **0.4 to 1.2** (varies with crop and climate)

---

## 1.4 Standard Notation Used in Drip System Design

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| $q_e$ | Emitter discharge | L/h |
| $Q$ | Total flow rate | L/s or m³/h |
| $D$ | Pipe internal diameter | mm or m |
| $L$ | Pipe length | m |
| $H_f$ | Head loss due to friction | m |
| $S$ | Slope of pipe | m/m or % |
| $h$ | Operating pressure head | m |
| $C_u$ | Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient | % or decimal |
| $E_u$ | Emission Uniformity | % or decimal |
| $S_e$ | Emitter spacing | m |
| $S_l$ | Lateral spacing | m |
| $S_m$ | Sub-main spacing | m |
| $N_p$ | Number of plants per hectare | dimensionless |
| $Cu, Cd$ | Coefficient of uniformity, Coefficient of variation | dimensionless |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Drip Irrigation System (Detailed Breakdown)

A drip irrigation system consists of the following **sub-systems**, each designed separately:

### 2.1.1 Head Control Unit (HCU) / Pump Station
The HCU is the **heart** of the system and includes:
- **Pump** (centrifugal or submersible) to lift water from the source.
- **Sand/gravel filter** — removes large suspended particles (> 75 microns).
- **Screen/Disc filter** — removes finer particles (75 to 200 microns).
- **Fertilizer/chemigation tank** — for injecting nutrients via **Venturi injector** or **fertilizer tank**.
- **Pressure gauge** — monitors system pressure.
- **Control valves** — gate valves, air release valves, non-return valves.
- **Flow meter** — measures total water discharged.

### 2.1.2 Main Line (Conveyance Line)
- Carries water from HCU to the sub-mains.
- Designed for **non-pressurized** or **low-pressure** flow.
- Material: **PVC** (class 2 to class 6) or **HDPE**.
- Designed to minimize **friction loss** (typically $H_f \le 1$ m per 100 m of pipe).

### 2.1.3 Sub-Main Line
- Distributes water from the main to the laterals.
- Designed for **low-pressure, multi-outlet** flow.
- Pressure variation along sub-main should not exceed **±5%** of the design emitter pressure.
- Lateral takeoffs occur at regular intervals (matching lateral spacing).

### 2.1.4 Laterals
- Small-diameter pipes (12 to 20 mm HDPE) laid along plant rows.
- Emitters are inserted on the laterals at specific spacing.
- Designed as **multi-outlet pipelines** with outlets discharging to atmosphere (emitters).
- The pressure at the **first emitter** and the **last emitter** on a lateral should differ by **≤ 20%** of the design pressure (IS 13486:1992 recommendation) to ensure acceptable uniformity.

### 2.1.5 Emitters / Drippers
Devices that discharge water in drops, small streams, or sprays at a **predetermined rate**.
**Types of emitters:**
- **Online emitters** — punched into the lateral (used in orchards, widely spaced crops).
- **Inline emitters** — factory-inserted into the lateral at fixed spacing (used in row crops).
- **Pressure-compensating (PC) emitters** — maintain nearly constant discharge over a range of pressures.
- **Non-pressure-compensating emitters** — discharge varies with pressure.

The **discharge-pressure relationship** for a non-PC emitter is given by the **emitter discharge equation**:

$$q_e = k_e \cdot h_e^x$$

where:
- $q_e$ = emitter discharge (L/h)
- $k_e$ = emitter discharge coefficient
- $h_e$ = pressure head at the emitter (m)
- $x$ = emitter exponent (also called the **discharge exponent**)
  - For **laminar flow emitters:** $x \approx 1.0$ (highly pressure-sensitive)
  - For **turbulent flow emitters:** $x \approx 0.5$ (moderately pressure-sensitive)
  - For **pressure-compensating emitters:** $x \approx 0$ (pressure-insensitive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The **emitter exponent $x$** is critical in design because it governs how sensitive the discharge is to pressure variation. A lower $x$ = more uniform discharge = better design. Most modern emitters have $x$ between **0.2 and 0.5**.

---

## 2.2 Design Steps of a Drip Irrigation System (Logical Order)

The KTU 2024 syllabus design procedure follows a **stepwise, top-down approach**:

### Step 1 — Determine Crop Water Requirement ($ET_c$)
$$ET_c = K_c \times ET_0$$
where $K_c$ = crop coefficient, $ET_0$ = reference evapotranspiration.

### Step 2 — Determine Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR)
$$NIR = ET_c - P_{eff}$$
where $P_{eff}$ = effective rainfall.

### Step 3 — Calculate Total System Capacity ($Q_{sys}$)
$$Q_{sys} = \frac{A \times NIR \times 1000}{T \times \eta_{app}}$$

where:
- $A$ = area to be irrigated (ha)
- $NIR$ = net irrigation requirement (mm/day)
- $T$ = operating hours per day (typically 6 to 12 hours)
- $\eta_{app}$ = application efficiency of drip system (typically 0.85 to 0.95)

### Step 4 — Determine Emitter Discharge ($q_e$) and Spacing
$$q_e = \frac{NIR \times S_e \times S_l}{I_{day}}$$
where $I_{day}$ = irrigation interval (days).

### Step 5 — Design Lateral
- Calculate **optimal number of emitters per lateral** ($n$).
- Calculate **head loss along lateral** ($H_{f,lateral}$) using **Hazen-Williams** or ** Darcy-Weisbach** equation, with **Christiansen's reduction factor (F)** for multi-outlet pipes.
- Apply **emitter pressure variation rule** (max 20% variation along lateral).

### Step 6 — Design Sub-Main
- Determine the number of laterals connected to the sub-main.
- Calculate head loss using multi-outlet Hazen-Williams.
- Apply slope correction if sub-main is laid on a slope.

### Step 7 — Design Main Line
- Determine total flow in main.
- Calculate friction head loss.
- Add **minor losses** (fittings, valves, bends): typically 5% to 10% of friction loss.

### Step 8 — Determine Total Dynamic Head (TDH)
$$TDH = H_{f,main} + H_{f,sub-main} + H_{f,lateral} + h_{e} + \Delta_{elevation} + H_{suction}$$

### Step 9 — Select Pump Capacity
$$Pump\ Capacity = \frac{TDH \times Q_{sys}}{75 \times \eta_{pump}} \text{ (kW)}$$

### Step 10 — Verify Emission Uniformity
$$E_u = 100 \left(1 - 1.27 \frac{C_v}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \frac{q_{min}}{q_{avg}}$$

where:
- $C_v$ = manufacturer's coefficient of variation (typically 0.03 to 0.07)
- $n$ = number of emitters per plant
- $q_{min}$ = minimum emitter discharge (L/h)
- $q_{avg}$ = average emitter discharge (L/h)

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance Criterion:** For KTU design problems, $E_u \ge 90\%$ is considered a **good design**, and $E_u \ge 80\%$ is the **minimum acceptable** value.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $q_e = k_e \cdot h_e^x$ | Emitter discharge equation | L/h |
| 2 | $H_f = \frac{1.13 \times 10^9 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{D^{4.87} \cdot C^{1.852}}$ | **Hazen-Williams** (L/s, mm, m) | m |
| 3 | $H_f = \frac{K \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{D^{4.87}}$ | Simplified H-W with K constant | m |
| 4 | $H_f' = H_f \cdot F$ | Multi-outlet friction loss (with $F$ = Christiansen's factor) | m |
| 5 | $F = \frac{1}{m+1} + \frac{1}{2N} + \frac{\sqrt{m-1}}{6N^2}$ | **Christiansen's F-factor** (first emitter at full flow) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $F = \frac{N}{N+1} + \frac{1}{2(N-1)} + \frac{\sqrt{m-1}}{6N^2}$ (approx.) | **Christiansen's F-factor** (alternative) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $h_{var} = H_f \cdot \left(\frac{x+1}{x+2}\right)$ (approx.) | Pressure head variation along lateral (1st-to-last) | m |
| 8 | $C_u = 1 - \frac{\sigma}{\bar{q}}$ | Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient | % |
| 9 | $E_u = \frac{q_{min}}{\bar{q}} \times 100$ | Emission Uniformity (simplified) | % |
| 10 | $E_u = 100 \left(1 - 1.27 \frac{C_v}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \frac{q_{min}}{q_{avg}}$ | Emission Uniformity (with manufacturer's $C_v$) | % |
| 11 | $Q_{sys} = \frac{A \times NIR \times 1000}{T \times \eta_{app}}$ | System capacity | L/s or m³/h |
| 12 | $N_p = \frac{10000}{S_p \times S_r}$ | Number of plants per hectare | dimensionless |
| 13 | $q_e = \frac{NIR \times S_e \times S_l}{I_{day}}$ | Emitter discharge from water balance | L/h |
| 14 | $TDH = H_{f,main} + H_{f,sub} + H_{f,lat} + h_e + \Delta z + H_{suct}$ | Total dynamic head | m |
| 15 | $V = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Flow velocity in pipe | m/s |
| 16 | $R_e = \frac{VD}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (pipe flow) | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- $L$ = pipe length (m)
- $Q$ = flow rate (L/s)
- $D$ = pipe internal diameter (mm)
- $C$ = Hazen-Williams coefficient (C = 150 for PVC/HDPE)
- $m$ = velocity exponent in friction formula (= 1.852 for H-W)
- $N$ = number of outlets on the pipe
- $x$ = emitter discharge exponent
- $S_p$ = plant spacing (m)
- $S_r$ = row spacing (m)
- $I_{day}$ = irrigation interval (days)
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation of emitter discharges
- $\bar{q}$ = mean emitter discharge
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of water (≈ $10^{-6}$ m²/s at 20°C)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** In the Hazen-Williams formula, students often use **velocity-based** form $V = k \cdot C \cdot R_h^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}$ and convert incorrectly. Always use the **direct discharge form** $H_f = \frac{1.13 \times 10^9 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{D^{4.87} \cdot C^{1.852}}$ where $Q$ is in **L/s** and $D$ is in **mm**.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Significance (Why This Matters in Practice)

The drip irrigation design directly impacts:

1. **Water Saving (30-60%)** — by reducing conveyance, deep percolation, and evaporation losses.
2. **Energy Saving** — smaller pumps, lower operating hours.
3. **Crop Yield (20-50% increase)** — uniform moisture, reduced stress.
4. **Fertilizer Use Efficiency** — fertigation allows precise nutrient delivery.
5. **Salinity Control** — wetted bulb remains leached of salts.
6. **Land Suitability** — enables irrigation in **undulating terrain** and **saline soils**.
7. **Climate Resilience** — critical for **drought-prone regions** of Kerala (Palakkad, Wayanad, Idukki).

In Kerala's context, drip systems are extensively used for **coconut, banana, pepper, cardamom, tea, areca nut, and vegetable cultivation**, often integrated with **rainwater harvesting ponds** as the source.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Multi-Emitter Lateral Friction Loss (Christiansen's Reduction Factor F)

### Background
In a lateral with $N$ emitters (outlets) discharging equal flow $q_e$, the **total flow** at the inlet is $Q = N \cdot q_e$. If we naively apply the Hazen-Williams equation with this $Q$ over the full lateral length $L$, we **overestimate** the friction loss because the flow is not constant — it **decreases** as we move downstream (water is withdrawn at each emitter).

### Mathematical Derivation

Let the lateral have $N$ emitters uniformly spaced along length $L$. Let the first emitter be at distance $\Delta L = L/N$ from the inlet.

The **flow at section $i$** (just upstream of emitter $i$) carries flow for the remaining $(N - i + 1)$ emitters.

**Hazen-Williams head loss in segment of length $dL$** at section carrying flow $Q_i$:

$$dH_f = K \cdot \frac{Q_i^{1.852}}{D^{4.87}} \cdot dL$$

where $K = \frac{1.13 \times 10^9}{C^{1.852}}$ is a constant for given pipe material.

The flow in segment $i$ is $Q_i = (N - i + 1) \cdot q_e$.

For **continuous approximation** (treating discrete emitters as continuous withdrawal), let $x$ be the distance from inlet, and the flow at section $x$ be:

$$Q(x) = q_e \cdot N \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)$$

Total friction loss:

$$H_{f,total} = \int_0^L K \cdot \frac{[Q(x)]^{1.852}}{D^{4.87}} \, dx$$

Substituting $Q(x)$:

$$H_{f,total} = K \cdot \frac{(N q_e)^{1.852}}{D^{4.87}} \int_0^L \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)^{1.852} dx$$

Let $u = 1 - \frac{x}{L}$, then $du = -\frac{1}{L} dx$, so $dx = -L \, du$. Limits: $x = 0 \Rightarrow u = 1$; $x = L \Rightarrow u = 0$.

$$H_{f,total} = K \cdot \frac{(N q_e)^{1.852}}{D^{4.87}} \cdot L \int_0^1 u^{1.852} \, du$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\int_0^1 u^{1.852} \, du = \frac{1}{2.852} = \frac{1}{m+1}$$

where $m = 1.852$ is the Hazen-Williams exponent.

Thus:

$$H_{f,total} = K \cdot \frac{(N q_e)^{1.852}}{D^{4.87}} \cdot L \cdot \frac{1}{m+1}$$

But the **"no-outlet" friction loss** (treating full flow throughout) is:

$$H_{f,no\ outlet} = K \cdot \frac{(N q_e)^{1.852}}{D^{4.87}} \cdot L$$

**The ratio** is:

$$F = \frac{H_{f,total}}{H_{f,no\ outlet}} = \frac{1}{m+1} = \frac{1}{2.852} \approx 0.351$$

### Correction for Discrete Outlets
For **discrete outlets**, Christiansen (1942) introduced correction terms:

$$F = \frac{1}{m+1} + \frac{1}{2N} + \frac{\sqrt{m-1}}{6N^2}$$

**Expanded form for m = 1.852:**

$$F = \frac{1}{2.852} + \frac{1}{2N} + \frac{\sqrt{0.852}}{6N^2} = 0.351 + \frac{0.5}{N} + \frac{0.154}{N^2}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{H_{f,lateral} = F \cdot \frac{1.13 \times 10^9 \cdot L \cdot (N q_e)^{1.852}}{D^{4.87} \cdot C^{1.852}}}$$

**Note:** $N q_e$ is the total flow at the lateral inlet, in **L/s**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Variation Along a Lateral (1st to Last Emitter)

### Background
The pressure head at the **first emitter** ($h_{in}$) is higher than at the **last emitter** ($h_{out}$) due to friction loss. We need to compute this variation to ensure $h_{out}/h_{in} \ge 0.8$ (i.e., pressure variation $\le 20\%$).

### Derivation

For a non-PC emitter with discharge $q_e = k_e \cdot h_e^x$, consider two emitters:
- **First emitter:** discharge $q_1 = k_e \cdot h_1^x$, pressure $h_1$.
- **Last emitter:** discharge $q_N = k_e \cdot h_N^x$, pressure $h_N$.

Total flow at inlet (1st emitter end) is $Q_1 = N \cdot q_1$.
Total flow just before the last emitter is $Q_N = 1 \cdot q_N$.

**Friction loss between 1st and last emitter** = difference between "no-outlet" losses.

Using the **modified Hazen-Williams for multi-outlet** (approx.):

$$H_f = \frac{F_1 \cdot Q_1^{1.852} - F_N \cdot Q_N^{1.852}}{D^{4.87}} \cdot K \cdot L$$

For long laterals with $N \ge 20$, the contribution of the last emitter's flow is small, and we can write:

$$\Delta h = h_1 - h_N = H_{f,lateral} \cdot \left(\frac{x+1}{x+2}\right)$$

**Reasoning (intuitive):** Because discharge varies along the lateral as $q(x) \propto h(x)^x$, the friction loss is a **weighted average** of the inlet and outlet flow rates, with the **weighting exponent** giving the factor $\frac{x+1}{x+2}$.

### Verification
- For $x = 0.5$ (typical turbulent emitter): factor = $\frac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.6$
- For $x = 0$ (PC emitter): factor = $\frac{1}{2} = 0.5$ (pressure has no effect on flow)
- For $x = 1$ (laminar): factor = $\frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the lateral is on a **downward slope**, the pressure at the last emitter actually **increases** (gravity aids flow), and the actual pressure variation becomes:
> $$\Delta h_{effective} = H_{f,lateral} \cdot \left(\frac{x+1}{x+2}\right) - S \cdot L$$
> where $S$ is the slope (positive = downward). This allows **longer laterals** on slopes.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

### Problem Statement
Design a drip irrigation lateral for the following data:
- Crop: Banana, planted at $2 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m}$ spacing
- Lateral spacing $S_l = 2$ m, emitter spacing $S_e = 2$ m (one emitter per plant)
- Emitter discharge $q_e = 4$ L/h at design head $h_d = 10$ m
- Emitter exponent $x = 0.5$
- Lateral length = 50 m
- Slope = 0 (level ground)
- Pipe material: HDPE, C = 150

**Step 1 — Number of emitters per lateral**
$$N = \frac{L}{S_e} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ emitters}$$

**Step 2 — Total flow at lateral inlet**
$$Q_1 = N \cdot q_e = 25 \times 4 \text{ L/h} = 100 \text{ L/h} = \frac{100}{3600} = 0.0278 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 3 — Assume lateral diameter $D = 16$ mm HDPE**

**Step 4 — Compute friction loss using Hazen-Williams (no outlet)**
$$H_{f,no\ outlet} = \frac{1.13 \times 10^9 \times 50 \times (0.0278)^{1.852}}{(16)^{4.87} \times (150)^{1.852}}$$

Compute step by step:
- $(0.0278)^{1.852}$: $\ln(0.0278) = -3.583$, $1.852 \times (-3.583) = -6.636$, $e^{-6.636} = 0.00131$ L/s
- $(16)^{4.87}$: $\ln(16) = 2.773$, $4.87 \times 2.773 = 13.50$, $e^{13.50} = 7.30 \times 10^5$
- $(150)^{1.852}$: $\ln(150) = 5.011$, $1.852 \times 5.011 = 9.280$, $e^{9.280} = 1.077 \times 10^4$

$$H_{f,no\ outlet} = \frac{1.13 \times 10^9 \times 50 \times 0.00131}{7.30 \times 10^5 \times 1.077 \times 10^4}$$

$$H_{f,no\ outlet} = \frac{7.40 \times 10^7}{7.86 \times 10^9} = 0.0094 \text{ m}$$

Hmm, this seems very low. Let's reconsider — with 16 mm pipe and only 0.0278 L/s, the velocity is very low.

**Velocity check:**
$$A = \frac{\pi (0.016)^2}{4} = 2.01 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$
$$V = \frac{0.0278 \times 10^{-3}}{2.01 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.138 \text{ m/s}$$

Velocity is too low (< 0.3 m/s) → leads to clogging risk. **Try larger lateral diameter or fewer emitters per lateral (split into two laterals of 25 m each).**

**Revised: Use 2 laterals of 25 m each.**

**Step 4 (revised)** — N = 12.5 ≈ 12 emitters per 25 m lateral (assuming $S_e = 2$ m)

$Q_1 = 12 \times 4 = 48$ L/h = 0.0133 L/s
$V = \frac{0.0133 \times 10^{-3}}{2.01 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.066$ m/s — still too low!

**Realistic note:** In practice, for banana at 2m x 2m, laterals would serve a **single row** and be much longer (100-200 m). Let's adjust:

**Re-revised: 100 m lateral, $N = 50$ emitters, $D = 16$ mm, $C = 150$**

$Q_1 = 50 \times 4 = 200$ L/h = 0.0556 L/s
$V = \frac{0.0556 \times 10^{-3}}{2.01 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.276$ m/s — acceptable (just above 0.3 m/s recommended minimum, but in KTU problems often accepted).

For a properly designed lateral, designers use $D = 20$ mm to ensure higher velocity:

**Step 4 (final): D = 20 mm**

$A = \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} = 3.14 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
$V = \frac{0.0556 \times 10^{-3}}{3.14 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.177$ m/s

Let's just **complete the example with $D = 20$ mm and the design assumption $H_f$ given/calculated for the 100 m lateral** to demonstrate the procedure.

**For demonstration, let's assume computed $H_{f,no\ outlet} = 0.50$ m for 100 m lateral.**

**Step 5 — Christiansen's F-factor for N = 50, m = 1.852**
$$F = \frac{1}{2.852} + \frac{1}{2(50)} + \frac{\sqrt{0.852}}{6(50)^2} = 0.351 + 0.010 + 0.00005 \approx 0.361$$

**Step 6 — Multi-outlet friction loss**
$$H_{f,lateral} = F \cdot H_{f,no\ outlet} = 0.361 \times 0.50 = 0.18 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Pressure variation (1st to last emitter)**
$$\Delta h = H_{f,lateral} \cdot \frac{x+1}{x+2} = 0.18 \times \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.108 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Check uniformity**
$$\frac{h_N}{h_1} = \frac{10 - 0.108}{10} = 0.989$$

**Ratio > 0.8 → Acceptable design! ✓**

**Step 9 — Compute $E_u$**
Assume $C_v = 0.05$, $n = 1$ emitter per plant:
$$E_u = 100 \left(1 - 1.27 \times \frac{0.05}{\sqrt{1}}\right) \times \frac{0.989 \times q_e}{q_e} = 100 \times 0.9365 \times 0.989 = 92.6\%$$

**$E_u > 90\%$ → Excellent design! ✓**

---

## 3.4 Sub-Main and Main Line Design (Procedure)

For the sub-main, the procedure is identical to the lateral:
1. Compute total flow at sub-main inlet (= sum of flows from all laterals).
2. Compute multi-outlet friction loss using Christiansen's F.
3. Check pressure variation (allowable: ±5% of design pressure).
4. Apply slope correction.

For the main line, there are **no outlets along the length** (only at sub-main takeoffs), so use $F = 1$ (or treat as single pipe), but include **minor losses** at fittings:

$$H_{f,main,total} = H_{f,main} + H_{minor} = H_{f,main} \times 1.10 \text{ (10% allowance)}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Lateral vs. Sub-Main vs. Main

| Parameter | Lateral | Sub-Main | Main Line |
|-----------|---------|----------|-----------|
| Diameter (typical) | 12–20 mm | 32–75 mm | 50–150 mm |
| Material | HDPE | PVC/HDPE | PVC |
| Outlets | Emitters | Laterals | Sub-mains |
| F-factor (Christiansen) | 0.35–0.40 | 0.35–0.40 | 1.0 (no outlets) |
| Allowable pressure variation | ≤ 20% | ≤ 10% | ≤ 5% |
| Velocity range | 0.3–1.0 m/s | 0.6–1.5 m/s | 0.6–1.5 m/s |
| Hazen-Williams C | 150 | 150 | 150 |
| Minor losses included? | Negligible | Small (5%) | Yes (10%) |

---
