<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define sprinkler irrigation. List any four advantages of sprinkler irrigation over surface irrigation. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Sprinkler irrigation is a method of applying irrigation water to the land in the form of a spray, similar to natural rainfall, by discharging it through small nozzles mounted on a pressurized pipe network.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Water saving (30 %–50 %):** Eliminates conveyance losses like deep percolation and runoff common in surface methods.
2. **Suitable for undulating terrain:** Can be used on lands where land leveling for surface flow is uneconomical.
3. **Uniform application:** Ensures even distribution of water over the field, leading to better crop growth.
4. **Fertigation possible:** Fertilizers and pesticides can be injected into the irrigation stream, saving labour and ensuring uniform application.
5. **Erosion control:** Fine droplets do not disturb soil structure or cause sheet erosion.
6. **Adaptable to light soils:** Works on sandy soils where surface irrigation losses are high.

*(Any four points — 3 marks: 1 mark definition + 0.5 each for four advantages)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between impact sprinkler and gear-driven (rotating) sprinkler. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Impact Sprinkler | Gear-Driven Sprinkler |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotation mechanism** | Spring-loaded arm strikes the jet, producing stepwise rotation | Internal gear train provides smooth, continuous rotation |
| **Rotation speed** | Slow, intermittent (0.5–2 rpm) | Faster and uniform (1–4 rpm) |
| **Spray pattern** | Distinctive "click–click" intermittent spray | Smooth, continuous circular spray |
| **Application** | Field crops, orchards | Lawns, golf courses, high-value crops |
| **Pressure range** | 1.5–4.0 kg/cm² | 2.0–4.0 kg/cm² |
| **Durability** | Robust, simple design | More moving parts, requires clean water |
| **Cost** | Low | Higher |

*(Full table = 3 marks; ½ mark per differentiating row for any 6 rows)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **components of a sprinkler irrigation system**. State the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A sprinkler irrigation system has a discharge of **0.6 lps** per sprinkler and operates at a nozzle pressure of **2.5 kg/cm²**. The sprinklers are spaced at **10 m × 12 m**. Determine: **(i)** Nozzle diameter assuming *C<sub>d</sub>* = 0.96. **(ii)** Precipitation rate. **(iii)** Whether the system is suitable for a soil with infiltration rate of **8 mm/hr**. **(iv)** Time required to apply a depth of **50 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-A(a): Components of a Sprinkler System

**1. Pump Unit:** Centrifugal or submersible pump to lift water and develop required pressure (1–7 kg/cm² depending on system type).

**2. Pipe Network:**
- **Mainline** — Carries water from pump to sub-mains; usually buried.
- **Submain** — Distributes water to laterals.
- **Laterals** — Portable/permanent lines on which sprinklers are mounted; usually **63 mm or 75 mm aluminium/PE pipes**.

**3. Couplers and Fittings:** Quick-connect couplers, tees, elbows, valves, plugs for joining and controlling flow.

**4. Sprinkler Heads:** Impact, gear-driven, or fixed-spray type with single or dual nozzles.

**5. Fertilizer/Chemical Tank (Fertigation unit):** For injection of fertilizers/pesticides into the line.

**6. Filters:** Screen or disc filters to prevent nozzle clogging.

**7. Pressure Regulators & Gauges:** Maintain uniform pressure and monitor system performance.

**8. Valves:** Isolation valves, air-relief valves, drain valves.

**9. Riser Pipes:** Vertical pipes connecting lateral to sprinkler; usually **1–2 m high**.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for neat sketch, 6 marks for naming 6 components + 1 function each)*

---

#### Solution to Q-A(b): Numerical Design

**Given:** *q* = 0.6 lps, *P* = 2.5 kg/cm², spacing = 10 m × 12 m, *C<sub>d</sub>* = 0.96, *f<sub>c</sub>* = 8 mm/hr, depth = 50 mm.

**(i) Nozzle diameter:**

Convert pressure head: *H* = 2.5 × 10 = **25 m** (1 kg/cm² ≈ 10 m of water).

Convert discharge: *q* = 0.6 lps = 0.6 × 10⁻³ m³/s = **6 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s**.

From $q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$:

$$ a = \frac{q}{C_d \sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{0.96 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 25}} $$

$$ \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 25} = \sqrt{490.5} = 22.15 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ a = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{0.96 \times 22.15} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{21.26} = 2.82 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ a = 28.2 \text{ mm}^2 $$

For circular nozzle, $a = \pi d^2 / 4$:

$$ d = \sqrt{\frac{4a}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 28.2}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{35.9} = 5.99 \approx 6 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \boxed{d \approx 6 \text{ mm}} $$

**(ii) Precipitation rate:**

$$ A = 10 \times 12 = 120 \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ P = \frac{3600 \times q}{A} = \frac{3600 \times 0.6}{120} = \frac{2160}{120} = 18 \text{ mm/hr} $$

$$ \boxed{P = 18 \text{ mm/hr}} $$

**(iii) Suitability check:**

Since *P* = 18 mm/hr **>** *f<sub>c</sub>* = 8 mm/hr, the application rate **exceeds the infiltration capacity**. This will cause **runoff and puddling** — the system is **NOT suitable** unless spacing is increased, discharge is reduced, or set-time rotation is adopted.

**(iv) Time of application for 50 mm depth:**

$$ t = \frac{d \times A}{q_{lps} \times 3600} = \frac{50 \times 120}{0.6 \times 3600} = \frac{6000}{2160} = 2.78 \text{ hr} \approx 2 \text{ hr } 47 \text{ min} $$

$$ \boxed{t \approx 2.78 \text{ hours}} $$

*(7 marks: 2 marks part (i) + 2 marks part (ii) + 1.5 marks part (iii) + 1.5 marks part (iv))*

---

### **Question B** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**a)** Classify the **types of sprinkler irrigation systems** based on portability, method of operation, and spray mechanism. Describe briefly the **centre-pivot system** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Define **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU)**. In a field test, the catch-can readings in mm (12 cans placed in a 4-sprinkler overlap test) are: **17, 19, 22, 20, 24, 21, 18, 23, 25, 20, 19, 22.** Calculate the CU and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-B(a): Classification & Centre-Pivot System

**Classification (3 marks):**

**Based on Portability:**
1. **Permanent (Fixed) System** — Mains and laterals are buried; sprinklers fixed permanently. High initial cost, low labour. Used in orchards, lawns, gardens.
2. **Semi-Permanent** — Mainline and submain fixed; laterals portable.
3. **Portable** — All components movable. Lowest cost, highest labour.

**Based on Method of Operation:**
1. **Solid Set** — System installed for the entire crop season; not moved.
2. **Set-Move (Hand-Move, Side-Roll)** — Laterals moved manually or mechanically between successive positions.
3. **Continuous-Move** — System moves continuously during irrigation: *Centre Pivot, Linear Move, Travelling Big Gun.*

**Based on Spray Mechanism:**
1. **Rotating Head (Impact / Gear-Driven)**
2. **Fixed Spray / Spray Nozzle**
3. **Perforated Pipe (Leaky Pipe)**

**Centre-Pivot System (4 marks):**

A centre-pivot (or "circular") system consists of a **lateral pipeline supported on a series of A-frame towers** (each 30–50 m apart), with multiple sprinklers mounted along its length. The lateral is **anchored at a central pivot point** connected to the water supply and pump. The whole structure rotates slowly around the pivot, completing a full circle in **8–60 hours**, irrigating an area that is essentially a **circular sector (≈ 130–160 acres per circle of 400 m radius)**.

The lateral is **self-propelled** by electric or hydraulic motors on each tower; alignment is maintained by a sensor at each tower that signals the next tower to advance or stop. An **end gun** at the far end extends the wetted radius. Centre-pivot systems are **highly labour-efficient** and are widely used in **sugarcane, maize, soybean, alfalfa** in India, USA, and Brazil.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for classification + 3 marks for description + 1 mark for diagram)*

---

#### Solution to Q-B(b): CU Calculation

**Given readings (mm):** 17, 19, 22, 20, 24, 21, 18, 23, 25, 20, 19, 22 (n = 12)

**Step 1 — Sum of readings:**

$$ \sum x_i = 17+19+22+20+24+21+18+23+25+20+19+22 = 250 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2 — Mean:**

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{250}{12} = 20.833 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 3 — Absolute deviations:**

| *x<sub>i</sub>* | $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ |
|---|---|
| 17 | 3.833 |
| 19 | 1.833 |
| 22 | 1.167 |
| 20 | 0.833 |
| 24 | 3.167 |
| 21 | 0.167 |
| 18 | 2.833 |
| 23 | 2.167 |
| 25 | 4.167 |
| 20 | 0.833 |
| 19 | 1.833 |
| 22 | 1.167 |
| **Sum** | **24.0** |

**Step 4 — CU:**

$$ CU = 100 \left( 1 - \frac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n \bar{x}} \right) = 100 \left( 1 - \frac{24.0}{12 \times 20.833} \right) $$

$$ CU = 100 \left( 1 - \frac{24.0}{250} \right) = 100 \left( 1 - 0.096 \right) = 100 \times 0.904 $$

$$ \boxed{CU = 90.4 \%} $$

**Step 5 — Comment:**

A CU of **90.4 %** is **excellent** (well above the 85 % benchmark for field crops). The sprinkler layout, pressure, and wind conditions are well-suited for **uniform application** in this test.

*(7 marks: 1 mark definition + 4 marks calculation + 2 marks for tabulation and comment)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit confusion (most common, –2 marks):** Forgetting to convert lps → m³/s in the nozzle equation, or writing *q* in m³/s directly in the precipitation formula. **Always state units at every step.**
> 2. **Confusing *C<sub>d</sub>* with *C<sub>v</sub>* or *C<sub>c</sub>*:***C<sub>d</sub>* = C<sub>c</sub> × C<sub>v</sub> is the lumped coefficient. Don't separate them unless explicitly asked.
> 3. **Forgetting to compare P vs *f<sub>c</sub>*:** Examiners will deduct marks if the "suitability" verdict is not accompanied by a numerical comparison.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch in classification questions:** A neat labelled diagram of the centre-pivot or the component layout is worth **1–1.5 marks** by itself; never skip it.
> 5. **Wrong sign in CU formula:** It is *100 × (1 – D/x̄)*, not *100 × (D/x̄)*. The "1 – " part is where uniformity comes from.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 1 hour before the exam**

- **Definition:** Sprinkler irrigation = pressurized application of water as spray mimicking rainfall.
- **Pressure range:** 1–4 kg/cm² (rotating head); 4–7 kg/cm² (gun-type).
- **Nozzle discharge:** $q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ (m³/s).
- **Precipitation rate:** $P = 3600 q / A$ mm/hr (q in lps, A in m²).
- **Application rate must be ≤ soil infiltration rate** ($P \le f_c$); otherwise runoff occurs.
- **Christiansen CU:** $CU = 100(1 - D/\bar{x})$ where $D$ is mean absolute deviation.
- **CU benchmarks:** ≥ 85 % = good for field crops; ≥ 90 % = excellent; < 70 % = poor.
- **Spacing rule:** Sprinklers spaced at **50 %–65 % of wetted diameter** for proper overlap.
- **Three classifications of systems:** (i) by portability (fixed, semi-permanent, portable); (ii) by operation (solid-set, set-move, continuous-move); (iii) by spray mechanism (rotating head, fixed spray, perforated pipe).
- **Centre-pivot:** rotates around a central anchor; irrigates a circular area; high efficiency, low labour.
- **Linear-move (lateral-move):** moves linearly along a rectangular field; suitable for square plots.
- **Travelling gun (big gun):** single high-pressure gun mounted on a trolley; large droplets, long throw, low uniformity.
- **Side-roll:** lateral is on wheels with a motor; rolled sideways to next set.
- **Key components to remember:** Pump, mainline, submain, lateral, coupler, riser, sprinkler head, nozzle, filter, fertigation unit, pressure gauge, valve.
- **Best-suited soils:** Sandy to medium loam; slopes up to 20 % (rotating head).
- **Best-suited crops:** Field crops, orchards, plantations, vegetables.
- **Wind limit:** Sprinkler operation restricted to **wind speed < 15 km/hr**.
- **Duty & delta still apply** — sprinkler doesn't change the crop's water requirement, only the method of delivery.
- **Wetted diameter D ≈ 1.0 to 1.5 d<sub>nozzle</sub> √H** for impact sprinklers.
- **Application efficiency** in sprinklers: typically **60 %–75 %** for field installations; up to **90 %** with careful design.
- **Nozzle materials:** Brass (most common), stainless steel, plastic; *C<sub>d</sub>* ≈ 0.95–0.98.
- **Quick exam tip:** Always state the **discharge unit, area unit, and resulting unit** of the answer — examiners scan for this first.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Sprinkler Irrigation System - Layout Selection, Performance Evaluation of Sprinkler Irrigation System - Uniformity Coefficient and Pattern Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Sprinkler Irrigation System - Layout Selection, Performance Evaluation of Sprinkler Irrigation System - Uniformity Coefficient and Pattern Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sprinkler Irrigation System — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sprinkler Irrigation System** is a pressurized irrigation method in which water is applied to the land surface in the form of a spray or artificial rainfall, simulating natural precipitation. The water is pumped through a pressurized pipe network and discharged through sprinklers (nozzles) to achieve uniform distribution over the cultivated field.

According to KTU 2024 scheme (PBAGT504 - Module 3), the design of a sprinkler irrigation system involves two critical engineering tasks:

1. **Layout Selection** — Geometric arrangement of sprinklers on the field (spacing pattern).
2. **Performance Evaluation** — Quantitative assessment of how uniformly water is distributed, measured using parameters like the **Uniformity Coefficient (UC)** and **Pattern Efficiency (PE)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rain Umbrella Problem"

Imagine you are standing in a garden holding a garden-hose spray nozzle. The water falls in a circle around you — heavy directly under the nozzle and lighter at the edges. If you place multiple such sprinklers across a field, just like overlapping umbrellas in rain, the water patterns from adjacent sprinklers overlap, ensuring the **entire field receives nearly equal water**.

- **Layout Selection** = deciding *how far apart* to place these "umbrellas" so that overlap is adequate but not wasteful.
- **Uniformity Coefficient** = a *mathematical fairness score* that tells you how equally every corner of the field is watered.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard performance metrics in sprinkler irrigation (IS 7911 / KTU 2024):**
> - **Operating Pressure:** $\mathbf{1.0 \text{ to } 4.0 \; kg/cm^2}$ (10 m to 40 m head)
> - **Sprinkler Spacing:** 6 m to 48 m
> - **Application Rate:** $\mathbf{2.5 \text{ to } 12.5 \; mm/hr}$ (typically must be less than soil infiltration rate)
> - **Application Efficiency:** $\mathbf{60\% \text{ to } 75\%}$ (well-designed systems)
> - **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CUC):** Acceptable field value $\mathbf{80\% \text{ to } 90\%}$
> - **Distribution Uniformity (DU):** Acceptable $\mathbf{75\%+}$

### 1.4 Formal Definition — Uniformity Coefficient (UC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Uniformity Coefficient (UC)**, also called **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CUC)**, is a statistical measure of how evenly water is distributed over the irrigated area. It expresses the ratio of the average depth of water infiltrated minus the average deviation, to the average depth infiltrated.

$$CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{x}}\right)$$

where:
- $x_i$ = depth of water collected at the $i^{th}$ point (catch-can reading)
- $\bar{x}$ = mean depth of water application
- $n$ = total number of observation points
- $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value

### 1.5 Formal Definition — Pattern Efficiency (PE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Efficiency (PE)** is the ratio of the average depth of water received in the area actually wetted (overlap region) to the average depth of water applied across the entire spacing pattern, expressed as a percentage.

$$PE = \frac{\text{Average depth in overlapped wetted area}}{\text{Average depth over the entire spacing pattern}} \times 100$$

- **A high pattern efficiency** means water overlaps in such a way that dry zones are minimized.
- **PE depends entirely on the spacing (S) and the wetted diameter (D) of the sprinkler.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sprinkler Layout Selection — Geometric Arrangements

The three standard geometric patterns used in sprinkler layout design (as per KTU 2024 syllabus) are:

| Layout Type | Geometry | Spacing Notation | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Square Layout** | Sprinklers at corners of a square | $S \times S$ | Uniform wind conditions, regular fields |
| **Rectangular Layout** | Sprinklers at corners of a rectangle | $S_l \times S_m$ | Rectangular plots, wind-affected fields |
| **Triangular (Equilateral) Layout** | Sprinklers at vertices of equilateral triangles | $S \times S \times S$ | Most uniform coverage, minimal dry spots |

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Logic:** Triangular layout gives the highest uniformity (~10–15% better than square) because of staggered overlapping. However, square/rectangular layouts are operationally simpler for field workers and are often preferred in practice.

### 2.2 Concept of Spacing Ratio (K)

The **spacing ratio $K$** is the dimensionless ratio that controls overlap geometry:

$$K = \frac{S}{D}$$

where:
- $S$ = distance between two adjacent sprinklers
- $D$ = effective wetted diameter of a single sprinkler

**Recommended Spacing Ratios (KTU/IS standard):**

| Wind Speed | Recommended K (Square) | Recommended K (Triangular) |
|---|---|---|
| Calm (0–2 m/s) | 1.0 – 1.2 | 1.2 – 1.3 |
| Light (2–3.5 m/s) | 0.9 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 1.1 |
| Moderate (3.5–5 m/s) | 0.7 – 0.9 | 0.8 – 1.0 |
| Strong (>5 m/s) | 0.6 – 0.7 | 0.7 – 0.8 |

### 2.3 Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient — Step-by-Step Logic

The CUC formula derivation logic (Section 3 will derive it in full):

1. **Step 1:** Collect water in catch-cans placed at grid points across the wetted area.
2. **Step 2:** Compute the mean $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$.
3. **Step 3:** Compute deviations $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ for every can.
4. **Step 4:** Sum the absolute deviations $\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$.
5. **Step 5:** Apply the formula.

The coefficient $C_a$ in the original formula accounts for the **number of sprinklers contributing to a point**:

$$CUC = 100 \left(1 - \frac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n\bar{x}}\right)$$

A simpler form (when all cans receive water from the same number of sprinklers):

$$CUC \approx 100 \left(1 - \frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}}\right)$$

where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the catch-can readings.

### 2.4 Pattern Efficiency — Theoretical Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern efficiency depends solely on geometry**, NOT on water pressure, nozzle size, or wind. It is a function of the spacing ratio $K = S/D$.

**Empirical Pattern Efficiency Table (Standard overlap curves):**

| Spacing Ratio (S/D) | Square Pattern PE (%) | Triangular Pattern PE (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 100.0 | 100.0 |
| 0.7 | 96.0 | 97.0 |
| 0.8 | 92.0 | 95.0 |
| 0.9 | 87.0 | 91.0 |
| 1.0 | 78.0 | 86.0 |
| 1.1 | 67.0 | 79.0 |
| 1.2 | 54.0 | 71.0 |
| 1.3 | 40.0 | 62.0 |
| 1.4 | — | 50.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Inference:** A spacing ratio of $K = 1.0$ in square pattern yields PE = 78%, while in triangular it gives 86%. This 8% jump is why triangular layouts are mathematically superior.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CUC = 100\left(1 - \frac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n\bar{x}}\right)$ | Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient | % |
| 2 | $K = S/D$ | Spacing Ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $PE = \dfrac{\bar{d}_{wetted}}{\bar{d}_{spacing}} \times 100$ | Pattern Efficiency | % |
| 4 | $q = C_d \cdot A_n \sqrt{2gh}$ | Sprinkler Discharge (orifice equation) | L/s or m³/s |
| 5 | $P_{rate} = \dfrac{q_{spr} \times 3600}{S_l \times S_m}$ | Application / Precipitation Rate | mm/hr |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{d_{req}}{P_{rate}}$ | Duration of irrigation | hours |
| 7 | $DU = 100 \cdot \dfrac{\bar{x}_{25\%}}{\bar{x}}$ | Distribution Uniformity (lower quarter) | % |
| 8 | $SC = \dfrac{P_{rate}}{I_{soil}}$ | Storing Coefficient (must be $\leq 1$) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $A = \dfrac{L \times B}{S_l \times S_m}$ | Number of sprinklers per block | integer |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** Always state units of $x_i$ and $\bar{x}$ in **the same units** (mm) before applying the CUC formula. Mixing mm and cm is a common 1-mark deduction error.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CUC and PE** are used by IS 7911 (Indian Standard) for certifying sprinkler systems.
- Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Kerala Kisan Call Centres use these metrics to audit micro-irrigation subsidies.
- In **NABARD-funded sprinkler projects**, a minimum CUC of 85% is mandatory for release of subsidy.
- Designers use CUC values to determine whether to **redesign spacing** before installing systems on farmer fields.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient

**Starting Premise:** We have $n$ catch-cans placed in a grid. Each can collects a depth $x_i$ of water during a test run.

**Goal:** Express uniformity as a single percentage index.

**Step 1 — Define the mean depth applied:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

This is the arithmetic average of all catch-can readings.

**Step 2 — Define the mean absolute deviation:**

The deviation of the $i^{th}$ reading from the mean is $(x_i - \bar{x})$. To avoid cancellation, take absolute value:

$$MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$$

**Step 3 — Formulate the uniformity index:**

A perfectly uniform system would have $MAD = 0$, so we want the index to be **maximum when MAD is small** and **smaller when MAD is large**. The natural choice is:

$$UC \propto \left(1 - \frac{MAD}{\bar{x}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 100 to express as percentage:**

$$CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{MAD}{\bar{x}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute MAD back:**

$$CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{x}}\right)$$

This is the **final, KTU-board accepted form** of Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Form (equivalent derivation):** Since $MAD$ approximates the standard deviation $\sigma$ for symmetric distributions, an equivalent form is $CUC \approx 100 \cdot (1 - \sigma / \bar{x})$.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Example 1 — CUC Calculation (Full Working)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A field test was conducted using 4 sprinklers arranged in a square pattern. Catch-can readings (in mm) collected at 8 observation points are:

$x_1 = 11, \; x_2 = 13, \; x_3 = 10, \; x_4 = 12, \; x_5 = 14, \; x_6 = 9, \; x_7 = 12, \; x_8 = 11$

Calculate the Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean depth $\bar{x}$:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{11 + 13 + 10 + 12 + 14 + 9 + 12 + 11}{8} = \frac{92}{8} = 11.5 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ for each can:**

| Can $i$ | $x_i$ (mm) | $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert = \vert x_i - 11.5 \vert$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 11 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 13 | 1.5 |
| 3 | 10 | 1.5 |
| 4 | 12 | 0.5 |
| 5 | 14 | 2.5 |
| 6 | 9  | 2.5 |
| 7 | 12 | 0.5 |
| 8 | 11 | 0.5 |

**Step 3 — Sum the absolute deviations:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert = 0.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 0.5 + 2.5 + 2.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 10.0 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply CUC formula:**

$$CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{10.0}{8 \times 11.5}\right) = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{10.0}{92.0}\right)$$

$$CUC = 100 \times (1 - 0.1087) = 100 \times 0.8913$$

$$\boxed{CUC = 89.13\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $CUC = 89.13\%$ lies in the acceptable range of $80\%-90\%$ — the system is well-designed.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example 2 — Pattern Efficiency Evaluation

**Problem:**
A sprinkler has an effective wetted diameter of $D = 16 \; m$. It is installed in a **square layout** with spacing $S = 12 \; m$.
Calculate the **spacing ratio** and determine the **pattern efficiency** from the standard PE chart.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute spacing ratio:**

$$K = \frac{S}{D} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75$$

**Step 2 — Read PE from Standard Table (Square Pattern):**

For $K = 0.7 \Rightarrow PE = 96\%$
For $K = 0.8 \Rightarrow PE = 92\%$

**Step 3 — Linear interpolation for $K = 0.75$:**

$$PE = 96 - \frac{0.75 - 0.70}{0.80 - 0.70} \times (96 - 92)$$

$$PE = 96 - \frac{0.05}{0.10} \times 4 = 96 - 2.0 = 94.0\%$$

$$\boxed{PE = 94\% \; \text{(Square Layout)}}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Triangular Layout (same $K = 0.75$):**

From triangular table:
For $K = 0.7 \Rightarrow PE = 97\%$
For $K = 0.8 \Rightarrow PE = 95\%$

$$PE_{tri} = 97 - \frac{0.05}{0.10} \times 2 = 96.0\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For the same spacing ratio, the triangular layout gives $PE = 96\%$ versus the square layout's $94\%$. Therefore, **triangular layout is recommended** for higher uniformity.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example 3 — Sprinkler Discharge and Application Rate

**Problem:**
A sprinkler nozzle has area $A_n = 2.5 \;\text{cm}^2$ and discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.95$. Operating head $h = 30 \; m$. Sprinklers are placed in a $12 \; m \times 12 \; m$ square grid. Find:
(a) Sprinkler discharge $q$
(b) Precipitation / Application rate
(c) Time required to apply 50 mm depth

**Solution:**

**(a) Sprinkler Discharge:**

$$q = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

Convert area: $A_n = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2$. Use $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$, $h = 30 \; m$:

$$\sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 30} = \sqrt{588.6} = 24.26 \; m/s$$

$$q = 0.95 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 24.26 = 5.76 \times 10^{-3} \; m^3/s$$

$$\boxed{q = 5.76 \; L/s}$$

**(b) Application / Precipitation Rate:**

Area covered by one sprinkler = $12 \times 12 = 144 \; m^2$

$$P_{rate} = \frac{q \times 3600 \;(L)}{A \times 1000 \;(L/mm/m^2)} = \frac{5.76 \times 3600}{144 \times 1000} \times 1000$$

$$P_{rate} = \frac{5.76 \times 3600}{144 \times 1} = 144 \; mm/hr$$

Wait, this is unrealistically high. Recheck unit handling:

$$P_{rate} = \frac{q \; (L/s) \times 3600 \; (s/hr)}{A \; (m^2) \times 1000 \; (L/m^2/mm)} = \frac{5.76 \times 3600}{144 \times 1} = \frac{20736}{144} = 144 \; mm/hr$$

This is too high. Therefore, such a system would only be feasible on sandy, high-infiltration soils. Let me re-check the problem logic — yes, the calculation is mathematically correct; in practice, sprinklers of that size would operate on a slower application.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Always check whether $P_{rate} \leq$ soil infiltration rate. If $P_{rate}$ exceeds infiltration, surface runoff and erosion will occur. For most soils, $P_{rate} < 12 \; mm/hr$ is safe.

**(c) Time to apply 50 mm depth:**

$$t = \frac{d_{req}}{P_{rate}} = \frac{50}{144} = 0.347 \; hr = 20.8 \; min$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 21 \; \text{minutes}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sprinkler Layout Selection — Decision Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Sprinkler Layout Design] --> B[Field Geometry Assessment]
    B --> C{Is the field Rectangular?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Rectangular Layout S1 x S2]
    C -->|No| E{Is the field Square?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Square Layout S x S]
    E -->|No| G[Triangular Layout S x S x S]
    D --> H[Apply Wind Correction Factor]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Spacing Ratio K = S/D]
    I --> J{K in Acceptable Range?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Finalize Layout]
    J -->|No| L[Adjust Spacing and Recheck]
    L --> I
    K --> M[Conduct Field Test]
    M --> N[Compute CUC and PE]
    N --> O{CUC > 85% and PE > 90%?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Layout ACCEPTED]
    O -->|No| Q[Redesign Required]
    Q --> I
```

### 4.2 Performance Evaluation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sprinkler Field Test] --> B[Catch-Can Grid Setup]
    B --> C[Collect Catch-Can Readings x_i]
    C --> D[Compute Mean x bar]
    C --> E[Compute Deviations x_i - x bar]
    D --> F[Compute CUC]
    E --> F
    C --> G[Compute Spacing Ratio K = S/D]
    G --> H[Look up PE from Standard Chart]
    H --> I[Pattern Efficiency PE]
    F --> J[Performance Report]
    I --> J
    J --> K{CUC >= 85 percent?}
    K -->|Yes| L[System Approved]
    K -->|No| M[Re-design Layout]
```

### 4.3 Comparison of Layout Geometries — Schematic View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph squareLayout["SQUARE LAYOUT S x S"]
        S1[S1] --- S2[S2]
        S3[S3] --- S4[S4]
        S1 --- S3
        S2 --- S4
    end
    subgraph triLayout["TRIANGULAR LAYOUT"]
        T1[T1] --- T2[T2]
        T2 --- T3[T3]
        T3 --- T1
        T4[T4] --- T5[T5]
        T5 --- T6[T6]
        T6 --- T4
    end
    squareLayout -->|Less Uniformity| Compare[Compare PE]
    triLayout -->|More Uniformity| Compare
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture of Sprinkler System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pump Source] --> M[Main Pipeline]
    M --> S[Sub-main Pipeline]
    S --> L[Lateral Pipeline]
    L --> SP[Sprinkler Head]
    SP --> N[Nozzle Discharge]
    N --> W[Water Spray Pattern]
    W --> CC[Catch-Cans for Field Test]
    CC --> EVAL[Performance Evaluation Module]
    EVAL --> CUC[Compute CUC]
    EVAL --> PE[Compute PE]
    CUC --> DECISION{Acceptable?}
    PE --> DECISION
    DECISION -->|Yes| OK[System Certified]
    DECISION -->|No| RD[Redesign Layout]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with respect to sprinkler irrigation:**
**(a)** Spacing Ratio
**(b)** Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CUC)
**(c)** Pattern Efficiency

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Spacing Ratio (K):** The spacing ratio is defined as the ratio of the distance between two adjacent sprinklers ($S$) to the effective wetted diameter of a single sprinkler ($D$). It is a dimensionless quantity, expressed as $K = S/D$. It is the governing parameter that controls overlap geometry and uniformity.

**(b) Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CUC):** It is a statistical measure of the uniformity of water distribution in a sprinkler system, expressed as a percentage. It is given by:

$$CUC = 100 \left(1 - \frac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n\bar{x}}\right)$$

where $x_i$ = catch-can reading, $\bar{x}$ = mean reading, $n$ = number of cans.

**(c) Pattern Efficiency (PE):** It is the ratio of the average depth of water received in the overlapped wetted area to the average depth of water applied over the entire spacing pattern, expressed as a percentage. PE depends only on the spacing ratio and the layout type (square/rectangular/triangular).

---

#### **Question 2 (3 marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Compare square and triangular layout of sprinklers. Which one is more efficient and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Square Layout | Triangular (Equilateral) Layout |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Sprinklers at corners of a square | Sprinklers at vertices of equilateral triangles |
| Spacing notation | $S \times S$ | $S \times S \times S$ |
| Pattern Efficiency (for $K=1.0$) | 78% | 86% |
| Field accessibility | Easier (rows/columns) | Slightly more complex |
| Overlap geometry | Less uniform overlap | Staggered overlap → better uniformity |
| Operational simplicity | High | Moderate |

**Conclusion:** **Triangular layout is more efficient** because the staggered overlap produces higher pattern efficiency (8–10% more) and a more uniform water distribution compared to the square layout for the same spacing ratio.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Modified)]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three types of sprinkler layouts. State the conditions under which each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field test was conducted on a sprinkler system. The catch-can readings (in mm) collected at 10 observation points during the test are:

$x_1 = 9.5, \; x_2 = 11.0, \; x_3 = 10.2, \; x_4 = 12.5, \; x_5 = 8.8, \; x_6 = 10.0, \; x_7 = 11.5, \; x_8 = 9.0, \; x_9 = 10.5, \; x_{10} = 9.0$

Calculate the **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CUC)**. Comment on the quality of the design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**The three types of sprinkler layouts are:**

**1. Square Layout:**
In a square layout, sprinklers are placed at the corners of a square of side $S$. The spacing is denoted as $S \times S$. This is the most common layout used in fields where wind speed is low and the field shape is approximately square. It is simple to lay out, easy to operate, and suitable for regular-shaped plots.

**2. Rectangular Layout:**
In a rectangular layout, sprinklers are placed at the corners of a rectangle with sides $S_l$ (lateral spacing along the lateral) and $S_m$ (along the mainline). The spacing is denoted $S_l \times S_m$. This layout is preferred for **rectangular fields** and for **windy conditions** where the spacing must be reduced along the windward direction to maintain uniformity.

**3. Triangular (Equilateral) Layout:**
In a triangular layout, sprinklers are placed at the vertices of equilateral triangles of side $S$. Adjacent rows are staggered by $S/2$. The spacing is denoted $S \times S \times S$. This layout gives the **highest pattern efficiency** because of the staggered overlap geometry, and is preferred where **uniformity is critical** and wind conditions are mild.

**Conditions of preference:**
- Square → regular fields, calm wind, ease of operation
- Rectangular → rectangular plots, moderate wind
- Triangular → highest uniformity required, calm wind, irregular fields

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{9.5 + 11.0 + 10.2 + 12.5 + 8.8 + 10.0 + 11.5 + 9.0 + 10.5 + 9.0}{10}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{102.0}{10} = 10.2 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute absolute deviations $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$:**

| $i$ | $x_i$ (mm) | $\vert x_i - 10.2 \vert$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 9.5  | 0.7 |
| 2 | 11.0 | 0.8 |
| 3 | 10.2 | 0.0 |
| 4 | 12.5 | 2.3 |
| 5 | 8.8  | 1.4 |
| 6 | 10.0 | 0.2 |
| 7 | 11.5 | 1.3 |
| 8 | 9.0  | 1.2 |
| 9 | 10.5 | 0.3 |
| 10 | 9.0 | 1.2 |

**Step 3 — Sum of absolute deviations:**

$$\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert = 0.7 + 0.8 + 0.0 + 2.3 + 1.4 + 0.2 + 1.3 + 1.2 + 0.3 + 1.2 = 9.4 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply CUC formula:**

$$CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{9.4}{10 \times 10.2}\right) = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{9.4}{102}\right)$$

$$CUC = 100 \times (1 - 0.0922) = 100 \times 0.9078$$

$$\boxed{CUC = 90.78\%}$$

**Comment:** Since $CUC = 90.78\% > 85\%$, the system is **excellently designed** and qualifies for NABARD-class certification.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Modified)] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A sprinkler has a wetted diameter $D = 20 \; m$. Sprinklers are placed in (i) a **square layout** with $S = 16 \; m$, and (ii) a **triangular layout** with $S = 18 \; m$.
For each case, compute the **spacing ratio** and the **pattern efficiency** (use the standard PE table). Compare the two layouts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure for conducting a **field evaluation test** of a sprinkler system. List the parameters measured and discuss their significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Case (i) — Square Layout, S = 16 m:**

$$K_1 = \frac{S}{D} = \frac{16}{20} = 0.80$$

From standard PE table (square):
- At $K = 0.7 \Rightarrow PE = 96\%$
- At $K = 0.8 \Rightarrow PE = 92\%$

$$PE_1 = 92\% \; \text{(direct read, K = 0.80)}$$

**Case (ii) — Triangular Layout, S = 18 m:**

$$K_2 = \frac{S}{D} = \frac{18}{20} = 0.90$$

From standard PE table (triangular):
- At $K = 0.8 \Rightarrow PE = 95\%$
- At $K = 0.9 \Rightarrow PE = 91\%$

$$PE_2 = 91\% \; \text{(direct read, K = 0.90)}$$

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Square Layout | Triangular Layout |
|---|---|---|
| Spacing $S$ (m) | 16 | 18 |
| Spacing ratio $K$ | 0.80 | 0.90 |
| Pattern efficiency $PE$ (%) | 92 | 91 |

**Inference:** Both layouts yield nearly equivalent pattern efficiency (~91–92%). However, the **triangular layout uses a larger spacing** (18 m vs 16 m), which means **fewer sprinklers per hectare** and hence **lower capital cost** for the same uniformity. The triangular layout is therefore **economically preferable** in this case.

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Field Evaluation Procedure:**

1. **Setup:** Install 4 sprinklers at the corners of a square (or as per design layout) on a level field. Place catch-cans (collection containers of standard size, typically 100 mm diameter) on a **grid** between the sprinklers. The grid spacing is usually 2 m × 2 m or 3 m × 3 m.

2. **Operation:** Run the system at the **design operating pressure** for a fixed duration (e.g., 30 minutes to 1 hour). Record the pressure using a pressure gauge fitted near the sprinkler nozzle.

3. **Measurement:** Measure the depth of water collected in each catch-can using a graduated cylinder or measuring jar. Record all readings $x_i$ in mm.

4. **Calculation:** Compute the **mean depth** $\bar{x}$ and the **deviations** $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$. Apply the **CUC formula** to obtain the uniformity coefficient.

5. **Wind and Pressure Correction:** Note the wind speed and direction during the test. Check that operating pressure is within $\pm 10\%$ of the design pressure.

**Parameters Measured and Their Significance:**

- **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CUC):** Indicates the **overall uniformity** of water application. A value $\geq 85\%$ is desirable.
- **Pattern Efficiency (PE):** Indicates the **geometric efficiency** of the spacing pattern. Should be $\geq 90\%$ for good design.
- **Distribution Uniformity (DU):** Ratio of the **lower-quarter average** depth to the **overall average** depth. $DU \geq 75\%$ is acceptable.
- **Application Rate (mm/hr):** Must be **less than the soil infiltration rate** to prevent runoff and erosion.
- **Operating Pressure:** Should match design value; deviations cause non-uniform droplet size and throw radius reduction.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in this Topic (mark deductions):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in CUC:** Mixing mm and cm in catch-can readings → -1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting absolute value bars** $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ → entire formula becomes algebraically wrong.
> 3. **Confusing Pattern Efficiency (PE) with Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CUC):** PE is geometry-based (from standard chart), CUC is field-measured (computed). Examiners deduct 2 marks for interchange.
> 4. **Skipping the comment on quality of design** after computing CUC. Always state whether CUC > 85% is acceptable per IS 7911.
> 5. **Not specifying layout type** (square/triangular) before reading PE from table. Examiner expects explicit mention of "Square layout with S/D = 0.8".
> 6. **Wrong spacing ratio formula:** Some students use $K = D/S$ instead of $K = S/D$. This is a fatal conceptual error costing 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CUC Formula:** $CUC = 100 \left(1 - \frac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n\bar{x}}\right)$ — never forget the absolute value.
- **PE depends only on geometry** ($K = S/D$ and layout type), not on pressure or wind.
- **Triangular layout > Square layout** in pattern efficiency by 8–10% for the same $K$.
- **Acceptable CUC** for KTU 2024 certified systems: **$\geq 85\%$**.
- **Acceptable DU (Distribution Uniformity):** $\geq 75\%$.
- **Recommended $K$ values:** $K = 0.8$ to $1.2$ for square; $K = 1.0$ to $1.3$ for triangular.
- **Spacing ratio $K$** must be selected based on **wind speed** — higher wind demands lower $K$.
- **Application rate** must be less than **soil infiltration rate** to prevent runoff.
- **Christiansen (1942)** developed the CUC formula — historically important for KTU theory questions.
- **PE chart values** must be memorized for: $K = 0.5, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0, 1.1, 1.2, 1.3$.
- **For rectangular layouts**, the equivalent $K$ is computed using the geometric mean: $K_{eq} = \sqrt{K_l \times K_m}$.
- **Field test grid spacing** for catch-cans is typically 2 m × 2 m or 3 m × 3 m.
- **Operating pressure tolerance:** $\pm 10\%$ of design pressure is acceptable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Pump Capacity from Head and Discharge, Selection and Installation of Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Determination of Pump Capacity from Head and Discharge, Selection and Installation of Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pump Capacity — Formal Definition

In the context of **Irrigation and Drainage Engineering (PBAGT504)**, the **Pump Capacity** of an irrigation pump is defined as the volume rate of water (discharge) that the pump can deliver against a specified total dynamic head, while operating at its **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** under standard atmospheric conditions (typically **at sea level with atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar = 10.33 m of water**).

$$Q_{\text{pump}} = \frac{\text{Volume of water delivered}}{\text{Time}} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s or L/s or m}^3\text{/h)}$$

The capacity is intrinsically linked to two primary hydraulic parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard KTU Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge (Volume flow rate) | $Q$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\text{L/s}$, or $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ |
| Total Head (Energy per unit weight) | $H$ | meters of water column (m) |
| Power required | $P$ | kW or HP (1 HP = 0.7457 kW) |
| Efficiency of pump | $\eta$ | dimensionless (%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pump capacity is NOT just discharge. It is the unique combination of $(Q, H)$ that the pump can deliver reliably under design conditions. A pump selected on $Q$ alone without considering $H$ will fail on the field.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are filling buckets of water from a deep well to irrigate a farm. Three things determine whether you can do this efficiently:

1. **How big is the bucket and how fast can you fill it?** → This is the **Discharge ($Q$)**.
2. **How deep is the well, and how high do you need to lift the water?** → This is the **Head ($H$)**.
3. **How strong are you, and how much energy do you burn per bucket?** → This is the **Power ($P$)** and **Efficiency ($\eta$)**.

A *centrifugal pump* is simply a mechanical "bucket filler" — but instead of one bucket, it lifts continuous water through a rotating impeller. The **pump capacity** is the *rated combination* of bucket size (discharge) and lift height (head) for which the pump was designed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Hook:** Think of the pump as a delivery truck. Pump capacity = how much load (Q) it can carry, and Head = how steep the climb is. A truck loaded heavily on a flat road is easy; the same load uphill requires a much bigger engine (more power). Same applies to pumps!

## 1.3 Why "Head" Instead of Pressure?

In KTU board problems, students often confuse **head** (in meters) with **pressure** (in $\text{N/m}^2$ or Pascal). The relationship is:

$$H = \frac{p}{\rho g}$$

where $p$ = pressure, $\rho$ = density of water ($1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$), and $g$ = $9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. Engineers prefer head because it is **independent of fluid density** in clean water systems and can be directly added across pump, pipe, and elevation components.

## 1.4 Classification of Pumps for Irrigation (KTU Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, the **Centrifugal Pump** (especially the **submersible** and **monoblock** variants) is the most commonly tested category in irrigation contexts.

| Type | Best Suited For | Typical Use in Irrigation |
|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal (Single-stage) | Low-to-medium head, high discharge | Canal lift schemes, surface water pumping |
| Centrifugal (Multi-stage) | High head, moderate discharge | Deep tube wells, borewells |
| Submersible Pump | Deep borewells, high head | Groundwater irrigation |
| Axial/Propeller Pump | Very high discharge, very low head | River lift, large canal pumping stations |
| Jet Pump | Moderate head, shallow wells | Small farms, domestic + farm use |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Pump Power Equations

The water power delivered by a pump (output hydraulic power) is the product of discharge and total head, weighted by the specific weight of water:

$$P_{\text{water}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

For practical irrigation engineering, using $\gamma_w = \rho g = 9810\ \text{N/m}^3$:

$$P_{\text{water}} = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H \quad \text{(kW when } Q \text{ in m}^3/\text{s)}$$

If discharge is expressed in **L/s**, the formula is:

$$P_{\text{water}} = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000} \quad \text{(kW when } Q \text{ in L/s)}$$

And the **shaft power (input power required at the pump coupling)** is:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{\text{pump}}}$$

The **electrical input power** to the motor is:

$$P_{\text{electric}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_{\text{motor}}}$$

## 2.2 Total Head (H) — The KTU Working Equation

The Total Dynamic Head (TDH) a pump must overcome is the sum of:

$$H_{\text{total}} = H_s + H_d + \frac{v^2}{2g} + H_f$$

where:
- $H_s$ = **Static suction head / suction lift** (m) — vertical distance from water source to pump centerline (negative if pump is above water)
- $H_d$ = **Static delivery head / static discharge head** (m) — vertical distance from pump centerline to delivery point
- $\dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ = **Velocity head** at the discharge point (usually small, often neglected)
- $H_f$ = **Frictional head loss** in suction and delivery pipes (m)

$$H_f = \frac{4 f L v^2}{2 g d} + \Sigma \text{minor losses}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Head** does not depend on flow rate. **Friction Head** depends on $Q^2$ (since $v \propto Q$ for fixed pipe diameter). Hence **Total Head varies with discharge** — this is critical for pump selection.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{water}} = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic (water) power output in W |
| 2 | $P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_p}$ | Brake power input to pump shaft (W) |
| 3 | $\eta_p = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{\text{shaft}}}$ | Pump efficiency (typically 60–85%) |
| 4 | $P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_t}$ | Electric motor input; $\eta_t$ = transmission efficiency (belt = 0.95–0.98) |
| 5 | $H_s = \text{water level} - \text{pump datum}$ | Negative if pump is above water (suction lift) |
| 6 | $H_f = \dfrac{4 f L v^2}{2 g d}$ | Darcy-Weisbach friction loss |
| 7 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed — used to classify pump type |
| 8 | $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ | Pump affinity laws (varying speed $N$) |
| 9 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Affinity law for discharge |
| 10 | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity law for head |
| 11 | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Affinity law for power |
| 12 | NPSH$_a$ > NPSH$_r$ | Net Positive Suction Head available > required (avoid cavitation) |

## 2.4 Pump Affinity Laws — Why They Matter

When the rotational speed of a centrifugal pump is changed from $N_1$ to $N_2$ (or when impeller is trimmed), the discharge, head, and power scale according to the **affinity laws**. These laws are essential for KTU problems involving:

- Variable speed operation (VFD drives)
- Impeller trimming/diameter changes
- Plotting new characteristic curves from test data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Affinity laws apply to **dynamic similarity** of geometrically similar impellers. They do NOT apply if the pump has cavitation, operates far from BEP, or has variable geometry unlike the original.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Determining pump capacity from head and discharge is the foundation for:

- **Lift Irrigation Schemes** (e.g., Jayakwadi, Nagarjuna Sagar lift projects) — where water is pumped from rivers to elevated canals.
- **Tube Well Irrigation** — submersible pumps in alluvial aquifers of Punjab, UP, and Kerala's laterite zones.
- **Drainage Engineering** — sump pumps for land drainage, dewatering pumps for construction sites.
- **Solar Pump Programs (PM-KUSUM)** — sizing pumps for off-grid solar arrays.
- **Micro-irrigation systems** — matching pump pressure to drip/sprinkler requirements.

The selection and installation phase ensures the pump operates within its safe envelope, has adequate NPSH margin, uses correct pipe sizing, and incorporates non-return valves, foot valves, and priming arrangements.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Water Power and Pump Efficiency

### Step 1 — Definition of work done by the pump
Consider a pump that lifts a volume $V$ of water through a vertical height $H$ against gravity in time $t$. The work done by the pump on the water is:

$$W = F \times H = (\text{mass} \times g) \times H = (\rho V) g H$$

### Step 2 — Convert to power (work per unit time)
Divide both sides by time $t$:

$$P_{\text{water}} = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{\rho V g H}{t} = \rho g \left(\frac{V}{t}\right) H$$

Recognizing $Q = V/t$:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{water}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to kW (using $\rho g = 9810\ \text{N/m}^3$ and $Q$ in m³/s)

$$P_{\text{water}} = 9810 \cdot Q \cdot H \quad \text{(W)}$$

$$P_{\text{water}} = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H \quad \text{(kW, with } Q \text{ in m}^3/\text{s)}$$

### Step 4 — When $Q$ is given in L/s, convert:

Since $1\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 1000\ \text{L/s}$, we have:

$$P_{\text{water}} = 9.81 \cdot \frac{Q_{(\text{L/s})}}{1000} \cdot H = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000}\ \text{kW}$$

### Step 5 — Include pump efficiency to get shaft power
By definition, $\eta_p = \dfrac{\text{Output (water) power}}{\text{Input (shaft) power}}$, so:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_p} = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_p}\ \text{kW}}$$

### Step 6 — Include motor + transmission efficiency for electric input

$$P_{\text{electric input}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_p \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_t}\ \text{kW}$$

This is the **billing kW** that the electricity board will record — directly relevant in KTU irrigation economics problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of Affinity Laws (Geometric Similarity)

Assume a geometrically similar impeller operates at two speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$ with discharges $Q_1, Q_2$, heads $H_1, H_2$, and powers $P_1, P_2$.

From dimensional analysis and Euler's turbomachinery equation:

$$g H = U_2 C_{2\theta} = (\pi D N)^2 \times \text{(velocity triangle coefficient)}$$

Since $U \propto D N$:

$$H \propto D^2 N^2 \quad \text{and} \quad Q \propto D^3 N \quad \text{and} \quad P \propto D^5 N^3$$

For the same impeller ($D_1 = D_2$), these reduce to:

$$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}, \qquad \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2, \qquad \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

For **impeller trimming** at constant $N$, diameter changes yield similar proportions with $N$ replaced by $D$.

## 3.3 Worked Example (Numerical — KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump delivers 30 L/s of water against a total head of 25 m. The pump efficiency is 70% and the motor efficiency is 85%. Find:
> (a) Water power output
> (b) Shaft power input to pump
> (c) Electric power input to motor
> (d) HP rating of motor required (with 20% safety factor)

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $Q = 30\ \text{L/s} = 0.030\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H = 25\ \text{m}$
- $\eta_p = 0.70$
- $\eta_m = 0.85$
- Safety factor = 20%

### Part (a): Water power

$$P_{\text{water}} = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H = 9.81 \times 0.030 \times 25$$

$$P_{\text{water}} = 9.81 \times 0.75 = 7.3575\ \text{kW}$$

### Part (b): Shaft power

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{water}}}{\eta_p} = \frac{7.3575}{0.70} = 10.51\ \text{kW}$$

### Part (c): Electric input power

$$P_{\text{electric}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_m} = \frac{10.51}{0.85} = 12.36\ \text{kW}$$

### Part (d): Motor HP with safety factor

$$P_{\text{motor required}} = 12.36 \times 1.20 = 14.83\ \text{kW}$$

Convert to HP ($1\ \text{HP} = 0.7457\ \text{kW}$):

$$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{14.83}{0.7457} \approx 19.89\ \text{HP}$$

**Standard next size = 20 HP motor.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Note:** KTU examiners often test whether students convert units correctly. The three most common errors are: (1) forgetting to convert L/s to m³/s, (2) omitting motor/transmission efficiency, and (3) forgetting the safety factor in motor selection.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pump Selection Decision Table

| Selection Parameter | Centrifugal (Radial) | Mixed Flow | Axial Flow | Submersible |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Discharge range | $0.001$–$0.5\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $0.05$–$5\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $>1\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $0.005$–$0.2\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Head range | $10$–$150\ \text{m}$ | $5$–$40\ \text{m}$ | $<10\ \text{m}$ | $50$–$350\ \text{m}$ |
| Specific speed $N_s$ | $10$–$80$ | $80$–$300$ | $300$–$1500$ | $20$–$100$ |
| Typical efficiency | $60$–$80\%$ | $75$–$85\%$ | $80$–$90\%$ | $65$–$80\%$ |
| NPSH requirement | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| Application in irrigation | Lift from canals | River intake | Low-head river lift | Deep borewells |

The **specific speed** $N_s$ uniquely identifies the pump type:

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

where $Q$ is in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $H$ in m, and $N$ in rpm.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Determining Pump Capacity from Head & Discharge

The **system curve** of an irrigation scheme plots $H_{\text{total}}$ vs $Q$:

$$H_{\text{system}} = H_{\text{static}} + k Q^2$$

where $k$ is the **system resistance coefficient** that lumps together friction and minor losses for the pipe network. The **pump characteristic curve** is supplied by the manufacturer as $H_{\text{pump}}$ vs $Q$.

**Pump Capacity** at the **Operating Point** is the intersection of these two curves. The design rule is:

> The operating point should lie within $\pm 10\%$ of the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** on the pump curve, otherwise:
> - Efficiency drops
> - Vibration increases
> - Bearing life reduces
> - Cavitation may occur

For **parallel pumps** ($n$ identical pumps in parallel): $Q_{\text{total}} = n \cdot Q_1$ at the same head.
For **series pumps**: $H_{\text{total}} = n \cdot H_1$ at the same discharge.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Pump Capacity Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Irrigation Requirement] --> B[Determine Crop Water Demand ETc]
    B --> C[Estimate Design Discharge Q]
    C --> D[Survey Site: Suction and Delivery Levels]
    D --> E[Compute Static Head Hs plus Hd]
    E --> F[Compute Friction and Minor Losses Hf]
    F --> G[Total Dynamic Head H equals Hs plus Hd plus Hf]
    G --> H[Plot System Curve H vs Q]
    H --> I[Consult Manufacturer Pump Curves]
    I --> J[Locate Intersection: Operating Point]
    J --> K{Operating Point within BEP Range}
    K -- Yes --> L[Select Pump: Match Q and H]
    K -- No --> M[Adjust: Change Impeller, Speed, or Use Parallel or Series]
    M --> I
    L --> N[Compute Shaft Power P equals rho g Q H over eta]
    N --> O[Select Motor HP with Safety Factor]
    O --> P[Verify NPSH availability and margin]
    P --> Q[Final Pump Selection Complete]
    Q --> R[Proceed to Installation Design]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Standard Pump Installation Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Water Source: Well, River, Sump] -->|Suction Pipe| FV[Foot Valve with Strainer]
    FV -->|With Priming| P[Pump Casing and Impeller]
    P -->|NRV Check Valve| DCV[Delivery Control Valve]
    DCV -->|Pressure Gauge| PP[Delivery Pipeline to Field]
    PP -->|Flow Meter| IRR[Irrigation Field or Sprinkler or Drip]
    P --- MS[Motor Coupled via Flexible Coupling or Belt]
    MS --- EL[Electrical Supply and Starter Panel]
    P -.->|Vent and Air Release| ARV[Air Release Valve at Highest Point]
    PP -.->|Pressure Relief| PRV[Pressure Relief Valve]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Pump Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input: Required Q and H] --> S2[Compute Specific Speed Ns]
    S2 --> S3{Ns value}
    S3 -->|10 to 80| R1[Radial / Centrifugal Pump]
    S3 -->|80 to 300| R2[Mixed Flow Pump]
    S3 -->|300 to 1500| R3[Axial / Propeller Pump]
    R1 --> C1[Verify Suction Lift Limit approx 6 to 7 m]
    R2 --> C2[Verify Cavitation Margin NPSH]
    R3 --> C3[Verify Submergence Requirement]
    C1 --> SEL[Final Selection from Catalogue]
    C2 --> SEL
    C3 --> SEL
    SEL --> INST[Installation Plan: Foundation, Alignment, Pipework, Valves]
    INST --> TEST[Commissioning: Trial Run, Performance Test]
```

## 4.4 Pump Curve vs System Curve — Operating Point Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYS[System Curve: H equals Hstatic plus kQ squared] --> OP
    PMP[Pump Curve: H decreases with Q] --> OP
    OP[Intersection equals Operating Point] --> OPT[Optimal if within BEP range]
    OPT --> EFF[Check Efficiency at Operating Point]
    EFF --> NPSH[Verify NPSH margin greater than 1 m]
    NPSH --> OK[Selection Approved]
    EFF --> BAD{Outside BEP range}
    BAD -->|Above BEP Q| FIX1[Trim Impeller or Reduce Speed]
    BAD -->|Below BEP Q| FIX2[Add Pump in Parallel]
    FIX1 --> OP
    FIX2 --> OP
```

## 4.5 Installation Considerations Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Preparation] --> B[Foundation: RCC, Vibration Isolators]
    B --> C[Pump and Motor Alignment: Laser or Dial Gauge]
    C --> D[Suction Piping: Short, Straight, No Air Pockets]
    D --> E[Delivery Piping: NRV, Gate Valve, Pressure Gauge]
    E --> F[Priming Arrangement: Foot Valve or Vacuum Pump]
    F --> G[Electrical: Starter, Overload, Earthling, Cabling]
    G --> H[Testing: No-Load, Load Test, Vibration Check]
    H --> I[Hand Over and Documentation]
```
