# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Total Dynamic Head" (TDH) of a centrifugal pump used in an irrigation system. List its components.**

**Model Answer:**
Total Dynamic Head is the total energy per unit weight that the pump must impart to water to move it from the suction source to the delivery point, expressed in meters of water column.

$$H_{\text{TDH}} = H_s + H_d + \frac{v^2}{2g} + H_f$$

Components:
- **$H_s$**: Static suction head/lift (vertical distance from water level to pump centerline)
- **$H_d$**: Static delivery head (vertical distance from pump centerline to delivery point)
- **$\frac{v^2}{2g}$**: Velocity head at discharge
- **$H_f$**: Frictional and minor head losses in suction and delivery pipes

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "Best Efficiency Point" (BEP) of a pump? Why should the operating point be close to BEP?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the discharge value on the pump characteristic curve at which the pump operates with maximum efficiency. Operating close to BEP ensures:
- Maximum energy utilization
- Minimum vibration and noise
- Longer bearing and seal life
- Reduced risk of cavitation
- Stable hydraulic performance

The KTU board norm is to allow the operating point within **$\pm 10\%$ of BEP discharge** for safe, efficient operation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> **(a)** A centrifugal pump is required to deliver 50 L/s of water against a total head of 30 m. The overall efficiency of the pump is 75% and the electric motor efficiency is 88%. Determine:
> - **(i)** Water power output
> - **(ii)** Shaft power input
> - **(iii)** Electric input power
> - **(iv)** Suitable motor rating in HP, assuming a 15% safety factor
>
> **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a centrifugal pump. State the affinity laws applicable to centrifugal pumps.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $Q = 50\ \text{L/s} = 0.050\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H = 30\ \text{m}$
- $\eta_p = 0.75$
- $\eta_m = 0.88$
- Safety factor = 15% (i.e., multiplier = 1.15)

**(i) Water power output:**

$$P_{\text{water}} = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H = 9.81 \times 0.050 \times 30$$

$$P_{\text{water}} = 9.81 \times 1.5 = 14.715\ \text{kW}$$

**(ii) Shaft power input:**

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{water}}}{\eta_p} = \frac{14.715}{0.75} = 19.62\ \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Electric input power:**

$$P_{\text{electric}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_m} = \frac{19.62}{0.88} = 22.30\ \text{kW}$$

**(iv) Motor rating with 15% safety factor:**

$$P_{\text{motor design}} = 22.30 \times 1.15 = 25.64\ \text{kW}$$

Convert to HP:

$$P_{\text{HP}} = \frac{25.64}{0.7457} \approx 34.39\ \text{HP}$$

**Recommended standard motor size = 35 HP (next higher standard rating).**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Working Principle of Centrifugal Pump:**
A centrifugal pump uses a rotating **impeller** to convert the kinetic energy of fluid into pressure energy. Water enters the impeller eye at the center (suction side) and is flung outward by centrifugal force into the volute casing, which then converts this velocity into pressure before discharge.

The sequence is:
1. **Priming** — suction pipe and casing filled with water (no air).
2. **Rotation** — impeller rotates at high speed.
3. **Centrifugal action** — water gains velocity as it moves outward.
4. **Conversion** — volute casing decelerates water, converting KE to pressure energy.
5. **Discharge** — pressurized water delivered through the delivery pipe.

**Affinity Laws:**

$$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}, \qquad \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2, \qquad \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

These apply to geometrically similar impellers and are valid only for the same pump operating in dynamic similarity, with no cavitation or operating far from BEP.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a)** A deep-borewell submersible pump must lift water from a depth of 90 m below ground level to a surface storage tank whose water level is 8 m above ground. The discharge required is 8 L/s. The suction pipe is 50 mm diameter, 5 m long; the delivery pipe is 75 mm diameter, 100 m long. Assume $f = 0.02$, neglect minor losses, and pump efficiency = 70%. Determine:
> - **(i)** Static head
> - **(ii)** Friction head loss
> - **(iii)** Total dynamic head
> - **(iv)** Pump shaft power required
>
> **(b)** List and explain the criteria for selection of an irrigation pump. Discuss the role of NPSH in preventing cavitation.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Suction depth (static lift) = 90 m (water level below pump by 90 m, since pump is submerged)
- Delivery static lift = 8 m
- Total static head = 90 + 8 = **98 m** (pump to tank)
- $Q = 8\ \text{L/s} = 0.008\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Suction pipe: $d_s = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.050\ \text{m}$, $L_s = 5\ \text{m}$
- Delivery pipe: $d_d = 75\ \text{mm} = 0.075\ \text{m}$, $L_d = 100\ \text{m}$
- $f = 0.02$
- $\eta_p = 0.70$

**(i) Static head:**

$$H_{\text{static}} = 90 + 8 = 98\ \text{m}$$

**(ii) Friction head loss:**

Using Darcy-Weisbach: $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L v^2}{2 g d}$

Velocity in suction pipe:

$$v_s = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{0.008}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2} = \frac{0.008}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.074\ \text{m/s}$$

Velocity in delivery pipe:

$$v_d = \frac{Q}{A_d} = \frac{0.008}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2} = \frac{0.008}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.811\ \text{m/s}$$

Suction friction loss:

$$h_{f,s} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 5 \times (4.074)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.050} = \frac{0.4 \times 16.60}{0.981} = \frac{6.641}{0.981} \approx 6.77\ \text{m}$$

Delivery friction loss:

$$h_{f,d} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 100 \times (1.811)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075} = \frac{8 \times 3.279}{1.4715} = \frac{26.234}{1.4715} \approx 17.83\ \text{m}$$

Total friction loss:

$$H_f = 6.77 + 17.83 = 24.60\ \text{m}$$

**(iii) Total dynamic head:**

$$H_{\text{TDH}} = H_{\text{static}} + H_f = 98 + 24.60 = 122.60\ \text{m}$$

**(iv) Pump shaft power:**

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_p} = \frac{9.81 \times 0.008 \times 122.60}{0.70} = \frac{9.62}{0.70} \approx 13.74\ \text{kW}$$

**Convert to HP:**

$$P_{\text{shaft, HP}} = \frac{13.74}{0.7457} \approx 18.43\ \text{HP}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Selection Criteria for Irrigation Pump:**

1. **Discharge (Q) Match:** Pump rated Q should be ≥ design Q with margin of 10–15%.
2. **Head (H) Match:** Pump H must equal TDH (static + friction) at the design Q, with operating point on pump curve.
3. **Specific Speed (Ns):** Determines pump type — radial, mixed, or axial.
4. **NPSH Margin:** Available NPSH must exceed required NPSH by ≥ 1 m.
5. **Efficiency:** Higher BEP efficiency = lower operating cost.
6. **Speed & Power Supply:** Compatibility with available electric supply (415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz).
7. **Material of Construction:** Cast iron for clean water, bronze/SS for corrosive/saline irrigation water.
8. **Reliability & After-sales Service:** Important for rural irrigation in Kerala.
9. **Cost:** Capital cost vs running cost (lifetime cost analysis).

**Role of NPSH in Cavitation Prevention:**

**NPSH** stands for **Net Positive Suction Head**. It is the absolute pressure head at the pump suction above the vapor pressure of water.

- **NPSH$_a$ (Available):** Determined by site conditions — atmospheric pressure + static suction head − vapor pressure − suction friction.
- **NPSH$_r$ (Required):** Specified by the pump manufacturer for each model.

If NPSH$_a$ < NPSH$_r$, the water vaporizes at the impeller eye, forming vapor bubbles that collapse violently — this is **cavitation**. Cavitation causes pitting, noise, vibration, efficiency loss, and eventual impeller destruction.

**Design Rule:**

$$\text{NPSH}_a \geq \text{NPSH}_r + 1\ \text{m (safety margin)}$$

For submersible pumps (set below water), NPSH$_a$ is naturally high because of positive submergence — another reason submersible pumps are favored for deep borewells.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Pump Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (2–3 marks lost):** Forgetting to convert L/s to m³/s. Always write the conversion step explicitly.
>
> 2. **Ignoring Motor Efficiency (1 mark):** If motor efficiency is given in the problem, you MUST include it. If you skip it, even with the right water power, you lose marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Friction Losses (2–3 marks):** When a problem provides pipe length, diameter, and $f$, friction MUST be calculated. Students often take the "given head" as static only.
>
> 4. **No Safety Factor on Motor (1 mark):** Always apply 10–20% safety factor when selecting motor HP, and round UP to next standard rating.
>
> 5. **Affinity Law Misuse:** Affinity laws apply only for geometrically similar impellers — students often apply them between different pump models, which is wrong.
>
> 6. **NPSH Ignored:** Cavation-related failures cost marks. Mention NPSH always when discussing pump installation.
>
> 7. **Sketch Missing:** In 14-mark questions, a labeled diagram of the pump + system is worth 1–2 marks by itself. Always draw it.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pump Capacity** is uniquely defined by **discharge ($Q$)** and **head ($H$)** simultaneously — never by $Q$ alone.
- **Water Power:** $P_w = \rho g Q H = 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H$ (kW, with $Q$ in m³/s).
- **Shaft Power:** $P_{\text{shaft}} = P_w / \eta_p$.
- **Electric Input Power:** $P_{\text{elec}} = P_{\text{shaft}} / (\eta_m \cdot \eta_t)$.
- **TDH:** $H_{\text{total}} = H_s + H_d + v^2/2g + H_f$.
- **Friction loss (Darcy-Weisbach):** $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L v^2}{2 g d}$.
- **Affinity laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- **Specific Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ (in SI units).
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Operating point should be within $\pm 10\%$ of BEP.
- **NPSH Rule:** NPSH$_a > $ NPSH$_r + 1\ \text{m}$.
- **Submersible pumps:** Best for high head + low discharge (deep borewells).
- **Centrifugal pumps:** Best for low-to-medium head + moderate-to-high discharge (lift schemes).
- **Axial pumps:** Best for very high discharge + very low head (river lift).
- **Motor HP Rating:** Always round up to next standard size after applying safety factor.
- **Standard Conversion:** $1\ \text{HP} = 0.7457\ \text{kW}$; $1\ \text{kW} = 1.341\ \text{HP}$.
- **Installation essentials:** priming, foot valve, NRV, pressure gauge, flow meter, foundation, alignment, earthing, and safety valves.
- **Operating Point = Pump Curve ∩ System Curve** — must lie within BEP region.
- **Parallel operation:** Same head, $Q$ adds up.
- **Series operation:** Same discharge, $H$ adds up.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agricultural Drainage - Factors Affecting Drainage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Agricultural Drainage - Factors Affecting Drainage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Agricultural Drainage:** Agricultural drainage is the engineered removal and disposal of excess surface water (surface drainage) and excess subsurface water (sub-surface drainage) from agricultural lands to maintain a favorable soil-water-air environment conducive to crop growth, prevent waterlogging and soil salinization, and sustain agricultural productivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Factors Affecting Drainage:** These are the physical, climatic, soil, hydrological, geological, crop-based, and anthropogenic conditions that govern the **necessity**, **design**, **spacing**, **depth**, and **capacity** of an agricultural drainage system.

### Classification of Factors Affecting Drainage

The need for drainage and the design of a drainage system is influenced by a complex interplay of factors. These can be systematically classified as:

| **S.No** | **Factor Category** | **Governing Parameters** |
|:--------:|:--------------------|:--------------------------|
| 1 | Climatic Factors | Rainfall, Temperature, ET, Humidity |
| 2 | Soil Factors | Texture, Structure, Permeability, Porosity |
| 3 | Topographic Factors | Land slope, Surface elevation, Micro-topography |
| 4 | Groundwater Factors | Water table depth, Aquifer properties, Gradient |
| 5 | Crop Factors | Root depth, Crop tolerance, Water requirements |
| 6 | Geological / Sub-Surface Factors | Stratification, Hardpan, Bedrock depth |
| 7 | Human / Management Factors | Irrigation method, Land use, Seepage |

### Conceptual Analogy — The Plant's "Lungs and Plumbing"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine the soil as a sponge holding the root zone of a plant. If the sponge is over-saturated (waterlogged), the plant literally "drowns" because oxygen cannot reach the roots — the soil pores are filled with water instead of air. Agricultural drainage acts like a **plumbing and ventilation system** for this sponge. The *factors affecting drainage* are precisely the conditions that determine how quickly the sponge fills up (rainfall, irrigation, seepage), how easily water moves through it (permeability, texture, structure), and how urgently the plant needs the water removed (crop tolerance, water table depth). A good drainage design is one that customizes the plumbing based on **all** these factors together.

### Why Factors Matter in KTU Context

In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 4 emphasizes that **no two fields are drained identically**. A coastal clayey field in Kuttanad and a sandy lateritic field in Wayanad require fundamentally different drainage designs because their *factors affecting drainage* differ dramatically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Climatic Factors

Climate is the **primary driver** of drainage need. The balance between water input and water loss determines whether excess water accumulates.

#### (a) Rainfall
- **Intensity** ($I$, **mm/hr**): High-intensity storms exceed infiltration capacity → surface runoff & surface drainage need.
- **Duration** ($t$, **hours**): Long-duration rainfall saturates the root zone → sub-surface drainage need.
- **Distribution**: Monsoon-concentrated rainfall (as in Kerala) creates peak drainage demand in **June–September**.
- **Annual Rainfall** ($P$, **mm**): Higher annual rainfall ⇒ greater drainage requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — Drainage Coefficient ($D_c$):** The depth of water to be removed in **24 hours** from the drainage area, expressed in **mm/day** or **cm/day**. It is **directly proportional to rainfall intensity**.

#### (b) Temperature & Humidity
- Higher temperatures → higher **evapotranspiration (ET)** → natural removal of excess water.
- High humidity reduces ET, worsening drainage needs.

#### (c) Wind Velocity
- Affects ET rate and thus the natural drying of soil.

### 2.2 Soil Factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soil properties are the MOST CRITICAL factor group in drainage design.** They control how water enters, moves through, and is held by the soil profile.

#### (a) Soil Texture
The relative proportion of sand, silt, and clay determines pore-size distribution:

| **Soil Texture** | **Drainage Rate** | **Drainage Need** |
|:-----------------|:------------------|:------------------|
| Sandy soil | Very fast | Low (natural drainage sufficient) |
| Loam | Moderate | Moderate |
| Clay | Very slow | Very High (artificial drainage essential) |

#### (b) Soil Structure
- Granular / crumb structure → high macroporosity → good drainage.
- Platy / massive structure → low macroporosity → poor drainage.

#### (c) Soil Permeability (Hydraulic Conductivity, $K$)
The single most important soil property for drainage design.

$$K = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot h} \quad \text{(from Darcy's Law)}$$

Where:
- $K$ = Hydraulic conductivity (**mm/hr** or **m/day**)
- $Q$ = Discharge (**m³/s** or **cm³/s**)
- $L$ = Length of soil column (**m**)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area (**m²**)
- $h$ = Head difference (**m**)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *permeability* (ability to transmit water) with *infiltration* (entry of water into soil). They are related but distinct — permeability is a soil property; infiltration is a process.

#### (d) Porosity & Effective Porosity
- **Total porosity** ($n$): Ratio of total void volume to total volume.
- **Effective porosity** ($n_e$): Ratio of drainable pore volume to total volume. This is used in drainage design.

$$n_e = \frac{V_{drainable}}{V_{total}}$$

#### (e) Soil Profile & Depth
- Shallow soils over impervious layers (hardpan, bedrock) restrict vertical drainage → horizontal drainage needed.

### 2.3 Topographic Factors

- **Land Slope** ($S$, **m/m** or **%**): Influences the velocity of surface runoff.
- **Flat lands** (slope < **0.5%**): Surface drainage becomes difficult; sub-surface drainage preferred.
- **Steep lands**: Risk of erosion; surface drainage with proper gradient.
- **Micro-topography**: Depressions and low spots become waterlogged pockets.

### 2.4 Groundwater (Water Table) Factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Table Depth ($d$, m):** The vertical distance from the soil surface to the saturated zone. This is the **MASTER variable** for sub-surface drainage design.

| **Water Table Depth** | **Effect** | **Drainage Need** |
|:----------------------|:-----------|:------------------|
| > **1.5 m** | Aerated root zone | None |
| **0.6 – 1.5 m** | Marginal aeration | Preventive drainage |
| < **0.6 m** | Severe waterlogging | Intensive drainage |

- **Water Table Fluctuations**: Seasonal rise during monsoon, recession in summer.
- **Hydraulic Gradient** ($i$): Drives lateral flow toward drains.

### 2.5 Crop Factors

Different crops have different tolerance to waterlogging and salinity:

| **Crop** | **Root Depth (m)** | **Waterlogging Tolerance** |
|:---------|:-------------------|:---------------------------|
| Rice | 0.3 – 0.6 | Very High (actually requires flooded conditions) |
| Wheat | 1.0 – 1.5 | Low |
| Coconut | 2.0 – 3.0 | Moderate |
| Vegetables | 0.3 – 0.6 | Very Low |
| Tea / Coffee | 1.5 – 2.5 | Low |

### 2.6 Geological / Sub-Surface Factors

- **Stratification**: Layered soils with alternating permeable and impervious strata drastically affect lateral flow paths.
- **Hardpan / Claypan**: Restricts vertical percolation → lateral drains needed.
- **Bedrock depth**: Determines feasibility of deep open drains.

### 2.7 Human / Management Factors

- **Seepage from canals** (a major issue in command areas).
- **Over-irrigation** in command areas (e.g., Malabar region).
- **Defective irrigation practices**: Poor leveling, broken field channels.
- **Land use changes**: Paddy-to-coconut conversion alters drainage dynamics.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Application** | **Units** |
|:------------|:---------------|:----------------|:----------|
| Darcy's Law | $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | Subsurface flow | m³/s |
| Hydraulic Conductivity | $K = \dfrac{QL}{Ah}$ | Soil permeability | m/day |
| Drainage Coefficient | $D_c = \dfrac{\text{Depth of water to be removed in 24 hrs}}{1 \text{ day}}$ | Design discharge | mm/day |
| Flow per unit width (Hooghoudt) | $q = \dfrac{4K_m(2d_m^2 + s^2)}{s}$ | Drain spacing (steady state) | m²/day |
| Specific Yield / Effective Porosity | $n_e = \dfrac{V_{drained}}{V_{total}}$ | Storage capacity | dimensionless |
| Drainage Discharge | $Q_d = D_c \cdot A$ | Pipe / channel sizing | m³/s |
| Reynolds Number (drain flow) | $Re = \dfrac{vD}{\nu}$ | Flow regime check | dimensionless |
| Hydraulic Gradient | $i = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}$ | Driving head | m/m |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

These factors collectively determine:
- **Whether drainage is needed at all** (water table, rainfall, crop tolerance).
- **Type of drainage system** (surface vs. sub-surface).
- **Drain depth and spacing** (permeability, soil texture).
- **Drain capacity / pipe diameter** (drainage coefficient).
- **Cost-benefit feasibility** of the drainage intervention.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Darcy's Law (The Foundation of Sub-Surface Drainage)

Darcy's Law is the empirical relationship governing laminar flow of water through saturated porous media. It is the **cornerstone equation** of all sub-surface drainage design.

#### Experimental Setup (Conceptual)
A soil column of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is saturated with water. A constant head $h$ is maintained across the column, and the outflow discharge $Q$ is measured.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the volumetric discharge per unit time as $Q$.

**Step 2:** Observe empirically that $Q$ is proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$Q \propto A$$

**Step 3:** Observe that $Q$ is proportional to the head difference $h$:

$$Q \propto h$$

**Step 4:** Observe that $Q$ is inversely proportional to the length $L$ of the soil column:

$$Q \propto \frac{1}{L}$$

**Step 5:** Combine the proportionalities:

$$Q \propto A \cdot \frac{h}{L}$$

**Step 6:** Introduce the proportionality constant — the **hydraulic conductivity** $K$:

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{h}{L}$$

**Step 7:** Define the hydraulic gradient $i$:

$$i = \frac{h}{L}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute $i$ into the equation to obtain **Darcy's Law**:

$$\boxed{Q = K \cdot i \cdot A}$$

**Step 9:** The **Darcy velocity** (flux, or specific discharge) is obtained by dividing both sides by $A$:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = K \cdot i$$

This $v$ is the **apparent velocity**, not the true seepage velocity. The true seepage velocity $v_s$ accounts for porosity:

$$v_s = \frac{v}{n_e} = \frac{K \cdot i}{n_e}$$

Where $n_e$ is the **effective porosity**.

#### Validation of Units

$$[Q] = [K] \cdot [i] \cdot [A] = \left(\frac{m}{day}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{m}{m}\right) \cdot (m^2) = m^3/day$$

Units check passes. ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Drain Discharge from Drainage Coefficient

**Step 1:** Define drainage coefficient $D_c$:

$$D_c = \frac{\text{Depth of water to be removed in 24 hours}}{\text{1 day}}$$

**Step 2:** Express $D_c$ in consistent units, e.g., **m/day**.

**Step 3:** Consider a drainage area $A$ (m²). The volume of water to be removed per day is:

$$V = D_c \cdot A$$

**Step 4:** Convert volume per day to discharge per second:

$$Q_d = \frac{V}{86400} = \frac{D_c \cdot A}{86400}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{Q_d = \frac{D_c \cdot A}{86400} \quad \text{(in m}^3\text{/s, when } D_c \text{ in m/day and } A \text{ in m}^2)}$$

**Numerical Example:**

Given:
- $D_c$ = **10 mm/day** = **0.01 m/day**
- Area $A$ = **5 hectares** = **50,000 m²**

Step-by-step solution:

$$Q_d = \frac{0.01 \times 50000}{86400}$$

$$Q_d = \frac{500}{86400}$$

$$Q_d = 5.787 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_d \approx 5.79 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Factor Affects Drainage Design Parameters

This matrix shows the **cause-and-effect** linkage from each factor to the design output. This is high-yield for KTU exam answers.

| **Factor** | **Affects Which Design Parameter?** | **Direction of Effect** |
|:-----------|:------------------------------------|:------------------------|
| High Rainfall Intensity ($I$) | Drainage Coefficient ($D_c$) | $\uparrow I \Rightarrow \uparrow D_c$ |
| Low Hydraulic Conductivity ($K$) | Drain Spacing ($s$) | $\downarrow K \Rightarrow \downarrow s$ (closer drains) |
| Shallow Water Table | Drain Depth | $\uparrow$ Need for deeper drains |
| Clayey Soil Texture | Drain Type | Surface + Sub-surface drains required |
| Steep Land Slope | Surface Drain Design | $\uparrow$ Velocity, needs erosion control |
| Crop Waterlogging Tolerance | Critical Water Table Depth | High tolerance → shallow drains acceptable |
| Stratified Soil Profile | Effective Hydraulic Conductivity | $K_{eff}$ = weighted average of strata |
| Canal Seepage | Drainage Need in Command Areas | Adds extra inflow to drain design |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Effect of Soil Texture on Hydraulic Conductivity

**Given:** Two fields with the following textures, both with $i = 0.02$ and $A = 100 \text{ m}^2$.

| **Field** | **Texture** | **$K$ (m/day)** |
|:----------|:------------|:----------------|
| A | Sandy Loam | **1.5** |
| B | Clay | **0.05** |

Compute discharge $Q$ for both fields.

**Solution for Field A:**

$$Q_A = K_A \cdot i \cdot A = 1.5 \times 0.02 \times 100$$

$$Q_A = 3.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Solution for Field B:**

$$Q_B = K_B \cdot i \cdot A = 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 100$$

$$Q_B = 0.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{Q_A}{Q_B} = \frac{3.0}{0.1} = 30$$

**Interpretation:** The sandy loam transmits water **30 times faster** than the clay. Therefore, clay soils need **denser drain spacing** and **higher drainage investment**. This is why drainage is a critical issue in clay-dominated regions like Kuttanad.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** List any **four** soil factors affecting agricultural drainage.

**Model Answer:**

The four major soil factors affecting agricultural drainage are:

1. **Soil Texture** — Determines pore size distribution. Clayey soils have tiny pores → poor drainage; sandy soils have large pores → good drainage.

2. **Soil Structure** — Granular/crumb structures promote macroporosity and better drainage; platy/massive structures impede drainage.

3. **Hydraulic Conductivity (K)** — Governs the rate at which water moves through soil. Low $K$ (clay) requires closer drain spacing.

4. **Effective Porosity ($n_e$)** — Determines the volume of drainable water stored in the soil profile. Higher $n_e$ means more water available for removal by drains.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "soil texture" and "soil structure." Remember — texture is the *particle size distribution*; structure is the *arrangement of particles into aggregates*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define **drainage coefficient** and state its typical units.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The drainage coefficient ($D_c$) is defined as the **depth of water (in mm or cm) that must be removed from the drainage area in 24 hours** to maintain a water-table depth suitable for crop growth.

**Typical Units:** **mm/day** or **cm/day**.

**Significance:** $D_c$ is used to compute the design discharge of the drain:

$$Q_d = \frac{D_c \cdot A}{86400} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}$$

For agricultural lands in humid regions like Kerala, typical $D_c$ values range from **5 to 15 mm/day**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified for Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **climatic and soil factors** that affect agricultural drainage design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A drainage area of **8 hectares** has a drainage coefficient of **12 mm/day**. Compute the design discharge of the main drain in **litres per second (L/s)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Climatic and Soil Factors (7 marks)**

**Climatic Factors:**

1. **Rainfall** — The most important climatic factor. High intensity and prolonged duration rainfall saturate the root zone and raise the water table, increasing the drainage demand. The drainage coefficient is directly derived from peak rainfall events.

2. **Temperature** — Higher temperatures accelerate evaporation and ET, providing natural removal of excess water.

3. **Humidity** — High atmospheric humidity reduces ET, worsening waterlogging.

4. **Evapotranspiration (ET)** — The natural counterbalance to rainfall and irrigation. Low ET in monsoon season amplifies drainage needs.

5. **Wind Velocity** — Influences ET and surface drying.

**Soil Factors:**

1. **Soil Texture** — Clay soils have very small pores → low permeability → water moves slowly → drains must be closely spaced. Sandy soils have large pores → high permeability → natural drainage suffices.

2. **Soil Structure** — Well-aggregated (granular) soils allow rapid drainage; massive or platy structures impede it.

3. **Hydraulic Conductivity ($K$)** — The single most important design parameter. Determines drain spacing using Hooghoudt's equation:

$$s^2 = \frac{4K_m \cdot d_m^2}{q} \quad \text{(simplified form)}$$

4. **Effective Porosity ($n_e$)** — Used to compute the volume of drainable water.

5. **Soil Profile Depth and Stratification** — A shallow impervious layer (hardpan) prevents vertical percolation → lateral sub-surface drains become necessary.

---

**(b) Design Discharge Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Area $A$ = 8 hectares = **80,000 m²**
- Drainage Coefficient $D_c$ = 12 mm/day = **0.012 m/day**

**Step 1:** Compute volume of water to be removed per day:

$$V = D_c \cdot A = 0.012 \times 80000$$

$$V = 960 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to discharge per second:

$$Q_d = \frac{V}{86400} = \frac{960}{86400}$$

$$Q_d = 0.01111 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to litres per second (1 m³/s = 1000 L/s):

$$Q_d = 0.01111 \times 1000 = 11.11 \text{ L/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_d \approx 11.11 \text{ L/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert **mm to m** OR forget the conversion **86400 seconds/day**. A common error is writing $Q_d = D_c \cdot A$ directly in L/s, which gives an incorrect dimensional answer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **groundwater factors and crop factors** in deciding the type and depth of drainage system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two fields have the following soil properties with the same hydraulic gradient $i = 0.015$ and same area $A = 200 \text{ m}^2$. Compute the discharge for both fields and comment on the drainage implication. **(7 marks)**

| **Field** | **Hydraulic Conductivity $K$ (m/day)** |
|:----------|:---------------------------------------|
| Field I   | 2.0                                    |
| Field II  | 0.08                                   |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Groundwater and Crop Factors (7 marks)**

**Groundwater Factors:**

1. **Water Table Depth ($d$)** — The depth of water table below ground surface is the master variable. For most crops, $d$ must be maintained at **0.6 to 1.2 m** during the growing season. Shallow water tables demand **deeper drains** with closer spacing.

2. **Water Table Fluctuations** — Seasonal variations (rise during monsoon, fall in summer) determine whether drainage is needed **year-round** or only seasonally.

3. **Hydraulic Gradient ($i$)** — Drives lateral flow toward drains. Influences drain spacing significantly.

4. **Aquifer Properties** — Confined vs. unconfined aquifers behave differently under drainage pumping.

**Crop Factors:**

1. **Root Depth** — Deep-rooted crops (coconut, sugarcane) tolerate lower water tables; shallow-rooted crops (rice, vegetables) need higher water tables → deeper drainage.

2. **Waterlogging Tolerance** — Rice tolerates standing water (does not need drainage); wheat, cotton, and most vegetables are highly sensitive (need intensive drainage).

3. **Cropping Season and Water Requirements** — Kharif crops coincide with monsoon → maximum drainage demand. Rabi crops require supplemental irrigation but not heavy drainage.

4. **Salt Tolerance** — In saline soils, drainage must leach salts below the root zone — depth depends on crop salt tolerance.

---

**(b) Discharge Calculation for Two Fields (7 marks)**

**Given:** $i = 0.015$, $A = 200 \text{ m}^2$.

**Field I:** $K_I = 2.0 \text{ m/day}$

$$Q_I = K_I \cdot i \cdot A$$

$$Q_I = 2.0 \times 0.015 \times 200$$

$$Q_I = 6.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Field II:** $K_{II} = 0.08 \text{ m/day}$

$$Q_{II} = K_{II} \cdot i \cdot A$$

$$Q_{II} = 0.08 \times 0.015 \times 200$$

$$Q_{II} = 0.24 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{Q_I}{Q_{II}} = \frac{6.0}{0.24} = 25$$

**Comment on Drainage Implication:**

Field I (sandy soil, $K = 2.0$ m/day) transmits water **25 times faster** than Field II (clayey soil, $K = 0.08$ m/day). Therefore:

- Field I: Natural drainage is **largely adequate**; only surface drainage during peak storms may be needed.
- Field II: Requires **intensive sub-surface drainage** with closely spaced lateral drains (typically **15–30 m spacing**) and possibly pumped drainage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When comparing fields, do not just compute the discharge — always **interpret** what it means for drainage design. Examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for the engineering interpretation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Factors Affecting Drainage**

**A. The 7 Factor Categories (Must Memorize):**
- ✅ **Climatic** — Rainfall, Temperature, Humidity, ET, Wind
- ✅ **Soil** — Texture, Structure, Hydraulic Conductivity ($K$), Effective Porosity ($n_e$), Profile Depth
- ✅ **Topographic** — Slope, Micro-topography, Elevation relative to outlet
- ✅ **Groundwater** — Water Table Depth, Fluctuations, Hydraulic Gradient, Aquifer Properties
- ✅ **Crop** — Root Depth, Waterlogging Tolerance, Cropping Season
- ✅ **Geological** — Stratification, Hardpan, Bedrock Depth
- ✅ **Human** — Irrigation method, Canal seepage, Land use changes

**B. Critical Numerical Values to Remember:**
- ✅ Drainage Coefficient typical range: **5–25 mm/day**
- ✅ Critical Water Table Depth: **0.6 m** (most field crops)
- ✅ Hydraulic Conductivity of Clay: **< 0.1 m/day**
- ✅ Hydraulic Conductivity of Sand: **1–10 m/day**
- ✅ Conversion: **1 hectare = 10,000 m²**
- ✅ Conversion: **1 day = 86,400 seconds**

**C. Must-Know Formulas:**
- ✅ Darcy's Law: $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$
- ✅ Drain Discharge: $Q_d = D_c \cdot A / 86400$
- ✅ Seepage velocity: $v_s = K \cdot i / n_e$

**D. KTU Exam Strategy:**
- ✅ In Part A, list factors with **brief** justification (not just names).
- ✅ In Part B, always **interpret numerical results** — engineering meaning matters.
- ✅ Always include **units** in every numerical answer.
- ✅ Mention **Kuttanad / Kerala-specific** examples wherever possible (e.g., laterite, clay, canal command areas) — KTU examiners reward contextual awareness.

**E. Single Most Important Takeaway:**
> The design of a drainage system is NOT dictated by any single factor but by the **interaction** of all seven factor categories. A holistic assessment — climatic + soil + groundwater + crop + topographic + geological + human — is essential for an optimal and sustainable drainage solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drainable Porosity, Drainage Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drainable Porosity, Drainage Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Drainable Porosity ($\mu$)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Drainable porosity is the volume of water released from a unit volume of saturated soil under the force of gravity per unit drop in the water table. It is a dimensionless soil hydraulic property that quantifies the fraction of the soil's bulk volume that can be emptied by gravity drainage to bring the water table from a higher elevation to a lower one.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\mu = \frac{V_{drained}}{V_{total}}$$

Where:
- $V_{drained}$ = Volume of water released by gravity from saturated soil
- $V_{total}$ = Total bulk volume of the soil sample

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drainable Porosity is NOT Total Porosity!** Total porosity includes all pore space (water removed by drying in oven), while drainable porosity only accounts for water that drains freely under gravity (between saturation and field capacity). The retained water (held by capillary forces) does NOT count as drainable.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **sponge soaked in water** held over a sink. When you let go, water flows out freely for a few seconds, then stops dripping even though the sponge still feels damp. The water that freely dripped out represents the **drainable porosity**, while the dampness still clinging to the sponge pores represents water held by capillary forces (not drainable). For a soil profile, when the water table drops, this same "free drainage" effect happens — only the gravity-mobile water leaves, and that's what we quantify as drainable porosity.

**Typical Range of Drainable Porosity Values:**

| Soil Type | Drainable Porosity ($\mu$) |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Coarse Sand | 0.20 – 0.35 |
| Fine Sand | 0.10 – 0.15 |
| Sandy Loam | 0.08 – 0.12 |
| Loam | 0.05 – 0.10 |
| Clay Loam | 0.03 – 0.07 |
| Clay | 0.01 – 0.05 |
| Peat | 0.05 – 0.15 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Sandy soils have HIGH drainable porosity because their large pores cannot hold water against gravity. Clay soils have LOW drainable porosity because their tiny pores hold water tightly through capillary action, releasing very little under gravity alone.

---

## 1.2 Drainage Coefficient ($q$)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
The drainage coefficient is defined as the depth of water that must be removed from a given area within **24 hours** to maintain the water table at a safe, desired depth below the ground surface. It represents the design rate at which the subsurface drainage system must operate to ensure proper aeration in the crop root zone.

The standard units used in KTU examinations are:
- **mm/day** (most common in Indian/KTU practice)
- **cm/day** (used in some FAO references)

Mathematically expressed as:

$$q = \frac{V_{water \; removed}}{A \times t}$$

Where:
- $V_{water \; removed}$ = Volume of water drained out (m³ or litres)
- $A$ = Drainage area (hectares or m²)
- $t$ = Time period (typically 1 day = 24 hours)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always express the drainage coefficient in **mm/day** (or sometimes **litres/sec/hectare** for pump-based drainage design). Convert using: $1 \; mm/day = 0.1157 \; L/s/ha$ per hectare.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the drainage coefficient as the **"design capacity" of your roof gutter system**. Just as a roof gutter must be sized to handle the heaviest expected rainfall in a day (say, 50 mm/day rainfall intensity), the subsurface drain must be sized to remove the worst-case excess water from the root zone in a day. The drainage coefficient IS that "worst-case daily removal rate" — but for water that has entered the soil profile and must be pulled out via drains.

**Typical Drainage Coefficient Values (KTU/FAO Standards):**

| Region / Crop Type | Drainage Coefficient (mm/day) |
|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| Arid/Semi-arid regions (general crops) | 3 – 6 |
| Humid regions (general crops) | 10 – 25 |
| Sensitive crops (vegetables, sugarcane) | 25 – 50 |
| Rice/paddy fields (special cases) | 5 – 10 |
| Highly permeable sandy soils | 8 – 12 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy:** Higher drainage coefficients mean **closer drain spacing** and **larger drain pipes** — increasing the cost of the subsurface drainage system. Engineers choose a drainage coefficient that balances crop water-table sensitivity against construction economics.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Parameters Are Co-Taught in KTU Module 4

Both parameters appear together in the **unsteady-state (transient) drain spacing equations** developed by Glover, Dumm, and van Schilfgaarde. Drainable porosity governs **how much water** is stored in the soil profile above the water table, while the drainage coefficient dictates **how fast** that water must be removed. Together, they determine the spacing between subsurface drains.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Meaning of Drainable Porosity (Detailed Analysis)

When the water table in a soil profile falls by a small increment $\Delta h$ over a horizontal area $A$:

1. **Step 1 — Pore water release:** The pores in the soil column of height $\Delta h$ above the receding water table begin to empty under gravity. Not all water leaves; only the portion bound loosely to soil particles drains out.

2. **Step 2 — The "Drainable" fraction:** The fraction of water that leaves = **drainable porosity** $\mu$. The remaining water is held by matric suction (capillary forces) and stays in the soil even after gravity drainage stops.

3. **Step 3 — Why this matters for design:** The volume of water stored in the soil profile above the water table (and available to be removed by drains) is directly controlled by $\mu$. A soil with $\mu = 0.10$ releases 10% of its bulk volume as drainable water per unit drop in water table.

4. **Step 4 — Field measurement:** $\mu$ is determined by:
   - Pumping test (large-scale, in-situ)
   - Drainage lysimeter (controlled, slow)
   - Laboratory undisturbed column drainage test

**The "Why" Behind the Parameter:**
Soils behave like a **bank account** for water. When it rains, the account fills up (water table rises). When drains operate, the account withdraws water. The drainable porosity tells you **how big each withdrawal is** when the water table drops a unit distance. Without knowing $\mu$, you cannot convert a desired water-table drop into a required drain discharge.

---

## 2.2 Physical Meaning of Drainage Coefficient (Detailed Analysis)

The drainage coefficient answers the engineering question: **"How fast must the drain remove water to protect the crop?"**

1. **Step 1 — Crop sensitivity sets the upper bound:** Sensitive crops (sugarcane, vegetables) suffer yield loss if the water table stays within 30–50 cm of the surface for more than 24–48 hours. This sets the minimum acceptable removal rate.

2. **Step 2 — Climate sets the water input rate:** In humid Kerala, heavy monsoon rainfall (50–100 mm/day) can rapidly raise the water table. The drain must remove this excess before the next rainfall event. Hence humid regions require higher $q$.

3. **Step 3 — The drainage coefficient links input to output:** If rain/irrigation adds $I$ mm/day to the water table and the drain removes $q$ mm/day, then for a steady water table:
$$q \geq I_{effective}$$

4. **Step 4 — Translating to drain geometry:** Once $q$ is fixed, the spacing $L$ between drains is determined using steady-state (Hooghoudt) or unsteady-state (Glover) equations, depending on whether the water table is allowed to fluctuate or not.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following table consolidates every formula you will need for Part A and Part B questions on this topic. Memorize the boxed equations — they appear in nearly every KTU exam paper.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Drainable Porosity | $\mu = \dfrac{V_{drained}}{V_{total}}$ | Ratio of drainable water volume to total soil volume | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Drainable Porosity (water content form) | $\mu = \theta_{sat} - \theta_{FC}$ | Difference between saturated and field-capacity water content | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Volume of Water Drained per Unit Area | $V_d = \mu \times \Delta h$ | Water released per unit area for water table drop $\Delta h$ | m or mm |
| 4 | Drainage Coefficient | $q = \dfrac{V_{removed}}{A \cdot t}$ | Depth of water removed per day | mm/day |
| 5 | Unit Conversion | $1 \; mm/day = 0.1157 \; L/s/ha$ | Conversion factor for design | — |
| 6 | Time to Drain (Glover Equation, basic form) | $t = \dfrac{F \cdot \mu \cdot L^2}{4 \cdot K \cdot (d_0 - d_t)} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{2d_0}{d_t}\right)$ | Unsteady drawdown between drains | days |
| 7 | Drainable Porosity (from drawdown test) | $\mu = \dfrac{q \cdot t}{\Delta h}$ | Re-arranged storage-release form | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Specific Yield Approximation | $\mu \approx S_y$ | In drainage, drainable porosity $\approx$ specific yield | Dimensionless |

> **Where:** $V_{drained}$ = water drained out, $V_{total}$ = total soil volume, $\theta_{sat}$ = volumetric water content at saturation, $\theta_{FC}$ = volumetric water content at field capacity, $\Delta h$ = water table drop, $L$ = drain spacing, $K$ = hydraulic conductivity, $d_0$ = initial water table depth above drain, $d_t$ = water table depth at time $t$, $F$ = geometry factor (typically 4 for parallel drains).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

**In Agricultural Drainage Design (KTU Core):**
- $\mu$ and $q$ together are the **two most critical inputs** to subsurface drain spacing equations.
- Used in **Command Area Development** projects in Kerala (Kuttanad, Kole lands) where high water tables threaten paddy and coconut cultivation.
- Used in **salinity control** in coastal Karnataka and Tamil Nadu irrigated belts.

**Beyond Agriculture:**
- **Land reclamation** of waterlogged mine spoils and marshlands.
- **Landfill drainage design** — removing leachate from beneath municipal solid waste sites.
- **Road and railway subgrade drainage** — controlling capillary rise into pavement layers.
- **Sports turf and golf course drainage** — maintaining playable conditions.
- **Urban stormwater management** — sizing infiltration basins and underdrains.

> [!NOTE]
> **Career Tip:** Civil engineers working in agricultural consultancy, irrigation departments (Kerala IWTD, CADA), or environmental remediation firms regularly use these parameters. Master them — they are foundational for water-logging mitigation and Command Area Development projects.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drainable Porosity from First Principles

**Statement:** Show that the volume of water drained per unit area when the water table falls by a depth $\Delta h$ equals $\mu \cdot \Delta h$.

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Consider a soil column of unit cross-sectional area $A = 1 \; m^2$.
- The soil profile above the water table has uniform drainable porosity $\mu$.
- The water table drops by an amount $\Delta h$ (in metres).

**Step 1 — Define the bulk volume of soil that is dewatered:**
The volume of soil from which water drains is the volume of the slab of soil above the new water table position:

$$V_{total} = A \times \Delta h = 1 \times \Delta h = \Delta h \; m^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of drainable porosity:**
By definition, drainable porosity is the fraction of bulk volume that drains:

$$\mu = \frac{V_{drained}}{V_{total}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{drained}$:**
Rearranging the equation:

$$V_{drained} = \mu \times V_{total}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 3:**

$$V_{drained} = \mu \times \Delta h$$

**Step 5 — Express depth of water drained per unit area (depth form):**
Since $A = 1 \; m^2$, the volume $V_{drained}$ in m³ is numerically equal to the depth of water in metres. Converting to mm (multiply by 1000):

$$\boxed{d_{drained} = \mu \times \Delta h \times 1000 \; mm}$$

This is the **fundamental storage-release equation** used in every KTU derivation of unsteady-state drainage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Drainage Coefficient from Volume-Rate Definition

**Statement:** Derive the drainage coefficient formula starting from a controlled drainage scenario.

**Scenario:** A farmer has a 2-hectare field. After 100 mm of rainfall in one day, the water table rises dangerously close to the root zone. The subsurface drain is operated for 24 hours and removes a total of 180 m³ of water. Find the drainage coefficient.

**Step 1 — Express the volume removed in mm·ha units:**
Since 1 mm depth over 1 hectare = 10 m³ of water (because 1 ha = 10,000 m², so 1 mm × 10,000 m² = 10 m³):

$$d_{removed} = \frac{V_{removed}}{A \times 10} = \frac{V_{removed}}{A} \times 0.1$$

where $d_{removed}$ is in mm, $V_{removed}$ is in m³, and $A$ is in hectares.

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$d_{removed} = \frac{180 \; m^3}{2 \; ha \times 10} = \frac{180}{20} = 9 \; mm$$

**Step 3 — Express the drainage coefficient:**
The drainage coefficient is the depth removed per day:

$$\boxed{q = \frac{d_{removed}}{t} = \frac{9 \; mm}{1 \; day} = 9 \; mm/day}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to L/s/ha for pump design (if asked):**
Using the conversion factor $1 \; mm/day = 0.1157 \; L/s/ha$:

$$q = 9 \times 0.1157 = 1.041 \; L/s/ha$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between $\mu$ and $q$ via Glover's Equation

**Statement:** Show that in unsteady-state drainage, the time to drain from initial water table $d_0$ to final water table $d_t$ depends on $\mu$ and $q$.

**Starting Principle:**
The volume of water released by the soil (storage decrease) equals the volume of water removed by the drains (outflow).

**Step 1 — Storage decrease (left side):**
For unit area, the volume released when water table falls from $d_0$ to $d_t$ is:

$$V_{released} = \mu \times (d_0 - d_t)$$

**Step 2 — Outflow from drains (right side):**
Over a time interval $t$, with drainage coefficient $q$:

$$V_{outflow} = q \times t$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two (mass balance for falling water table):**

$$\mu \times (d_0 - d_t) = q \times t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\mu$ (useful for field determination of $\mu$):**

$$\mu = \frac{q \cdot t}{d_0 - d_t}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $t$ (useful for design):**

$$t = \frac{\mu \cdot (d_0 - d_t)}{q}$$

**Step 6 — Verification with units:**
- $\mu$ = dimensionless
- $(d_0 - d_t)$ = m (or mm)
- $q$ = mm/day
- $t$ = days (correct!)

**Numerical Example:**
Given: $\mu = 0.08$, $d_0 = 1.0 \; m$ (initial water table 1.0 m above drain), $d_t = 0.4 \; m$ (final water table 0.4 m above drain), $q = 8 \; mm/day = 0.008 \; m/day$.

$$t = \frac{0.08 \times (1.0 - 0.4)}{0.008} = \frac{0.08 \times 0.6}{0.008} = \frac{0.048}{0.008} = 6 \; days$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The mass balance in Step 3 ($\mu \times \Delta h = q \times t$) is the **single most important equation** combining both parameters. KTU examiners love testing this directly in numerical problems.

---

## 3.4 Extended Form: Glover-Dumm Equation (Reference Derivation)

For more advanced designs, the time to drain a soil profile between parallel drains (spacing $L$, hydraulic conductivity $K$, drainable porosity $\mu$) is given by Glover's equation:

$$t = \frac{F \cdot \mu \cdot L^2}{4 \cdot K \cdot (d_0 - d_t)} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2 \cdot d_0}{d_t}\right)$$

**Derivation Outline (for KTU conceptual understanding):**

1. **Step A — Start with Boussinesq's unsteady groundwater flow equation** for a confined/unconfined aquifer:
$$\mu \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = K \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(h \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right)$$

2. **Step B — Apply the Dupuit-Forchheimer assumption** (horizontal flow, no vertical gradient):
The equation reduces to a non-linear parabolic PDE.

3. **Step C — Linearize** by substituting $u = h^2$ and assume a sinusoidal initial water table shape between drains.

4. **Step D — Solve with boundary conditions:**
- $h(x=0) = 0$ (at drain)
- $h(x=L) = 0$ (at adjacent drain)
- Initial condition: $h(x, t=0) = d_0$ (uniform initial water table)

5. **Step E — Obtain the exponential decay solution** for the midpoint water table:
$$d_t = d_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{4 \cdot K \cdot t \cdot \pi^2}{\mu \cdot L^2}\right)$$

6. **Step F — Re-arrange to obtain $t$ in terms of $d_0, d_t$:**

$$\boxed{t = \frac{\mu \cdot L^2}{4 \pi^2 \cdot K} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{d_0}{d_t}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $F$ in the original Glover equation absorbs $\pi^2$ and other geometric corrections. For KTU, the simplified mass-balance form in Section 3.3 is sufficient for numerical problem solving.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mass Balance Closure Between $\mu$ and $q$

The core engineering relationship between drainable porosity and drainage coefficient is captured in this mass-balance block.

