# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]
**Define drainable porosity. How is it different from total porosity? Mention the typical range for clay and sandy soils.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Drainable porosity ($\mu$) is defined as the ratio of the volume of water that drains out of a saturated soil mass under the force of gravity to the total volume of the soil mass. Mathematically:

$$\mu = \frac{V_{drained \; by \; gravity}}{V_{total}}$$

**Difference from Total Porosity (1 Mark):**
Total porosity represents the entire void space in the soil, including water held by capillary forces (which can only be removed by evaporation or oven drying). Drainable porosity, on the other hand, accounts only for the water that is released by gravity drainage — i.e., the water occupying the larger pores (macropores) that cannot retain water against gravity.

**Typical Range (0.5 Mark):**
- **Sandy soils:** $\mu = 0.10$ to $0.20$ (high drainable porosity due to large pores)
- **Clay soils:** $\mu = 0.01$ to $0.05$ (low drainable porosity due to small, capillary-dominated pores)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**What is drainage coefficient? State its typical values for humid and arid regions. How is it expressed in SI units?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
The drainage coefficient ($q$) is defined as the depth of water to be removed from a drainage area in 24 hours to maintain the water table at the desired depth below the ground surface. It represents the design discharge rate of the subsurface drainage system.

$$q = \frac{\text{Volume of water removed per unit area}}{\text{Time (24 hours)}}$$

**Typical Values (1 Mark):**
- **Arid regions:** $q = 3$ to $6 \; mm/day$
- **Humid regions:** $q = 10$ to $25 \; mm/day$
- **Sensitive crops:** $q = 25$ to $50 \; mm/day$

**SI Expression (0.5 Mark):**
The drainage coefficient is commonly expressed in **mm/day** (preferred in Indian practice) or in **litres/second/hectare (L/s/ha)** for pump-based drainage design. The conversion is:

$$1 \; mm/day = 0.1157 \; L/s/ha$$

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse drainable porosity with specific retention.** Specific retention is the water RETAINED after drainage (complement of drainable porosity in saturated conditions). Many students write them as synonyms — this costs full marks.
> - **Always state units of $q$ explicitly** (mm/day is the KTU default; avoid cm/day unless asked).
> - **In numerical problems, convert all depths to consistent units (m or mm) BEFORE substituting** into equations. A common error: mixing metres and millimetres in Glover's equation.
> - **Show the mass balance $\mu \cdot \Delta h = q \cdot t$ explicitly** in derivations; this is the gold-standard 2-mark step that examiners hunt for.
> - **Mention "field capacity" and "saturation"** as the boundary water contents when defining $\mu$ — examiners reward this conceptual linkage.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model Paper]

**(a)** Define drainable porosity and drainage coefficient with suitable units. Derive the relationship between them for an unconfined falling water table condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a subsurface drainage project, a sandy loam soil has a drainable porosity of 0.10. The initial water table is at 1.5 m depth and must be lowered to 0.5 m depth in 8 days. Calculate the required drainage coefficient. If the hydraulic conductivity of the soil is $K = 1.2 \; m/day$ and drains are installed at 1.0 m depth with spacing of 30 m, verify whether the time to drain satisfies Glover's criterion. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Define drainable porosity (1.5 Marks):**
Drainable porosity $\mu$ is the ratio of the volume of water drained out of saturated soil under gravity to the total soil volume. It is dimensionless.

$$\mu = \frac{V_{drained}}{V_{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Define drainage coefficient (1.5 Marks):**
Drainage coefficient $q$ is the depth of water removed per unit area per day from the drainage system, typically expressed in mm/day.

$$q = \frac{V_{removed}}{A \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — Mass balance derivation (3 Marks):**

Consider a unit area of soil with initial water table at depth $d_0$ above the drain. After time $t$, the water table falls to depth $d_t$.

Volume of water released (per unit area) from the soil column:

$$V_{released} = \mu \cdot (d_0 - d_t)$$

Volume of water removed by the drain (per unit area) in time $t$:

$$V_{removed} = q \cdot t$$

By conservation of mass (released water = removed water):

$$\mu \cdot (d_0 - d_t) = q \cdot t$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{q = \frac{\mu \cdot (d_0 - d_t)}{t} \quad \text{or} \quad t = \frac{\mu \cdot (d_0 - d_t)}{q}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering significance (1 Mark):**
This relationship forms the basis of all unsteady-state drain spacing equations. It links the soil's storage capacity ($\mu$) to the system's removal capacity ($q$), enabling the engineer to size drains based on desired water-table drawdown time.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.10$
- $d_0 = 1.5 \; m$ (initial water table above drain, taking drain at 1.0 m depth, water table at 0.5 m depth)

Wait — re-reading: "initial water table is at 1.5 m depth and must be lowered to 0.5 m depth."

Let me reinterpret. The drain is at 1.0 m depth. So:
- Initial water table above drain: $d_0 = 1.5 - 1.0 = 0.5 \; m$
- Final water table above drain: $d_t = 0.5 - 1.0 = -0.5 \; m$ ... 

**Re-interpretation:** The depths given are absolute water table depths from ground surface. For Glover's equation, we need water table heights ABOVE the drain. Since the drain is at 1.0 m depth:

- Initial water table above drain: $d_0 = 1.5 - 1.0 = 0.5 \; m$
- Final water table above drain: $d_t = 0.5 - 1.0 = -0.5 \; m$ (final water table is BELOW the drain)

This gives a negative $d_t$, which is physically impossible for the standard Glover form. The problem likely intends:

- $d_0 = 1.5 \; m$ above the drain (drain is at some reference depth; the "1.5 m" and "0.5 m" are measured ABOVE the drain axis).
- $d_t = 0.5 \; m$ above the drain.

**Step 1 — Calculate the required drainage coefficient (3 Marks):**

Using the mass balance equation derived in Part (a):

$$q = \frac{\mu \cdot (d_0 - d_t)}{t} = \frac{0.10 \times (1.5 - 0.5)}{8 \; days}$$

$$q = \frac{0.10 \times 1.0}{8} = \frac{0.10}{8} = 0.0125 \; m/day$$

Converting to mm/day:

$$\boxed{q = 12.5 \; mm/day}$$

**Step 2 — Glover's criterion verification (4 Marks):**

Glover's equation for the time to drain between parallel drains:

$$t = \frac{F \cdot \mu \cdot L^2}{4 \cdot K \cdot (d_0 - d_t)} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2 d_0}{d_t}\right)$$

For parallel drains, $F = 4$.

Given:
- $F = 4$
- $\mu = 0.10$
- $L = 30 \; m$
- $K = 1.2 \; m/day$
- $d_0 = 1.5 \; m$
- $d_t = 0.5 \; m$

**Substitute into Glover's equation:**

$$t = \frac{4 \times 0.10 \times (30)^2}{4 \times 1.2 \times (1.5 - 0.5)} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2 \times 1.5}{0.5}\right)$$

$$t = \frac{4 \times 0.10 \times 900}{4 \times 1.2 \times 1.0} \cdot \ln(6)$$

$$t = \frac{360}{4.8} \cdot \ln(6)$$

$$t = 75 \times 1.7918$$

$$t = 134.4 \; days$$

**Step 3 — Verification and conclusion (1 Mark):**

The Glover's equation predicts a drain time of **134.4 days** to lower the water table from 1.5 m to 0.5 m above the drain with 30 m spacing. However, the required time is only **8 days**. 

**Conclusion:** The 30 m drain spacing is **NOT adequate** for the required 8-day drainage. The spacing must be reduced (or a higher drainage coefficient must be used) to meet the design criterion. A smaller spacing would reduce $t$ proportionally to $L^2$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of drainable porosity using a soil-water characteristic. How does it relate to the difference between water content at saturation and field capacity? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 5-hectare agricultural field in the humid region of Kerala requires a drainage system. The design drainage coefficient is fixed at 20 mm/day. The soil is a clay loam with drainable porosity of 0.06. Calculate:
   (i) The volume of water to be removed per day from the entire field in m³.
   (ii) The water table drop expected in 5 days if no further recharge occurs.
   (iii) The drain discharge in L/s/ha for pump selection. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Concept of Drainable Porosity (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Soil-Water Characteristic (2 Marks):**
The soil-water characteristic curve (SWCC) plots volumetric water content ($\theta$) against soil water suction (matric potential, $\psi$). At saturation ($\psi = 0$), the water content is $\theta_{sat}$. As suction increases, water drains from progressively smaller pores.

**Step 2 — Key Points on the SWCC (2 Marks):**
- At $\psi = 0$ (saturation): $\theta = \theta_{sat}$ (all pores filled with water)
- At $\psi \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$ atm (field capacity): $\theta = \theta_{FC}$ (gravitational drainage ceases)
- The water held between $\psi = 0$ and $\psi = 0.3$ atm is the **drainable water**.

**Step 3 — Relationship (2 Marks):**

$$\boxed{\mu = \theta_{sat} - \theta_{FC}}$$

This equation expresses drainable porosity as the **difference between volumetric water content at saturation and at field capacity**. It represents the volume of water per unit bulk volume that is released by gravity drainage.

**Step 4 — Engineering Implication (1 Mark):**
A high $\theta_{sat} - \theta_{FC}$ difference (sandy soils) means more water is removed per unit drop in water table. A low difference (clayey soils) means slower drainage and longer drain spacing requirements for the same drainage target.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Area $A = 5 \; hectares$
- Drainage coefficient $q = 20 \; mm/day$
- Drainable porosity $\mu = 0.06$
- Time $t = 5 \; days$

**Part (i) — Volume of water to be removed per day (2 Marks):**

Volume per day = Drainage coefficient × Area

Since $1 \; mm$ depth over $1 \; ha = 10 \; m^3$:

$$V_{day} = q \times A \times 10 = 20 \; mm/day \times 5 \; ha \times 10 \; m^3/(mm \cdot ha)$$

$$\boxed{V_{day} = 1000 \; m^3/day}$$

**Part (ii) — Water table drop in 5 days (3 Marks):**

Using the mass balance equation:

$$\mu \times \Delta h = q \times t$$

$$\Delta h = \frac{q \cdot t}{\mu} = \frac{20 \; mm/day \times 5 \; days}{0.06}$$

$$\Delta h = \frac{100 \; mm}{0.06} = 1666.67 \; mm = 1.667 \; m$$

$$\boxed{\Delta h \approx 1.67 \; m}$$

**Part (iii) — Drain discharge in L/s/ha (2 Marks):**

Using the conversion factor $1 \; mm/day = 0.1157 \; L/s/ha$:

$$q = 20 \times 0.1157 = 2.314 \; L/s/ha$$

$$\boxed{q = 2.31 \; L/s/ha \; (approximately)}$$

**Verification check:** Total discharge from 5 ha:

$$Q_{total} = 2.314 \times 5 = 11.57 \; L/s$$

In one day: $11.57 \times 86400 \; s = 999,648 \; L \approx 1000 \; m^3$ ✓ (matches Part i)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- **Drainable porosity ($\mu$):** Volume of water released by gravity per unit volume of soil. **Dimensionless.** Typical: Sand $\approx 0.10$–$0.20$; Clay $\approx 0.01$–$0.05$.

- **Drainage coefficient ($q$):** Depth of water removed per day per unit area. **Units: mm/day** (KTU standard) or L/s/ha. Typical: Arid $3$–$6$ mm/day; Humid $10$–$25$ mm/day.

- **Critical distinction:** Drainable porosity is NOT total porosity. It is the **specific yield** equivalent in drainage engineering — water that ACTUALLY drains under gravity.

- **Mass balance equation (THE most tested formula):**
$$\mu \cdot (d_0 - d_t) = q \cdot t$$

- **Field capacity form:**
$$\mu = \theta_{sat} - \theta_{FC}$$

- **Volume conversion:** $1 \; mm \; \text{over} \; 1 \; ha = 10 \; m^3$ of water.

- **Discharge conversion:** $1 \; mm/day = 0.1157 \; L/s/ha$ (remember this — it appears in numerical problems).

- **Glover's equation (unsteady-state):**
$$t = \frac{\mu \cdot L^2}{4 \pi^2 \cdot K} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{d_0}{d_t}\right)$$

- **Order-of-magnitude check:** Higher $K$ and lower $\mu$ → faster drainage. Higher $L$ (drain spacing) → slower drainage ($L^2$ dependence!).

- **Typical exam traps:**
  1. Confusing $\mu$ with effective porosity or specific retention.
  2. Mixing units (m with mm) in Glover's equation.
  3. Forgetting the $\ln$ term in unsteady-state drain time calculations.
  4. Using drain spacing as drain diameter (frequent in poorly-read questions).

- **Kerala-specific application:** Kuttanad and Kole land drainage projects routinely use $\mu = 0.04$–$0.08$ (clay loam, alluvial) and $q = 15$–$25$ mm/day for paddy-coconut systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Flow through Soils, Darcy's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT504 - IRRIGATION AND DRAINAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Flow through Soils, Darcy's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Flow Through Soils

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> *Flow through soils* refers to the movement of water (or any fluid) through the interconnected void spaces (pores) of a soil mass under the influence of hydraulic head differences. In irrigation and drainage engineering, this phenomenon is governed by **Darcy's Law**, which states that the *discharge velocity* of flow through a saturated soil mass is **directly proportional** to the *hydraulic gradient*.

Mathematically, the foundational relationship is expressed as:

$$v = K \cdot i$$

where **$v$** is the discharge velocity (also called *Darcy flux* or *specific discharge*), **$K$** is the *hydraulic conductivity* of the soil, and **$i$** is the *hydraulic gradient* (dimensionless, ratio of head loss to flow length).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: A Simple Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Coffee Filter Analogy"** ☕
> Imagine pouring water through a column of fine sand held in a transparent tube. The water does *not* move instantly — it percolates slowly through the tiny gaps between sand grains. If you pour *faster* (greater head difference), the water flows *faster* through the sand. If you use *finer* sand (smaller pores), the water flows *slower*. This is exactly the principle behind **Darcy's Law**: flow rate depends on the *driving force* (head difference) and the *resistance* of the soil (hydraulic conductivity).

Another helpful analogy is the **"Kitchen Sponge"**:
- A dry sponge has air in its pores — water cannot flow until pores are saturated.
- A wet sponge allows water to drip out the bottom — this is *gravity-driven saturated flow* governed by Darcy's Law.
- A *very* wet sponge with large connected pores lets water pass easily — this corresponds to a *coarse* soil with **high hydraulic conductivity**.

## 1.3 Types of Soil Water

In soil science, the water occupying pore spaces is classified based on the energy with which it is held:

| Form of Soil Water | Energy State | Availability to Plants | Drainage Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gravitational Water** | Free energy (drains under gravity) | Available but transient | Primary concern in drainage design |
| **Capillary Water** | Held by surface tension | Available to plants | Critical for moisture retention |
| **Hygroscopic Water** | Strongly adsorbed to soil grains | Unavailable to plants | Negligible in drainage analysis |
| **Combined Water** | Chemically bound | Unavailable | Not considered in flow analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:**
> In **drainage engineering**, the analysis of subsurface flow almost exclusively deals with **gravitational water** that moves through saturated or unsaturated zones above or below the water table. The movement is fundamentally governed by **Darcy's Law**.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Units

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Practical Range in Soils |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic conductivity | $K$ | m/s (or cm/s) | $10^{-1}$ (gravel) to $10^{-9}$ (clay) |
| Hydraulic gradient | $i$ | dimensionless | 0 to >10 |
| Discharge velocity | $v$ | m/s | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-9}$ m/s |
| Porosity | $n$ | dimensionless | 0.25 (sand) to 0.70 (clay) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Basis of Darcy's Law

In **1856**, French engineer **Henry Darcy** (based on his work on water filtration through sand filters for the city of Dijon, France) experimentally established the relationship that bears his name. The law emerges from the following logical reasoning:

**Step 1 — Driving Force Identification**
Water moves through soil only when there is a *difference in total hydraulic head* between two points. The total head **$h$** at any point is the sum of three components:

$$h = h_e + h_p + h_v$$

where:
- **$h_e$** = elevation head (gravitational potential)
- **$h_p$** = pressure head (pore water pressure, $\frac{u}{\gamma_w}$)
- **$h_v$** = velocity head (negligible in soil flow due to very low velocities)

**Step 2 — Gradient Definition**
The driving force per unit length of flow path is the *hydraulic gradient*:

$$i = -\frac{dh}{dL}$$

The negative sign indicates that head *decreases* in the direction of flow.

**Step 3 — Proportionality Principle**
Through experiments with saturated sand columns of varying lengths and head differences, Darcy observed that the volumetric discharge **$Q$** through a soil cross-section is:

1. **Directly proportional** to the cross-sectional area **$A$** of the soil sample.
2. **Directly proportional** to the head difference **$\Delta h$**.
3. **Inversely proportional** to the length **$L$** of the flow path.

Combining these observations yields the **classic Darcy equation**:

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot i = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta h}{L}$$

Dividing both sides by $A$ gives the **discharge velocity form**:

$$v = K \cdot i$$

## 2.2 Discharge Velocity vs. Seepage Velocity

A critical conceptual distinction that examiners frequently test:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discharge Velocity ($v$ or $v_d$):**
> This is a *fictitious* velocity based on the *total* cross-sectional area of the soil. Water does not actually flow through solid grains, so the real velocity in the pores must be higher.

> **Seepage Velocity ($v_s$ or $v_a$):**
> This is the *actual* velocity of water through the void spaces only.

The relationship is governed by the **continuity of flow**:

$$v \cdot A = v_s \cdot A_v$$

where $A_v = n \cdot A$ is the actual void area (with $n$ being porosity). Therefore:

$$v_s = \frac{v}{n}$$

## 2.3 Intrinsic Permeability

Hydraulic conductivity **$K$** depends on *both* the porous medium *and* the flowing fluid. To isolate the property of the medium alone, the **intrinsic permeability** $k$ is defined:

$$k = \frac{K \cdot \mu}{\gamma_w} = \frac{K \cdot \nu}{g}$$

where:
- **$\mu$** = dynamic viscosity of the fluid (N·s/m²)
- **$\nu$** = kinematic viscosity of the fluid (m²/s)
- **$\gamma_w$** = unit weight of water (N/m³)
- **$g$** = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

This relationship is essential when analyzing flow of fluids *other* than water (e.g., oil contamination, geothermal studies).

## 2.4 Validity of Darcy's Law

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Validity Limits**
> Darcy's Law is a **linear** relationship valid **only** when flow is **laminar**. Outside this range, deviations occur.

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number ($Re$) | Validity of Darcy's Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar flow** | $Re \leq 1$ (or $Re \leq 10$ by some authors) | **Valid** ✓ |
| **Transitional** | $1 < Re < 100$ | **Partially valid** (with caution) |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 100$ | **Invalid** ✗ |

The Reynolds number for flow through porous media is defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_{10}}{\mu}$$

where $d_{10}$ is the **effective grain size** (10% of grains by weight are smaller).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | Darcy's Law (volumetric form) | m³/s |
| 2 | $v = K \cdot i$ | Darcy's Law (velocity form) | m/s |
| 3 | $i = \frac{\Delta h}{L}$ | Hydraulic gradient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $v_s = \frac{v}{n}$ | Seepage velocity from discharge velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $K = k \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu} = k \cdot \frac{g}{\nu}$ | Hydraulic conductivity from intrinsic permeability | m/s |
| 6 | $h = h_e + h_p + h_v$ | Total hydraulic head | m |
| 7 | $Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_{10}}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in porous media | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Factors Affecting Hydraulic Conductivity ($K$)

The value of $K$ is influenced by numerous factors, which the examiner often tests:

1. **Particle size and distribution** — Coarser soils (gravel, coarse sand) have *much* higher $K$ than fine soils (silt, clay).
2. **Porosity and pore structure** — Well-graded soils have lower $K$ than uniformly graded soils of same particle size.
3. **Soil structure** — Stratified, fissured, or aggregated soils exhibit *anisotropic* $K$ (different in vertical vs. horizontal directions).
4. **Degree of saturation** — $K$ is highest at *full saturation*; it drops sharply as air enters pores.
5. **Temperature** — Higher temperature reduces water viscosity, increasing $K$ (this is why hot water flows faster through the same soil).
6. **Fluid properties** — Denser or more viscous fluids have lower $K$.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Drainage System Design** — Determining pipe spacing and depth in agricultural drainage.
- **Earth Dam Seepage** — Estimating seepage losses and designing filters.
- **Foundation Engineering** — Calculating uplift pressure and piping potential.
- **Contaminant Transport** — Modeling groundwater pollution spread.
- **Land Reclamation** — Predicting water table drawdown rates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Darcy's Law

We start with a **horizontal soil column** of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ saturated with water, connected to two reservoirs at different levels.

**Step 1 — Defining the Setup**

Let:
- Water level at inlet = $H_1$ (m)
- Water level at outlet = $H_2$ (m)
- Length of soil column = $L$ (m)
- Volume of water collected in time $t$ = $V$ (m³)

The total head difference driving flow is:

$$\Delta h = H_1 - H_2$$

**Step 2 — Establishing the Hydraulic Gradient**

By definition, the hydraulic gradient is the head loss per unit length of flow:

$$i = \frac{\Delta h}{L} = \frac{H_1 - H_2}{L}$$

This is a *dimensionless* quantity (m of head ÷ m of length).

**Step 3 — Expressing the Experimental Observations**

Darcy's experiments revealed that the volume of water $V$ flowing through the soil in time $t$ depends on three factors:

$$\text{(i) } V \propto A \quad \text{(proportional to cross-sectional area)}$$

$$\text{(ii) } V \propto (H_1 - H_2) \quad \text{(proportional to head difference)}$$

$$\text{(iii) } V \propto t \quad \text{(proportional to time of flow)}$$

Additionally, the volume is *inversely* proportional to the flow length $L$.

Combining all proportionalities and introducing a proportionality constant **$K$**:

$$V = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{(H_1 - H_2)}{L} \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Converting to Volumetric Discharge**

The discharge $Q$ is volume per unit time:

$$Q = \frac{V}{t}$$

Substituting:

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{(H_1 - H_2)}{L}$$

Or in standard symbolic form:

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot i$$

This is the **canonical Darcy's Law** in volumetric form.

**Step 5 — Deriving the Velocity Form**

Dividing both sides by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\frac{Q}{A} = K \cdot i$$

Recognizing that $Q/A = v$ (the discharge velocity):

$$v = K \cdot i$$

This is the most commonly used form of Darcy's Law for engineering analysis.

**Step 6 — Deriving the Seepage Velocity Relationship**

The actual water flows only through the *void* space. If porosity is $n$, then the actual cross-sectional area available for flow is:

$$A_v = n \cdot A$$

By continuity of flow (assuming steady, incompressible flow):

$$Q = v \cdot A = v_s \cdot A_v$$

Substituting $A_v = n \cdot A$:

$$v \cdot A = v_s \cdot n \cdot A$$

Cancelling $A$ on both sides:

$$v = n \cdot v_s$$

Solving for seepage velocity:

$$v_s = \frac{v}{n}$$

This is the **seepage velocity equation**, indicating that $v_s > v$ since $n < 1$ for all soils.

**Step 7 — Deriving Intrinsic Permeability from Hydraulic Conductivity**

The hydraulic conductivity depends on both the medium and the fluid. We start with the experimental observation that $K$ is proportional to $\gamma_w$ (denser fluid → higher $K$) and inversely proportional to $\mu$ (more viscous fluid → lower $K$):

$$K \propto \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu}$$

Introducing the proportionality constant $k$ (intrinsic permeability):

$$K = k \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu}$$

Since $\gamma_w = \rho \cdot g$ and $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$:

$$K = k \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot g}{\rho \cdot \nu} = k \cdot \frac{g}{\nu}$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k = \frac{K \cdot \nu}{g} = \frac{K \cdot \mu}{\gamma_w}$$

This is the **intrinsic permeability** — a property of the soil *only*, independent of the fluid.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A saturated sand sample of length **$L = 0.5$ m** and cross-sectional area **$A = 100$ cm²** is tested in a constant-head permeameter. Under a head difference of **$\Delta h = 40$ cm**, a volume of **$500$ cm³** of water is collected in **$t = 60$ seconds**. Calculate: **(a)** the discharge velocity, **(b)** the hydraulic conductivity, and **(c)** the seepage velocity, given porosity **$n = 0.4$**.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Calculate Discharge:**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{500 \text{ cm}^3}{60 \text{ s}} = 8.333 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step (b) — Calculate Hydraulic Gradient:**

Convert lengths to consistent units (cm):

$$i = \frac{\Delta h}{L} = \frac{40 \text{ cm}}{50 \text{ cm}} = 0.8$$

**Step (c) — Calculate Discharge Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{8.333 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}}{100 \text{ cm}^2} = 0.0833 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step (d) — Calculate Hydraulic Conductivity:**

Rearranging Darcy's Law:

$$K = \frac{v}{i} = \frac{0.0833 \text{ cm/s}}{0.8} = 0.1042 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step (e) — Calculate Seepage Velocity:**

$$v_s = \frac{v}{n} = \frac{0.0833}{0.4} = 0.2083 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Discharge velocity: $v = 0.0833$ cm/s
- Hydraulic conductivity: $K = 0.1042$ cm/s
- Seepage velocity: $v_s = 0.2083$ cm/s

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Soil Flow Parameters

| Property | Sand (Coarse) | Silt | Clay | Peat |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K$ (m/s) | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-9}$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| Porosity ($n$) | 0.25 to 0.40 | 0.35 to 0.50 | 0.40 to 0.70 | 0.60 to 0.80 |
| $d_{10}$ (mm) | 0.1 to 1.0 | 0.01 to 0.1 | < 0.005 | Variable |
| Validity of Darcy | Excellent | Good | Cautious | Variable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Categories of Soil Water and Their Drainage Significance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Soil Water System] --> B[Gravitational Water]
    A --> C[Capillary Water]
    A --> D[Hygroscopic Water]
    A --> E[Combined Water]

    B --> B1[High Energy State]
    B1 --> B2[Drains Under Gravity]
    B2 --> B3[Primary Target of Drainage Engineering]
    B3 --> B4[Governed by Darcy Law]

    C --> C1[Held by Surface Tension]
    C1 --> C2[Available to Plant Roots]
    C2 --> C3[Field Capacity to Wilting Point]
    C3 --> C4[Modeled via Soil Water Characteristic Curve]

    D --> D1[Strongly Adsorbed on Grains]
    D1 --> D2[Unavailable to Plants]
    D2 --> D3[Negligible in Flow Analysis]

    E --> E1[Chemically Bound]
    E1 --> E2[Unavailable for Flow]
    E2 --> E3[Excluded from Drainage Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Architecture of Darcy Flow Through Soils

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InletRes[Inlet Reservoir Head H1] --> SoilCol[Soil Column Length L Area A]
    SoilCol --> OutletRes[Outlet Reservoir Head H2]
    SoilCol --> PorousMed[Porous Medium Property K]
    PorousMed --> DischargeV[Discharge Velocity v = Ki]
    DischargeV --> SeepageV[Seepage Velocity vs = v divided by n]
    SeepageV --> Q[Volumetric Discharge Q = KiA]

    Hdiff[Head Difference H1 minus H2] --> Gradient[Hydraulic Gradient i = H1 minus H2 divided by L]
    Gradient --> DischargeV

    FluidProp[Fluid Properties rho and mu] --> Conductivity[K Conversion via Intrinsic Permeability k]
    PorousMed --> Conductivity
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Validity Decision Logic for Darcy's Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Soil Flow Scenario] --> CalcR[Calculate Reynolds Number Re]
    CalcR --> Q1{Re less than or equal to 1}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Laminar[Flow is Laminar]
    Laminar --> UseD[Apply Darcy Law v = Ki]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Re between 1 and 100}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Trans[Transitional Flow]
    Trans --> Caution[Use Darcy with Caution]
    Q2 -->|No| Turb[Turbulent Flow]
    Turb --> NonDarcy[Apply Non-Darcy Equations]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Factors Governing Hydraulic Conductivity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Hydraulic Conductivity K] --> F1[Soil Particle Size]
    K --> F2[Porosity and Pore Structure]
    K --> F3[Soil Stratification]
    K --> F4[Degree of Saturation]
    K --> F5[Fluid Temperature]
    K --> F6[Fluid Density and Viscosity]

    F1 --> P1[Coarse Soils higher K]
    F1 --> P2[Fine Soils lower K]
    F2 --> P3[Well-graded lower K]
    F2 --> P4[Uniform-graded higher K]
    F3 --> P5[Anisotropic K]
    F4 --> P6[Full Saturation maximum K]
    F5 --> P7[Higher Temperature lower viscosity higher K]
    F6 --> P8[Heavier Fluid higher K]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Hydraulic Conductivity Definition
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q: Define hydraulic conductivity. List three factors affecting its value in soils.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Hydraulic conductivity ($K$) is defined as the *discharge velocity of water per unit hydraulic gradient* through a saturated soil medium. Its standard unit is **m/s** (or cm/s). It is mathematically expressed as:

$$K = \frac{v}{i}$$

It represents the *ease* with which water flows through the soil's pore network.

**Three Factors (1 mark — 0.33 per factor):**
1. **Particle size and gradation** — Coarse, uniformly graded soils have higher $K$.
2. **Soil structure and stratification** — Layered soils exhibit anisotropic $K$.
3. **Temperature and fluid viscosity** — Higher temperature increases $K$.

---

### Question 2: Darcy's Law Statement
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q: State Darcy's Law. Under what conditions is it valid?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** Darcy's Law states that for *saturated laminar flow* through a soil mass, the *discharge velocity* is directly proportional to the *hydraulic gradient*. The mathematical form is:

$$v = K \cdot i = K \cdot \frac{\Delta h}{L}$$

**Validity Conditions (1.5 marks):**
1. The flow must be **laminar** (Reynolds number ≤ 1 in porous media).
2. The soil must be **saturated** (or, in unsaturated extension, $K$ becomes a function of moisture content).
3. The fluid must be **incompressible** (true for water under normal conditions).
4. The soil must be **homogeneous and isotropic** for constant $K$ throughout.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, Comprehensive]**

**(a) Derive Darcy's Law from first principles, clearly stating all assumptions made. Explain the difference between discharge velocity and seepage velocity with a labeled diagram description. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The soil is **saturated**.
2. Flow is **steady, laminar, and one-dimensional**.
3. The soil is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
4. Water is **incompressible** and Newtonian.
5. Darcy's Law is **valid** (Re ≤ 1).

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final form is:

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot i = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{H_1 - H_2}{L}$$

$$v = K \cdot i = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Difference Between Velocities (1 mark):**
- **Discharge velocity ($v$):** Velocity based on *total* cross-section (including solids). It is a *fictitious* velocity — water does not actually move at this rate.
- **Seepage velocity ($v_s$):** The *actual* velocity of water through the pore space only. Since voids occupy only a fraction $n$ of the total area, the actual velocity is higher:

$$v_s = \frac{v}{n} > v$$

---

**(b) A constant-head permeameter test was conducted on a saturated clayey-silt sample. The following data were recorded: Length of sample = 30 cm, cross-sectional area = 50 cm², head difference maintained = 75 cm, volume of water collected in 10 minutes = 250 cm³, porosity = 0.45. Determine: (i) the discharge velocity, (ii) the hydraulic conductivity, and (iii) the seepage velocity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $L = 30$ cm, $A = 50$ cm², $\Delta h = 75$ cm, $V = 250$ cm³, $t = 600$ s, $n = 0.45$

**Step 1: Calculate Discharge (1 mark):**
$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{250}{600} = 0.4167 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Discharge Velocity (1.5 marks):**
$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.4167}{50} = 0.00833 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Hydraulic Gradient (1 mark):**
$$i = \frac{\Delta h}{L} = \frac{75}{30} = 2.5$$

**Step 4: Calculate Hydraulic Conductivity (1.5 marks):**
$$K = \frac{v}{i} = \frac{0.00833}{2.5} = 0.00333 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Seepage Velocity (1 mark):**
$$v_s = \frac{v}{n} = \frac{0.00833}{0.45} = 0.0185 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Discharge velocity: $v = 0.00833$ cm/s
- Hydraulic conductivity: $K = 0.00333$ cm/s (= 3.33 × 10⁻⁵ m/s)
- Seepage velocity: $v_s = 0.0185$ cm/s

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, Conceptual]**

**(a) Define intrinsic permeability. How is it related to hydraulic conductivity? Discuss the validity of Darcy's Law with reference to Reynolds number for soil flow. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Intrinsic Permeability Definition (2 marks):**
Intrinsic permeability ($k$) is a property of the *porous medium alone*, independent of the fluid properties. It quantifies the ability of the soil *structure* to transmit fluid, without regard to whether the fluid is water, oil, or air.

**Relation to Hydraulic Conductivity (3 marks):**

Hydraulic conductivity $K$ depends on both the medium *and* the fluid. The relationship is:

$$K = k \cdot \frac{\gamma_w}{\mu} = k \cdot \frac{g}{\nu}$$

where:
- $k$ = intrinsic permeability (units: m² or darcy)
- $\gamma_w$ = unit weight of water
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration

Therefore, for a *given* soil, if the fluid changes (e.g., contamination by oil), only $K$ changes — $k$ remains constant because it depends solely on the pore geometry.

**Validity of Darcy's Law (2 marks):**

The Reynolds number for flow through porous media is:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_{10}}{\mu}$$

Darcy's Law is valid only for **laminar flow** with **Re ≤ 1** (some authors extend to Re ≤ 10). Beyond this range, the linear proportionality between $v$ and $i$ breaks down. For very coarse materials like gravel at high gradients, non-Darcy flow equations (e.g., Forchheimer equation) must be used.

---

**(b) A stratified soil profile consists of three horizontal layers with the following properties: Layer 1 (top): $K_1 = 10^{-4}$ cm/s, thickness = 2 m; Layer 2 (middle): $K_2 = 10^{-6}$ cm/s, thickness = 3 m; Layer 3 (bottom): $K_3 = 10^{-5}$ cm/s, thickness = 5 m. Calculate the equivalent hydraulic conductivity perpendicular to stratification (vertical flow). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $K_1 = 10^{-4}$ cm/s, $H_1 = 2$ m
- $K_2 = 10^{-6}$ cm/s, $H_2 = 3$ m
- $K_3 = 10^{-5}$ cm/s, $H_3 = 5$ m

**Concept (2 marks):**
For *vertical flow perpendicular* to stratification, the equivalent hydraulic conductivity is the *harmonic mean* weighted by layer thickness:

$$K_v = \frac{H_1 + H_2 + H_3}{\frac{H_1}{K_1} + \frac{H_2}{K_2} + \frac{H_3}{K_3}}$$

where total thickness $H = H_1 + H_2 + H_3 = 2 + 3 + 5 = 10$ m.

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Convert all to consistent units. Using m/s for $K$ values: $K_1 = 10^{-6}$, $K_2 = 10^{-8}$, $K_3 = 10^{-7}$ m/s.

$$\frac{H_1}{K_1} = \frac{2}{10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\frac{H_2}{K_2} = \frac{3}{10^{-8}} = 3 \times 10^{8}$$

$$\frac{H_3}{K_3} = \frac{5}{10^{-7}} = 5 \times 10^{7}$$

**Sum:**
$$\sum \frac{H_i}{K_i} = 2 \times 10^{6} + 3 \times 10^{8} + 5 \times 10^{7} = 0.02 \times 10^{8} + 3 \times 10^{8} + 0.5 \times 10^{8} = 3.52 \times 10^{8}$$

**Equivalent Vertical Conductivity:**
$$K_v = \frac{10}{3.52 \times 10^{8}} = 2.84 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s}$$

**Conclusion (in same units as original — cm/s):**
$$K_v = 2.84 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm/s}$$

This result confirms that **the least permeable layer controls the flow** in vertical flow through stratified soil — notice that the value is dominated by the very low $K_2$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing units** — Hydraulic conductivity $K$ varies by *eight orders of magnitude* across soil types. Always check units (m/s vs. cm/s vs. mm/s) in the problem statement.
> 2. **Confusing $v$ and $v_s$** — Discharge velocity uses *total* area; seepage velocity uses *void* area only. The relation $v_s = v/n$ must be cited correctly.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign in $i = -dh/dL$** — Examiners sometimes dock marks for missing this convention from fluid mechanics.
> 4. **Using Darcy's Law for turbulent flow** — Never apply $v = Ki$ when $Re > 1$; you must use the Forchheimer or non-Darcy equation.
> 5. **Harmonic mean vs. arithmetic mean** — Vertical flow uses *harmonic mean* of $K$; horizontal (parallel) flow uses *arithmetic mean*. Mixing these up costs full marks.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — Whenever deriving Darcy's Law, list the assumptions (saturated, laminar, steady, incompressible, etc.) explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ **Darcy's Law** in volumetric form: $Q = K \cdot A \cdot i$
>
> ✅ **Darcy's Law** in velocity form: $v = K \cdot i$
>
> ✅ Hydraulic gradient: $i = \Delta h / L$ (dimensionless)
>
> ✅ **Discharge velocity** $v$ is based on total area; **seepage velocity** $v_s = v / n$ is the actual pore velocity.
>
> ✅ **Intrinsic permeability** $k = K\mu / \gamma_w$ depends only on the soil structure, not the fluid.
>
> ✅ Darcy's Law is **valid only for laminar flow** ($Re \leq 1$ in porous media).
>
> ✅ **Vertical flow** through layered soil → equivalent $K$ = harmonic mean.
>
> ✅ **Horizontal flow** through layered soil → equivalent $K$ = arithmetic mean.
>
> ✅ Factors affecting $K$: particle size, gradation, porosity, saturation, temperature, fluid viscosity.
>
> ✅ Total hydraulic head: $h = h_e + h_p + h_v$ (velocity head is negligible in soil flow).
>
> ✅ The **least permeable layer** dominates vertical flow through stratified deposits.
>
> ✅ Typical $K$ values: gravel ≈ $10^{-1}$ m/s; sand ≈ $10^{-4}$ m/s; clay ≈ $10^{-9}$ m/s.
>
> ✅ For unsaturated soils, $K$ becomes a function of moisture content (this is the basis of the **Richards equation**).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Agricultural Drainage Engineering

Agricultural drainage is the engineered removal of excess water (surface and subsurface) and dissolved salts from the root zone of cropped soils to sustain productivity. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Module 4 transitions from water *delivery* (irrigation, Modules 1–3) to water *removal* (drainage) and water *quality* management — a logical bridge from engineering supply to engineering disposal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU PBAGT504 – Module 4):**
> *Drainage is the removal of gravitational water from the soil profile at a rate that prevents waterlogging, salinization, and the consequent loss of crop yield, while maintaining the soil's aerobic biological zone.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath-Tub" View of Farm Hydrology

Imagine a field as a **bathtub**:
- The **tap** is rainfall + irrigation inflow.
- The **overflow pipe** is the *drainage system*.
- The **sponge at the bottom** is the crop root zone (capillary + gravitational storage).

If the tap runs faster than the overflow pipe, the tub **overflows** → *waterlogging*.
If the tap runs slowly but the sponge stays damp for long, **salt accumulates** as pure water evaporates, leaving minerals behind → *salinization*.
A properly designed drainage system is simply a **bigger, better-positioned overflow pipe** that keeps the sponge (root zone) moist but aerated.

---

## 1.2 Surface Drainage Systems

### Formal Definition
A **Surface Drainage System (SDS)** is a network of natural or constructed open channels, land grading works, and structures that remove excess water from the *land surface* by gravity before it infiltrates deeply or ponds for long durations.

> [!NOTE]
> Surface drainage handles **excess precipitation, irrigation tail-water, and flood overflow** at the soil–atmosphere interface.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a city street after a thunderstorm — the **curb, gutter, and storm drain** are exactly the urban equivalent of a farm's surface drainage system. The water moves on the *skin* of the land, not through it.

### Types of Surface Drainage
1. **Land Grading (Land Smoothing / Planing)**
   - Uniform slope (typically **0.1% to 0.5%**) created by earth-moving.
   - Removes micro-depressions that pond water.
2. **Random (Natural) Drainage Systems**
   - Utilizes natural depressions, swales, and streams.
   - Lowest cost, but unpredictable performance.
3. **Parallel Drainage Systems**
   - Parallel field drains feeding a single main.
   - Used in low-relief, flat plains (e.g., Kuttanad).
4. **Interception (Cut-off) Drains**
   - Ditches dug *across* the slope to intercept runoff before it enters the cropped field.
5. **Bedding Systems**
   - Shallow parallel furrows (beds) alternating with furrows acting as drains.
   - Very common in row-crop cultivation.

---

## 1.3 Subsurface Drainage (SSD)

### Formal Definition
A **Subsurface Drainage System** is an underground network of perforated or open-jointed conduits, placed *below* the soil surface, that lowers the water table and removes gravitational water from the **root zone** through the soil matrix itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SSD does **NOT** remove surface water directly. It removes the **capillary fringe and water table** that have risen too close to plant roots.

### Intuitive Analogy
A subsurface drain is like a **French drain behind a retaining wall** in a house basement — you cannot see it working, but the soil never feels soggy because water silently trickles into the buried pipe and runs away.

### Components of Subsurface Drainage
| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Laterals** | Small-diameter pipes collecting water from soil |
| **Sub-mains** | Collect from laterals |
| **Main / Outfall** | Conveys to a receiving water body or sump |
| **Manholes / Junction Boxes** | Inspection and access points |
| **Filters / Envelopes** | Prevent fine soil entry (sand, gravel, geotextile) |
| **Outlet / Spillway** | Controlled discharge structure |

### Types of Subsurface Drains
1. **Pipe Drains** — Corrugated PVC (CPP), corrugated HDPE, concrete, clay tile.
2. **Mole Drains** — Unlined cylindrical channels formed by a plough-mounted "mole" in heavy clay soils.
3. **Drainage Wells (Vertical Drains)** — Tube wells used to lower regional water tables.
4. **Interceptor Drains (Cut-off / French Drains)** — Installed up-slope to capture seepage.
5. **Tile Drains** — Traditional term for short clay or concrete pipe sections.

---

## 1.4 Leaching Requirement (LR)

### Definition
The **Leaching Requirement (LR)** is the *minimum fraction* of irrigation water that must percolate *through* the root zone to drain out salts, expressed as a percentage of the total water applied, in order to maintain salt balance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why leaching?** Plants absorb **pure water** but leave salts behind. Over years, these salts accumulate in the root zone and reach toxic levels (Electrical Conductivity > **4 dS/m** for sensitive crops). Leaching flushes them below the root zone.

### Key Water-Quality Parameters
- **EC** = Electrical Conductivity (dS/m) — proxy for total dissolved salts.
- **SAR** = Sodium Adsorption Ratio — measures sodium hazard.
- **RSC** = Residual Sodium Carbonate (me/L) — carbonate/bicarbonate hazard.
- **TDS** = Total Dissolved Solids (mg/L) ≈ **EC × 640** (approximation).
- **pH** — acidity/alkalinity (1–14 scale).

> [!WARNING]
> Many KTU students confuse **EC of irrigation water (ECiw)** with **EC of drainage water (ECdw)**. Always identify the source before plugging values into the LR formula.

---

## 1.5 Why Module 4 Matters in KTU 2024

Module 4 carries **high-weightage conceptual questions** because it merges:
- Hydraulic design (steady-state equations, flow nets).
- Materials engineering (pipe selection).
- Soil chemistry (leaching, salinity).
- Project planning (final-year project topics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical **14-mark ESE question** combines *Hooghoudt's drain-spacing calculation* with a *leaching requirement* sub-part. Master the formulas, not just the theory.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Surface Drainage — Design Theory

### Design Parameters (in order of importance)
1. **Drainage Coefficient (Dc)** — depth of water to be removed in 24 hours (mm/day). Typical:
   - **10–25 mm/day** for humid regions (Kerala).
   - **5–15 mm/day** for arid/semi-arid regions.
2. **Return Period** of the design storm (usually **5–10 years** for farmland).
3. **Runoff Coefficient (C)** — fraction of rainfall that becomes runoff.
4. **Time of Concentration (tc)** — time for the most-distant point to reach the outlet.
5. **Channel Cross-section** — sized by **Manning's equation**.

### Manning's Equation (Recap)

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

where:
- **Q** = discharge (m³/s)
- **A** = cross-sectional flow area (m²)
- **R** = hydraulic radius = A / P (m)
- **S** = bed slope (m/m)
- **n** = Manning's roughness coefficient

> [!NOTE]
> For lined farm channels, **n = 0.015 to 0.025**. For earthen channels with weeds, **n = 0.030 to 0.040**.

### Rational Method for Peak Runoff

$$Q_p = \frac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360}$$

where:
- **Qp** = peak discharge (m³/s)
- **C** = runoff coefficient (0.0 to 1.0)
- **I** = rainfall intensity (mm/h) for duration = tc
- **A** = catchment area (hectares)

---

## 2.2 Subsurface Drainage — Design Theory

### Design Objectives
1. **Lower the water table** to a depth that permits root respiration (typically **0.6 m to 1.2 m** below ground surface for most crops; **0.6 m for sugarcane, 0.8 m for rice, 1.0 m for orchards**).
2. **Remove excess water** at a rate equal to the **drainage coefficient**.
3. **Maintain salt balance** via leaching.
4. **Prevent soil piping** by adequate envelope design.

### Key Design Variables
| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **q** | Drainage coefficient (depth of water removed per day) | mm/day or m/day |
| **L** | Drain spacing (centre-to-centre of laterals) | m |
| **d** | Equivalent depth from drain to impervious layer (Hooghoudt's d) | m |
| **D** | Actual depth from drain centre to impervious layer | m |
| **h** | Maximum water-table height midway between drains above drain level | m |
| **K** | Hydraulic conductivity of soil | m/day |
| **m** | Height of water table above drain at midpoint (Donnan's) | m |

---

## 2.3 Steady-State Design Equations

### A. Hooghoudt's Equation (Most Tested in KTU)

For drains **above an impervious layer**, parallel and equally spaced, with the water table reaching a stable position between two drains:

$$q = \frac{4K\,m\,(2d + m)}{L^{2}}$$

Expanded form (when the water table midway is at height **h** above the drain):

$$q = \frac{8K\,d\,m + 4K\,m^{2}}{L^{2}}$$

Solving for **L** (drain spacing):

$$L^{2} = \frac{4K\,m^{2} + 8K\,d\,m}{q}$$

$$\boxed{\,L = \sqrt{\frac{4K\,m^{2} + 8K\,d\,m}{q}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions for Hooghoudt's equation:**
> 1. Steady-state flow (input = output).
> 2. Homogeneous, isotropic soil.
> 3. Drains placed *above* the impervious layer.
> 4. Water table between drains is in equilibrium.
> 5. Drains run *full* but not under pressure.

### B. Donnan's Equation (For drains resting ON the impervious layer)

When the impervious layer is **shallow** and the drain is essentially on or very close to it, Hooghoudt's geometry breaks down. **Donnan (1946)** proposed a simpler relation:

$$q = \frac{4K\,m\,d}{L^{2}} \quad \text{(simplified Donnan)}$$

More accurately, Donnan's full form:

$$q = \frac{4K\,m\,d + 2K\,m^{2}}{L^{2}}$$

Or, in **height-based** form using **d** = depth from drain to impermeable layer and **m** = height of water table at midpoint:

$$\boxed{\,L = \sqrt{\frac{4K\,m\,d + 2K\,m^{2}}{q}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hooghoudt vs. Donnan** — Hooghoudt's is the *general* equation. Donnan's is the *limit case* when **d = 0** (drain on impervious layer). In KTU problems, Hooghoudt's is asked **90% of the time**.

### C. Equivalent Depth (Hooghoudt's `d_e`)

Hooghoudt introduced an **equivalent depth (d_e)** to handle the radial-flow correction near the drain. The equivalent depth depends on **L** and the **actual depth D**, and is read from Hooghoudt's tables or computed by:

$$d_{e} = \frac{D}{\left[1 + \frac{D}{L}\left(\frac{8}{\pi} \ln\frac{D}{u} - \alpha \right)\right]}$$

where **u** = drain radius (m), and **α** is a constant from Hooghoudt's chart.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam calculations, use the **tabulated values** of d_e rather than the formula. Values are usually provided in the question.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Km^{2} + 8Kdm}{q}}$ | Hooghoudt's drain spacing (drain above impervious) | L,m,K in m/day; q in m/day |
| 2 | $L = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Kmd + 2Km^{2}}{q}}$ | Donnan's spacing (drain on impervious) | Same as above |
| 3 | $q = \dfrac{4Km(2d+m)}{L^{2}}$ | Hooghoudt's discharge relation | m/day |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's open-channel flow | SI |
| 5 | $Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360}$ | Rational method peak runoff | m³/s |
| 6 | $LR = \dfrac{EC_{iw}}{EC_{dw}} \times 100$ | Leaching Requirement (%) | dS/m |
| 7 | $EC_{dw} = \dfrac{EC_{iw}}{LR/100}$ | Equivalent drainage-water EC | dS/m |
| 8 | $SAR = \dfrac{Na^{+}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+}}{2}}}$ | Sodium hazard | (me/L)^0.5 |
| 9 | $RSC = (CO_{3}^{2-} + HCO_{3}^{-}) - (Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+})$ | Residual Sodium Carbonate | me/L |
| 10 | $TDS \approx 640 \times EC$ | Total Dissolved Solids | mg/L |
| 11 | $D_{w} = 0.6 + 0.15 \cdot Y$ | Drain depth formula (FAO guideline) | m |
| 12 | $A_{pipe} = \dfrac{Q}{v}$ | Pipe cross-section | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols to remember by heart:**
> **K** = hydraulic conductivity, **L** = drain spacing, **d** = depth to impervious, **m** = midpoint water-table height, **q** = drainage coefficient.

---

## 2.5 Pipe Materials for Subsurface Drains

| Material | Diameter Range | Strength | Cost | Lifespan | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Clay tile (unglazed)** | 50–200 mm | Brittle | Moderate | 30–50 yr | Traditional, joints can admit fines |
| **Concrete (plain or perforated)** | 100–300 mm | Strong | Low | 25–40 yr | Heavier, can be sulphate-attacked |
| **Corrugated PVC (CPP)** | 50–200 mm | Flexible, strong | Moderate | 50+ yr | Most common today |
| **Corrugated HDPE** | 50–200 mm | Very flexible | High | 50+ yr | Chemically inert |
| **Bituminized fibre** | 75–200 mm | Moderate | Moderate | 20–30 yr | Older, rarely used |
| **Smooth PVC (perforated)** | 50–160 mm | Rigid | Moderate | 30 yr | Less common |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection criteria:**
> 1. **Soil pH and chemical aggressiveness** → avoid concrete if soil pH < 6.0 (sulphate attack).
> 2. **Soil texture** → fine sand needs smaller slot perforations + envelope.
> 3. **Hydraulic capacity** → use Manning's for smooth or Hazen-Williams for corrugated.
> 4. **Installation method** → flexible pipes (HDPE) for trenchless ploughing.

---

## 2.6 Mole Drains

### Definition
A **mole drain** is an unlined, cylindrical, subsurface channel formed by drawing a torpedo-shaped **mole plough** through a heavy clay soil at a depth of **0.4–0.6 m**. The compressed channel persists for **3–8 years** before the clay rehydrates and closes it.

### Applicability
- **Heavy clay soils** (> **30% clay**, typically > **40%**).
- **Low hydraulic conductivity topsoil** overlying a more permeable subsoil.
- Cost-effective in **estates and large farms** in Kerala (e.g., rice paddies in Kuttanad).

### Design
- **Spacing:** **1.5 m to 4.0 m** depending on soil swell-shrink potential.
- **Depth:** **0.4 m to 0.6 m** (deep enough not to be destroyed by tillage).
- **Grade:** minimum **0.1% (1:1000)** for self-cleaning flow.
- **Lifespan extension:** install **gravel or tyre-chip mole-channel liners** to delay collapse.

> [!WARNING]
> Mole drains **fail in sandy or silty soils** — the channel collapses within weeks. Always verify the **plasticity index (Ip > 25)** before recommending.

---

## 2.7 Drainage Wells (Vertical Drains / Tubewell Drainage)

### Definition
A **drainage well** is a vertical borehole (typically **0.3–0.6 m** diameter) that pumps water out of a confined or semi-confined aquifer, lowering the regional water table over a large area. They are also called **relief wells** or **vertical drainage wells**.

### Types
1. **Shallow open wells** — for domestic + small farm drainage.
2. **Deep tube wells (gravel-packed)** — for regional dewatering.
3. **Recharge wells** — *inject* excess water into an aquifer (reverse function).

### Design
- **Spacing** based on radius of influence (R) and drawdown (s):

$$s = \frac{Q}{2\pi K D} \ln\frac{R}{r_w}$$

- **Q** = pump discharge (m³/day)
- **R** = radius of influence (m), typically **100–500 m**
- **rw** = well radius (m)
- **D** = aquifer thickness (m)
- **K** = aquifer hydraulic conductivity (m/day)

> [!NOTE]
> In Kerala's coastal lowlands, **vertical drainage wells** are an emerging alternative to open-ditch systems because land is scarce and agriculture is intense.

---

## 2.8 Leaching Requirement — Theory

### Salt-Balance Equation
For a field in **long-term equilibrium**, the salt input (irrigation + rainfall + fertilizer) must equal salt output (drainage + crop uptake):

$$V_i \cdot C_i + V_r \cdot C_r = V_d \cdot C_d + M_{crop}$$

For **negligible rainfall salt and crop uptake**:

$$V_i \cdot C_i \approx V_d \cdot C_d$$

Since **LR = Vd / Vi** (fraction leached):

$$\boxed{\,LR = \frac{C_i}{C_d} = \frac{EC_{iw}}{EC_{dw}} \times 100\,(\%)\,}$$

### Common Threshold EC Values
| Crop | Threshold EC (dS/m) | Yield Decline Begins |
|---|---|---|
| Rice | **3.0** | Sensitive |
| Wheat | **6.0** | Moderate |
| Sugarcane | **1.7** | Sensitive |
| Cotton | **7.7** | Tolerant |
| Barley | **8.0** | Tolerant |
| Coconut | **4.0** | Moderate (Kerala) |

> [!TIP]
> The **FAO-56** guideline states: *"Leach at least 10–15% of the applied irrigation water whenever ECiw > 1.5 dS/m."*

---

## 2.9 Water Quality for Irrigation and Drainage

### Irrigation Water Quality
- **Salinity Hazard (ECiw):** Classified as **C1 (<0.25), C2 (0.25–0.75), C3 (0.75–2.25), C4 (2.25–5.0), C5 (>5.0)** dS/m.
- **Sodium Hazard (SAR):** **S1 (<10), S2 (10–18), S3 (18–26), S4 (>26)**.
- **Combined USDA Diagram** plots EC on the Y-axis vs. SAR on the X-axis, classifying water into **16 categories**.

### Drainage Water Quality
- **Reuse** for irrigation of salt-tolerant crops if **EC < 4 dS/m** and **SAR < 10**.
- **Disposal** standards depend on receiving water body (BOD, COD, nutrient load, heavy metals).
- In Kerala, drainage water reuse is mandated for **coconut and rice** belts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala Context:** In Kuttanad and Pokkali fields, drainage water is **brackish** during summer months (EC 2–6 dS/m) and is a key constraint for double-cropping.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hooghoudt's Steady-State Equation

### Starting Geometry

Consider **two parallel subsurface drains**, spacing **L** apart, installed at depth **d_e** (equivalent depth) above a horizontal impervious layer. Let the water table rise to a midpoint height of **m** above the drain centre, and let the system reach **steady state** so that inflow = outflow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Dupuit-Forchheimer flow — hydraulic gradient equals the slope of the water table.

### Derivation

Let **x** be the horizontal distance from the midpoint (so **x = 0** at midpoint, **x = L/2** at the drain). The water-table height above the drain is **y(x)**, with **y(0) = m** and **y(L/2) = 0**.

By **Darcy's Law**, the flow per unit width in the x-direction at any section is:

$$q_x = -K \cdot y \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}$$

For **steady state**, the drain must carry away a constant **q** (m³/day per m of drain length). Therefore the *net lateral inflow* into the soil column between **x = 0** and **x = L/2** must equal **q·L/2**:

$$\int_{0}^{L/2} q \, dx = \int_{0}^{m} K \cdot y \, dy$$

This gives the standard **Dupuit parabola** for the water table. Now Hooghoudt's contribution is to account for the **radial flow zone** near the drain through the **equivalent depth d_e**.

The full **Hooghoudt formula** is derived by integrating the radial-flow correction around the drain and combining it with the horizontal-flow term:

$$q \cdot \frac{L^{2}}{8} = K \cdot d_e \cdot m + \frac{1}{2} K \cdot m^{2}$$

Multiplying both sides by 8:

$$q \cdot L^{2} = 8 K d_e m + 4 K m^{2}$$

Rearranging for **L²**:

$$L^{2} = \frac{8 K d_e m + 4 K m^{2}}{q}$$

$$\boxed{\,L = \sqrt{\frac{4K\,m^{2} + 8K\,d_e\,m}{q}}\,}$$

### Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A heavy clay loam soil has **K = 1.2 m/day**. Drains are installed at a depth such that **d_e = 3.0 m**. The required midpoint water-table height is **m = 0.6 m** above drain level. Design discharge (drainage coefficient) is **q = 0.012 m/day**. Calculate the **drain spacing L**.

**Step 1 — Identify variables**

$$K = 1.2 \text{ m/day}, \quad d_e = 3.0 \text{ m}, \quad m = 0.6 \text{ m}, \quad q = 0.012 \text{ m/day}$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator term-by-term**

$$4K m^{2} = 4 \times 1.2 \times (0.6)^{2} = 4.8 \times 0.36 = 1.728$$

$$8K d_e m = 8 \times 1.2 \times 3.0 \times 0.6 = 28.8 \times 0.6 = 17.28$$

**Step 3 — Sum and divide by q**

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.728 + 17.28 = 19.008$$

$$\frac{19.008}{0.012} = 1584.0$$

**Step 4 — Take square root**

$$L = \sqrt{1584.0} = 39.80 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** Use **L ≈ 40 m** (typically rounded down to a multiple of 5 m in practice).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Donnan's Equation

Donnan (1946) considered the **limiting case** where the impervious layer lies **immediately below the drain** (so the radial-flow zone is truncated). In that case, **d → 0** in the Hooghoudt geometry, and only the **vertical-flow term survives**.

Starting from the steady-state balance for a drain *on* the impervious layer:

$$q \cdot \frac{L^{2}}{2} = 2 K m d + K m^{2}$$

Multiplying by 2:

$$q L^{2} = 4 K m d + 2 K m^{2}$$

Solving for **L**:

$$L = \sqrt{\frac{4 K m d + 2 K m^{2}}{q}}$$

If **d = 0** (drain exactly on impervious layer):

$$L = \sqrt{\frac{2 K m^{2}}{q}} = m \sqrt{\frac{2K}{q}}$$

### Numerical Example — Donnan

> **Problem:** A drain rests directly on an impervious clay pan. **K = 0.8 m/day**, **d = 0.5 m**, midpoint water-table height **m = 0.4 m**, **q = 0.008 m/day**. Find L.

**Step 1 — Substitute**

$$L = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.8 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 + 2 \times 0.8 \times (0.4)^{2}}{0.008}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term**

$$4Kmd = 4 \times 0.8 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.64$$

$$2Km^{2} = 2 \times 0.8 \times 0.16 = 0.256$$

$$\text{Sum} = 0.896$$

**Step 3 — Divide by q and take square root**

$$\frac{0.896}{0.008} = 112.0$$

$$L = \sqrt{112.0} = 10.58 \text{ m} \approx 10.5 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** **L ≈ 10.5 m**, very close spacing — typical of heavy clay overlying a pan.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Leaching Requirement

### Starting Principle

For a **sustainable irrigation block**, the **mass of salts** entering must equal the mass leaving (steady state). Let:

- **Vi** = volume of irrigation water applied (m³/ha/yr)
- **Ci** = salt concentration in irrigation water (mg/L or dS/m)
- **Vd** = volume of drainage water (m³/ha/yr)
- **Cd** = salt concentration in drainage water (mg/L or dS/m)

### Salt Balance Equation

$$V_i \cdot C_i = V_d \cdot C_d \quad \text{(neglecting rain salt and crop uptake)}$$

### Define Leaching Fraction (LF)

$$LF = \frac{V_d}{V_i} = \frac{LR}{100}$$

### Substituting

$$V_i \cdot C_i = (LF \cdot V_i) \cdot C_d$$

$$C_i = LF \cdot C_d$$

$$LF = \frac{C_i}{C_d}$$

Expressing as **percentage**:

$$\boxed{\,LR \,(\%) = \frac{EC_{iw}}{EC_{dw}} \times 100\,}$$

### Numerical Example — Leaching

> **Problem:** A rice field is irrigated with water of **ECiw = 1.0 dS/m**. The drainage water acceptable for safe salt balance is **ECdw = 4.0 dS/m**. Compute the **Leaching Requirement** and the **extra water required per 100 mm of irrigation**.

**Step 1 — Compute LR**

$$LR = \frac{1.0}{4.0} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute leaching depth**

For every **100 mm of irrigation**, leaching fraction = 25% means drainage must equal **25 mm**.

So **extra water = 25 mm** must pass through the root zone (i.e., 100 mm irrigation + 25 mm extra = 125 mm total pumped/diverted).

**Step 3 — Annual extra water**

For a crop needing **1000 mm of net irrigation**, annual extra water = **250 mm** = **2500 m³/ha**.

**Answer:** **LR = 25%**, **extra water = 25 mm per 100 mm irrigated**, or **2500 m³/ha/year** for a 1000 mm crop.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Manning's Equation (Recap for KTU)

Manning's empirical equation (1889) combines Chezy's and Bazin's work:

$$V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

Multiplying by **A** (cross-section) gives **Q**:

$$Q = A \cdot V = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

For a **trapezoidal channel** of bottom width **b**, side slope **z:1**, depth **y**:

$$A = (b + zy) \cdot y$$

$$P = b + 2y\sqrt{1 + z^{2}}$$

$$R = \frac{A}{P}$$

### Quick Example — Surface Drain Channel

> **Problem:** A trapezoidal surface drain has **b = 1.0 m**, **z = 1.5**, **y = 0.6 m**, **S = 0.001**, **n = 0.025**. Find Q.

**Step 1 — Area**

$$A = (1.0 + 1.5 \times 0.6) \times 0.6 = 1.9 \times 0.6 = 1.14 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Wetted Perimeter**

$$P = 1.0 + 2(0.6)\sqrt{1 + 1.5^{2}} = 1.0 + 1.2\sqrt{3.25} = 1.0 + 1.2 \times 1.8028 = 3.163 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Hydraulic Radius**

$$R = \frac{1.14}{3.163} = 0.3605 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Manning's**

$$Q = \frac{1}{0.025} \times 1.14 \times (0.3605)^{2/3} \times (0.001)^{1/2}$$

$$Q = 40 \times 1.14 \times 0.5106 \times 0.03162$$

$$Q = 40 \times 0.01840 = 0.736 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Answer:** **Q ≈ 0.74 m³/s** — sufficient to drain a small catchment.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hooghoudt vs. Donnan

| Parameter | Hooghoudt | Donnan |
|---|---|---|
| Drain position | **Above** impervious layer | **On or very near** impervious layer |
| Equivalent depth | Uses **d_e** | Uses **d** directly |
| Applicability | Most soils | Shallow aquitard case |
| Equation form | $L = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Km^{2} + 8Kdm}{q}}$ | $L = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Kmd + 2Km^{2}}{q}}$ |
| Frequency in KTU papers | **~90%** | ~10% |
| Handles radial flow | **Yes** (via d_e tables) | **No** (radial zone ignored) |
| Best for | Deep, permeable soils | Shallow soils with hard pan |

---

## 3.6 Quick Analytical Table — Common Drainage Numbers to Memorise

| Quantity | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Drainage coefficient (humid) | **10–25** | mm/day |
| Drainage coefficient (arid) | **5–15** | mm/day |
| Drain depth (laterals) | **0.8–1.5** | m |
| Drain spacing | **10–50** | m |
| Hydraulic conductivity (sand) | **1–100** | m/day |
| Hydraulic conductivity (clay) | **0.001–0.1** | m/day |
| Manning's n (earthen drain) | **0.025–0.040** | — |
| Manning's n (lined drain) | **0.012–0.018** | — |
| LR for ECiw = 1 dS/m | **10–25%** | % |
| Mole drain depth | **0.4–0.6** | m |
| Mole drain spacing | **1.5–4.0** | m |
| Mole drain lifespan | **3–8** | years |
| Corrugated pipe diameter | **50–200** | mm |
| Mole plough speed | **2–4** | km/h |

---

## 3.7 Complete Worked-Out KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

> **Problem (Combine 2 sub-parts):**
>
> **(a)** A field has the following data:
> - Soil: sandy loam, **K = 2.0 m/day**
> - Drain depth (effective) **d_e = 4.0 m**
> - Required midpoint water-table drawdown **m = 0.5 m**
> - Drainage coefficient **q = 0.010 m/day**
>
> Compute the **drain spacing** using Hooghoudt's equation.
>
> **(b)** The irrigation water has **ECiw = 1.5 dS/m**. For a crop whose salt-tolerance threshold requires **ECdw = 6.0 dS/m**, calculate the **Leaching Requirement** and the **extra water (m³/ha)** required if the seasonal crop demand is **600 mm**.

### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1 — Identify variables**

$$K = 2.0, \quad d_e = 4.0, \quad m = 0.5, \quad q = 0.010$$

**Step 2 — Hooghoudt's formula**

$$L = \sqrt{\frac{4Km^{2} + 8K d_e m}{q}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator terms**

$$4Km^{2} = 4 \times 2.0 \times 0.25 = 2.0$$

$$8K d_e m = 8 \times 2.0 \times 4.0 \times 0.5 = 32.0$$

**Step 4 — Sum**

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.0 + 32.0 = 34.0$$

**Step 5 — Divide and square-root**

$$\frac{34.0}{0.010} = 3400$$

$$L = \sqrt{3400} = 58.31 \text{ m}$$

**Answer (a):** **L ≈ 58 m** (round to **55–60 m** in practice).

### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1 — Leaching Requirement**

$$LR = \frac{EC_{iw}}{EC_{dw}} \times 100 = \frac{1.5}{6.0} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Step 2 — Leaching depth**

$$d_{leach} = LR \times d_{irrigation} = 0.25 \times 600 \text{ mm} = 150 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volume**

$$150 \text{ mm} = 0.150 \text{ m depth over 1 ha}$$

$$\text{Volume} = 0.150 \times 10{,}000 = 1500 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{ha}$$

**Answer (b):** **LR = 25%**, **extra water = 1500 m³/ha**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams use **safe alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `nodeA`, `flowB`) and **plain text labels** (no markdown formatting inside quoted labels) to ensure clean rendering.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Complete Farm Drainage System

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[RAINFALL AND IRRIGATION INFLOW] --> nodeB[LAND SURFACE]
    nodeB --> nodeC[INFILTRATION ZONE]
    nodeB --> nodeD[SURFACE RUNOFF]
    nodeD --> nodeE[SURFACE DRAIN NETWORK]
    nodeE --> nodeF[OPEN CHANNELS]
    nodeF --> nodeG[OUTFALL OR RIVER]
    nodeC --> nodeH[ROOT ZONE]
    nodeH --> nodeI[CAPILLARY FRINGE]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[SUBSURFACE DRAIN LATERAL]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[SUBMAIN]
    nodeK --> nodeL[MAIN COLLECTOR]
    nodeL --> nodeM[OUTLET AND SPILLWAY]
    nodeM --> nodeG
    nodeH --> nodeN[DEEP PERCOLATION]
    nodeN --> nodeO[GROUNDWATER AQUIFER]
    nodeO --> nodeP[DRAINAGE WELL OR TUBE WELL]
    nodeP --> nodeG
    nodeH --> nodeQ[SALT ACCUMULATION]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[LEACHING CYCLE]
    nodeR --> nodeH
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Inputs (rain, irrigation) feed the **land surface** and **root zone**.
- The **left branch** handles **surface drainage** (channels → outfall).
- The **right branch** handles **subsurface drainage** (laterals → main → outlet).
- A **vertical branch** shows deep percolation → aquifer → drainage well.
- A **feedback loop** indicates **salt accumulation → leaching → root zone**.

---

## 4.2 Hooghoudt's Geometry — Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[GROUND SURFACE LINE] --> nodeB[DRAIN LATERAL PIPE]
    nodeB --> nodeC[IMPERMEABLE LAYER HORIZONTAL]
    nodeD[WATER TABLE PARABOLA MIDPOINT HEIGHT M] --> nodeA
    nodeE[DRAIN SPACING L CENTRE TO CENTRE] --> nodeF[DRAIN SPACING L OPPOSITE DRAIN]
    nodeG[EQUIVALENT DEPTH D E FROM DRAIN TO IMPERMEABLE] --> nodeC
    nodeH[HALF SPACING L DIVIDED BY 2] --> nodeE
    nodeI[SYMMETRY AXIS AT MIDPOINT] --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeJ[COLLECTED WATER FLOWING IN PIPE]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[SUBMAIN]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Two parallel drains (left and right), spacing **L** apart.
- Water table is a **parabola** peaking at midpoint, height **m** above drain.
- **d_e** is the equivalent depth from drain centre to impervious layer.
- Half-spacing **L/2** is the horizontal distance from the symmetry axis to the drain.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing a Drainage Type

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[START: ASSESS SOIL AND CROP] --> nodeB[IS SOIL HEAVY CLAY GT 30 PERCENT]
    nodeB -->|YES| nodeC[IS PLASTICITY INDEX GT 25]
    nodeC -->|YES| nodeD[USE MOLE DRAINS WITH SPACING 1.5 TO 4 M]
    nodeC -->|NO| nodeE[USE PIPE DRAINS WITH ENVELOPE]
    nodeB -->|NO| nodeF[IS WATER TABLE WITHIN 1 M OF SURFACE]
    nodeF -->|YES| nodeG[USE CORRUGATED PVC LATERALS]
    nodeF -->|NO| nodeH[USE SURFACE GRADING ONLY]
    nodeD --> nodeI[CHECK LIFESPAN EVERY 3 YEARS]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[DESIGN WITH HOOGHOUDT]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK[END: SURFACE DRAINAGE ADEQUATE]
    nodeI --> nodeL[END: SUBSURFACE DRAINAGE INSTALLED]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Begins with **soil and crop assessment**.
- Branches into **clay content** → mole vs. pipe decision.
- If water table is high → **subsurface pipe drainage**.
- Otherwise → **surface grading suffices**.
- All subsurface paths converge on **Hooghoudt design**.

---

## 4.4 Subsurface Drain Cross-Section — Pipe + Envelope

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[TOPSOIL LAYER] --> nodeB[SUB GRADE AND ENVELOPE SAND OR GRAVEL]
    nodeB --> nodeC[PERFORATED CORRUGATED PIPE]
    nodeC --> nodeD[FILTER GEOTEXTILE WRAP]
    nodeD --> nodeB
    nodeC --> nodeE[PERFORATIONS OR SLITS]
    nodeE --> nodeF[SOIL WATER ENTERS PIPE]
    nodeF --> nodeG[COLLECTED FLOW TOWARDS SUBMAIN]
    nodeH[OUTLET TO MANHOLE OR JUNCTION BOX] --> nodeG
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Topsoil sits above the **envelope** (sand/gravel).
- **Geotextile** prevents fines from entering perforations.
- Water enters the **pipe** through perforations → flows to the **submain** → **manhole**.

---

## 4.5 Salt-Balance Flow in Leaching

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[IRRIGATION WATER WITH EC IW] --> nodeB[ROOT ZONE]
    nodeB --> nodeC[CROP UPTAKE PURE WATER]
    nodeB --> nodeD[SALT ACCUMULATES IN SOIL]
    nodeD --> nodeE[LEACHING EVENT EXCESS IRRIGATION]
    nodeE --> nodeF[PERCOLATION BELOW ROOT ZONE]
    nodeF --> nodeG[DRAINAGE WATER WITH EC DW]
    nodeG --> nodeH[OUTFALL OR REUSE]
    nodeA --> nodeI[RAINFALL LOW SALT]
    nodeI --> nodeB
    nodeJ[EC DW DETERMINED BY LR] --> nodeA
    nodeJ --> nodeG
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Irrigation water enters the root zone.
- Crop absorbs pure water → leaves salts.
- Periodic **leaching** flushes salts to drainage water.
- The **leaching requirement (LR)** sets the ratio of ECiw to ECdw.

---

## 4.6 Block Architecture — Mole Drain Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[FIELD WITH HEAVY CLAY SOIL] --> nodeB[SHALLOW SUBSOIL PLOUGHING]
    nodeB --> nodeC[MOLE PLOUGHS TUNNELS AT 0.4 TO 0.6 M]
    nodeC --> nodeD[UNDRAINED CHANNELS LIFESPAN 3 TO 8 YR]
    nodeD --> nodeE[OUTLET TO OPEN DITCH]
    nodeE --> nodeF[OUTFALL]
    nodeC --> nodeG[SPACING 1.5 TO 4 M]
    nodeD --> nodeH[RE-PLOUGH AFTER 5 YR TYPICAL]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Mole plough is drawn through clay, forming unlined channels.
- Channels drain to an **open ditch → outfall**.
- Spacing 1.5–4 m depending on clay type.
- Lifespan is 3–8 years; **re-ploughing** is necessary periodically.

---
